## Problem

Sentiment cards were showing only the last 200 characters of message text, making many distinct messages appear identical. This caused confusion about why "duplicate" cards weren't being grouped, when they were actually different messages that shared the same tail.

## Changes

- **Display**: Replaced tail-only truncation (last 200 chars) with start...end truncation (first 250 + last 250 chars) so users can identify messages at a glance
- **Expand**: Clicking a card now shows the full 2000-char classifier window (the actual text the sentiment model evaluated)
- **Dedup grouping**: Changed the grouping key from full raw text to the 2000-char classifier window, so messages that differ only in a prefix (e.g. varying system prompt headers) are correctly deduplicated

## How did you test this code?

Verified the truncation logic produces correct start...end output for texts of varying lengths. Confirmed the grouping key change aligns with the backend classifier window.

pull_requests.title: fix: don't block org deletion retry when user already deleted
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When a user deletes their organization and then immediately deletes their account (enabled by #50875), the async org deletion task can fail on retry. 

The task requires the initiating user to exist, but if they've already deleted their account, retries raise `ValueError("Cannot delete team data: user not found")` and never reach the org/project deletion step. 

This results in orphaned org and project records in Postgres, all the actual data (events, session replays, persons, etc.) gets deleted on the first run, but the org and project records remain.

More context [here](https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C08UM214Z50/p1773321450828089). 

## Changes
 - Don't block `_delete_teams_and_data` when the initiating user no longer exists - the user is only needed for the `deleted_by` audit string in recording deletion
 - Add test for org deletion when user is already deleted

## How did you test this code?
Tests

## Publish to changelog?
No

## Docs update
No

pull_requests.title: feat(notebooks): add MCP tools for notebook CRUD
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The PostHog MCP server has no tools for managing notebooks. This means agents cannot create, read, update, or delete notebooks programmatically.

## Changes

- Add MCP YAML tool definition at `products/notebooks/mcp/tools.yaml` with 5 enabled tools: list, create, retrieve, partial-update, destroy
- Add `help_text` to `NotebookMinimalSerializer` and `NotebookSerializer` fields so descriptions flow through to MCP tool parameters
- Generate Orval API schemas at `services/mcp/src/generated/notebooks/api.ts`
- Kernel/execution endpoints left disabled (not needed for basic CRUD)

## How did you test this code?

All 5 tools tested end-to-end against local dev environment via `posthog-local-testing` MCP:

| Tool | Test | Result |
|---|---|---|
| `notebooks-create` | Created notebook with ProseMirror content | OK |
| `notebooks-retrieve` | Fetched by `short_id` | OK |
| `notebooks-list` | Listed with limit/pagination | OK |
| `notebooks-partial-update` | Updated title + content with version for concurrency control | OK |
| `notebooks-destroy` | Soft-deleted by `short_id` | OK |

Also verified the agent update pattern: retrieve current content + version, modify ProseMirror JSON, send full content back via partial-update.

pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Removes the duplicate `MCP_APPS_BASE_URL: undefined` entry from three `env` object literals in two MCP test files, fixing TS1117 ("An object literal cannot have multiple properties with the same name") errors that broke the `Build Package` CI job.

## Root Cause

Commit `ad9635db` re-added `MCP_APPS_BASE_URL: undefined` to test `Env` objects that already had the key from a prior fix commit (`17d9ccd6ed`, PR #51516). TypeScript strict mode catches duplicate object keys as a hard error (TS1117), causing `tsc --noEmit` to exit with code 2.

## Files Changed

- `services/mcp/tests/unit/tool-filtering.test.ts` — removed duplicate at lines 323 and 349
- `services/mcp/tests/unit/tool-schema-snapshots.test.ts` — removed duplicate at line 22

Fixes: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/23266245779

pull_requests.title: chore(ci): add timeout and partial clone to bundle size job
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The frontend bundle size CI job can hang indefinitely (observed 35+ min) when `compressed-size-action` runs `git fetch -n origin master:master` on a degraded runner. The job has no timeout, so it burns runner minutes for up to 6 hours (GitHub Actions default).

## Changes

- Add `timeout-minutes: 10` to the `frontend-bundle-size` job (normal runtime is ~4 min)
- Add `filter: blob:none` to the checkout step so the action's internal `git fetch` only pulls commit/tree objects, not blobs — blobs are lazy-fetched on demand during `git reset --hard`, and most are already local from the PR checkout

pull_requests.title: fix(web-analytics): handle clock skew in session profile timestamp extraction
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When extracting timestamps from UUIDv7 session IDs, there can be clock skew between the client (which generates the UUID) and the server (which records event timestamps). This can cause the session profile to miss events if the client clock is ahead of the server, resulting in an incomplete session view.

## Changes

- Updated `getTimestampFromUUIDv7()` to apply bidirectional buffers
- Simplified callers of `getTimestampFromUUIDv7()` to use both `startDate` and `endDate` from the function instead of computing custom date ranges
- Updated comments to clarify the purpose of the buffers for handling clock skew and enabling ClickHouse partition pruning (We will be able to do point queries easier on sessions v3)

## How did you test this code?

This is a straightforward refactor that consolidates date buffer logic into a single function. The changes maintain the same query behavior while improving consistency across all callers. Existing tests should continue to pass as the overall time windows remain the same or larger.

## Publish to changelog?

no

pull_requests.title: feat(hogli): add anonymous opt-out telemetry
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

We have no visibility into which hogli commands are used, how often, or in what environments. This makes it hard to prioritize CLI improvements.

We want to leverage `hogli` more for other features in the future; this sets us up to start monitoring some metrics and take advantage of the fact that we have one unified tooling for local dev! 

## Changes

- Anonymous opt-out telemetry via direct HTTP POST to PostHog's `/capture` endpoint (no SDK due to package name collision)
- Opt-out precedence: `POSTHOG_TELEMETRY_OPT_OUT=1` > `DO_NOT_TRACK=1` > config file
- First-run notice on stderr with opt-out instructions (skipped when running `telemetry:off`)
- `telemetry:on`, `telemetry:off`, `telemetry:status` commands
- Two-event model: `command_started` fires before the subcommand runs (ensures coverage for long-running commands like `hogli start` that may be killed), `command_completed` fires after with duration/exit_code/full properties
- Event properties: command name, category, duration, exit code, has_args, terminal_width, OS/arch, CI detection, flox/worktree status
- Background daemon thread with 0.5s flush timeout so CLI exit isn't blocked
- Telemetry wrapping via `CategorizedGroup.invoke()` override using `ctx.meta` for state (idiomatic Click pattern, no module-level singletons)
- `telemetry:status` no longer generates an anonymous ID as a side effect when telemetry is disabled
- Devenv news.txt notice about telemetry collection

## PostHog Das

pull_requests.title: chore(flags): drop unused group_key columns from person table
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

Drops the `group_0_key` through `group_4_key` columns from the ClickHouse person table. These columns were added in migration 0218 for mixed user+group targeting, but the design was revised to use event-time group context instead of persisting group membership on persons.

The columns were never populated (the ingestion PR was closed without merging).

## Changes

- Adds migration 0219 to drop the columns from person table, writable table, Kafka table, and MV
- Updates `posthog/models/person/sql.py` to remove the column definitions
- Updates test snapshots

## Context

The original design for [mixed user+group targeting](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/46288) planned to denormalize group keys onto the person table. After design review, we realized this was unnecessary — groups are event-contextual (attached to events, not persons), and the SDK already provides group context at flag evaluation time.

See the revised design in #46288.

## Test plan

- [x] Migration file follows existing patterns
- [x] Schema test snapshots updated
- [ ] Migration runs successfully in CI

pull_requests.title: feat: Migrate MCP UI apps from self-contained HTML to Workers Static Assets
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The MCP UI Apps build system was using self-contained HTML files with inlined assets, which created several issues:
- Large HTML strings (~12.5MB) being parsed during Worker startup, approaching Cloudflare's 1-second CPU limit
- Complex build process with asset inlining that was difficult to maintain
- No separation between build artifacts and runtime serving

This refactors the system to use Cloudflare Workers Static Assets, providing better performance and a cleaner architecture.

## Changes

**Build System Refactor:**
- Moved from `ui-apps-dist/` to `public/ui-apps/` output directory for Workers Static Assets
- Converted apps from `apps/{name}/index.html` + `main.tsx` structure to single `apps/{name}.tsx` files
- Changed build output from self-contained HTML to separate `main.js` + `styles.css` files
- Removed complex asset inlining plugin in favor of IIFE format bundles

**Runtime Changes:**
- Added `buildAppStubHtml()` function to generate minimal HTML stubs at runtime
- Updated resource registration to use stub HTML that loads JS/CSS from static assets
- Added `MCP_APPS_BASE_URL` environment variable for configuring asset serving
- Enhanced CSP configuration to include the base URL in resource domains

**CI/Build Updates:**
- Updated build validation to check for `main.js` files instead of self-contained HTML
- Modified size reporting to show separate JS and CSS sizes
- Updated gitignore patterns and documentation

**File Structure:**
- Flattened 21 app dire

pull_requests.title: feat(llma): add Traceloop/OpenLLMetry OTel compatibility
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Users instrumenting LLM applications with Traceloop/OpenLLMetry (which covers LangChain, OpenAI, Anthropic, Cohere, Bedrock, VertexAI and ~20 more libraries) send OTel spans using pre-spec `llm.*` and `traceloop.*` namespaces instead of the official `gen_ai.*` conventions. These spans arrive but aren't properly classified or normalized — event types stay as `$ai_span`, messages remain flattened, and deprecated token attributes are dropped.

## Changes

- **Traceloop middleware** (`middleware/traceloop.ts`): Detects Traceloop spans via marker keys (`llm.request.type`, `traceloop.span.kind`, `traceloop.entity.name`), reassembles flattened `gen_ai.prompt.{i}.*` → `$ai_input`, `gen_ai.completion.{i}.*` → `$ai_output_choices`, `llm.request.functions.{i}.*` → `$ai_tools`, and strips Traceloop-specific noise keys.
- **Event reclassification** (`attribute-mapping.ts`): Maps `llm.request.type` (`chat`/`completion` → `$ai_generation`, `embedding`/`embeddings` → `$ai_embedding`) so Traceloop spans get correct event types.
- **Deprecated token fallbacks**: `gen_ai.usage.prompt_tokens` → `$ai_input_tokens` and `gen_ai.usage.completion_tokens` → `$ai_output_tokens` as fallbacks.
- **Rust classifier** (`event_name.rs`): Adds `llm.request.type` as event classifier at the capture layer.

## How did you test this code?

- 77 Node.js unit tests across attribute-mapping, traceloop middleware, and index integration
- 4 Rust unit tests for event name classification
- car

pull_requests.title: feat(llma): add observability to OTel ingestion path
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The OTel ingestion path (`POST /i/v0/ai/otel`) has minimal observability: only 2 counters, no latency histograms, no body size tracking, no error breakdowns by stage, and no Grafana dashboard. Error paths silently convert to HTTP responses without recording `capture_error_by_stage_and_type`.

## Changes

**Rust capture (`otel/mod.rs`, `prometheus.rs`):**
- Add `#[instrument]` tracing on `otel_handler` with `span_count` and `body_size` fields
- Add `capture_ai_otel_body_size_bytes` histogram (request payload size)
- Add `capture_ai_otel_spans_per_request` histogram (batch size distribution)
- Add `capture_ai_otel_requests_total` counter with `format` label (protobuf/json/unknown)
- Add `capture_ai_otel_events_ingested` counter (events sent to Kafka)
- Add `report_internal_error_metrics` at every error path with `otel_`-prefixed stages to avoid collision with v0 endpoint

**Grafana dashboard "LLMA OTel Ingestion" (`llma-otel-ingestion`):**
- Created in the LLMA folder with 4 rows, 15 panels covering endpoint traffic, spans, request characteristics, and Node.js OTel processing
- Dashboard: https://grafana.prod-us.posthog.dev/d/llma-otel-ingestion

## How did you test this code?

- `cargo fmt`, `cargo clippy -p capture`, `cargo test -p capture --no-run` all pass
- All panels will show "No data" until production OTel traffic flows — this is expected (zero traffic today)

## Publish to changelog?

no

N/A

pull_requests.title: fix(personhog): metrics need buckets
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

All histogram metrics in personhog-router and personhog-replica record values in **milliseconds** (e.g. `personhog_replica_db_query_duration_ms`, `personhog_router_backend_duration_ms`, `personhog_replica_db_pool_acquire_duration_ms`), but the Prometheus exporter was using the default bucket boundaries which are designed for **seconds** (`[0.005, 0.01, 0.025, ... 5.0, 10.0]`).

This means nearly every observation falls into the `+Inf` bucket (a typical 50ms query records as `50.0`, far above the max default bucket of `10.0`), making `histogram_quantile()` queries in Grafana return `+Inf` for p50/p90/p99 — rendering the latency dashboards useless.


## Changes

Configure explicit histogram buckets on the `PrometheusBuilder` for both services:

```
[1, 5, 10, 50, 100, 250, 500, 1000, 2000, 5000, 10000] ms
```

This gives meaningful resolution across the range we care about:
- **1–10ms**: fast cache hits / simple queries
- **50–250ms**: typical DB queries
- **500–2000ms**: slow queries, connection pool contention
- **5000–10000ms**: timeout-level latency

Applied identically to `personhog-replica` and `personhog-router` so all histogram metrics (`db_query_duration_ms`, `db_pool_acquire_duration_ms`, `backend_duration_ms`, `db_rows_returned`) get useful bucket distributions.


## How did you test this code?

Metrics change, will verify after deploy.

Confirmed code builds with cargo build.
pull_requests.author: nickbest-ph

pull_requests.title: fix(ui): ignore Enter key during IME composition
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When typing in Japanese, Chinese, Korean, or other languages using an IME (Input Method Editor), pressing Enter to confirm a character selection also triggers the `onPressEnter` handler. This makes it nearly impossible to type in these languages in any input that uses `onPressEnter`, most notably the PostHog AI chat.

For example, typing "ue" in Japanese IME shows candidates (上, 飢え, 植え...), and pressing Enter to select one immediately sends the message instead of confirming the character.

## Changes

Added `!e.nativeEvent.isComposing` check to the Enter key handlers in both `LemonTextArea` and `LemonInput`. When `isComposing` is true, the browser is in the middle of an IME composition session, so the Enter key should only confirm the composition, not trigger the submit action.

- [LemonTextArea.tsx](frontend/src/lib/lemon-ui/LemonTextArea/LemonTextArea.tsx): line 88
- [LemonInput.tsx](frontend/src/lib/lemon-ui/LemonInput/LemonInput.tsx): line 255

The fix is a standard pattern for IME-aware keyboard handling in web apps, using the `isComposing` property on the native keyboard event.

## How did you test this code?

I didn't test this in PostHog AI, but tested it on the login page (because the components are the same)
1. Open PostHog Login page
2. Try typing something in japanese
3. Pressing enter doesn't attempt a login, it confirms the composition.

## Publish to changelog?

no

## Docs update

No docs changes needed.

## 🤖 LLM cont
pull_requests.author: christiaan-ph

pull_requests.title: feat(hogvm): implement JSONExtract in TypeScript and Python STLs
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The generic `JSONExtract` function (ClickHouse-style, with return type hint as last argument) was only implemented in the Rust HogVM. The TypeScript and Python VMs, plus the JavaScript compiler STL, were missing it. The error tracking product already declares `JSONExtract` as a required STL function, so this gap could cause runtime failures when Hog code runs on non-Rust VMs.

Fix: https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C07AA937K9A/p1773237244717669

## Changes

- Add `JSONExtractFn` to the TypeScript HogVM STL (`common/hogvm/typescript/src/stl/stl.ts`)
- Add `JSONExtractGeneric` to the Python HogVM STL (`common/hogvm/python/stl/__init__.py`)
- Add `JSONExtract` to the HogQL-to-JavaScript compiler STL (`posthog/hogql/compiler/javascript_stl.py`)
- Add shared test cases covering string/int extraction, missing keys, nested paths, and object input (`common/hogvm/__tests__/json.hog`)
- Update generated snapshots (`.hoge`, `.js`, `.stdout`)

The implementation matches the Rust behavior: the last argument is the ClickHouse return type hint (ignored), intermediate arguments are path components, and `getNestedValue` handles traversal.

## How did you test this code?

Ran `common/hogvm/test.sh json.hog` which compiles the test and executes it through all three interpreters (Node.js, Python, compiled JS) — all produce identical output.

## Publish to changelog?

no

pull_requests.title: chore(devex): reduce OpenAPI schema generation warnings
pull_requests.body:
**Problem**
`hogli build:openapi-schema` emitted 386 warnings and 36 errors — noisy enough that real issues got buried.

**Changes**
- Added `@extend_schema_field` decorators to ~40 `SerializerMethodField` getters across 30+ serializer files that were defaulting to `string` in the generated schema (71 → 0 type hint warnings)
- Introduced `PostHogAutoSchema` in `documentation.py` that pre-supplies types for `project_id`, `environment_id`, `organization_id`, and `plugin_config_id` path params. `TeamAndOrgViewSetMixin` resolves these at runtime so models never have the field — this caused 200+ path param warnings (214 → 11)
- Added a GitHub Actions problem matcher (`.github/openapi-problem-matcher.json`) so remaining OpenAPI warnings show as inline PR annotations, same pattern as the `ty` checker
- Regenerated frontend/MCP types to reflect the improved schema field types

**Result**
Warnings: 386 → 113 (71% reduction). Errors unchanged at 36 (viewsets without `serializer_class` — separate effort).

Remaining clusters for follow-up: enum naming collisions (~17), operationId collisions (~40), identical component names (~8).

pull_requests.title: fix: race condition for tickets
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Race condition between loadTickets and restoreFromUrlToken on mount, combined with server-side caching of empty results.

## Changes

- Skip caching when the ticket list is empty (total_count > 0 guard). Empty results are cheap to compute (simple query returning 0 rows), and caching them is what causes the stale-after-restore problem.

sidepanelTicketsLogic.ts changes:
-  In afterMount, if ph_conv_restore is in the URL, only fire restoreFromUrlToken() — don't fire loadTickets() concurrently. This eliminates the race.
- In restoreFromUrlToken, moved actions.loadTickets() into the finally block so it always fires after the restore attempt completes (success or failure), ensuring tickets are always loaded.

pull_requests.title: fix(sig): assorted pipeline fixes
pull_requests.body:
Fixes:
-  We saw heartbeat timeouts while waiting for signals to land in clickhouse. Now wait_for_signal_in_clickhouse is called with a more generous heartbeat timeout, and heartbeats more frequently
- We saw wait_for_signals_in_clickouse fail to find signals entirely. Initial I thought this was due to emitting deleted signals, and it not looking for them, but it turns out the problem is actually that the sequential stage emits signals as-it-goes, but we only wait at the end - with an `inserted_at` threshold of 1 minute, the problem seems to be that the first signal in a batch might be emitted more than a minute before `wait_for_signals_in_clickhouse` is called. Changed the threshold to 30 minutes to account for this
- We saw signals in clickhouse that weren't deleted, but were associated with reports in postgres that _were_ deleted. This would cause us to emit a new signal as deleted, but for the rest of time, we'd have new signals being potentially associated with this never-deleted "poison pill" in clickhouse, and therefore the deleted report. Now, if we're associating a new signal with a deleted report, we also go delete all the signals in CH for that report, "healing" the data set over time.
pull_requests.author: oliverb123

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR adjusts the Signals Temporal workflows/activities to be more resilient to slow ClickHouse operations and to prevent “dead” (deleted) signal reports from continuing to attract new signals.

**Changes:**
- Increased Temporal activity `heartbeat_timeout` for ClickHouse-wait activities from 30s to 2m across deletion/reingestion/grouping workflows.
- When a signal matches a deleted report during grouping, trigger a cleanup that soft-deletes the report’s existing sign

pull_requests.title: fix(subscriptions): use correct region property key in subscription insights
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The subscription delivery insights merged in #51345 filter by `$set.region`, but the actual event property is `region` (a top-level super property, not nested under `$set`). This means both US and EU insights would show no data.

## Changes

pull_requests.title: chore(playwright): flake verification for new tests
pull_requests.body:
**What?** Adds a CI step that verifies changed Playwright test files aren't flaky by re-running them with `--repeat-each 10`. Also consolidates test-writing best practices between the README and the Claude Code skill.

**Flake verification**
New script detects spec files touched by the PR via two-dot `git diff --name-only`, then runs them all in a single Playwright invocation with `--repeat-each=10 --retries=0 --max-failures=1`. Simple approach — if you touched a spec file, it gets verified.

**Docs consolidation**
Moved the skill's best-practice rules into `playwright/README.md` so they're useful for humans too. Skill now references the README via `@playwright/README.md` instead of duplicating content.

The annotations test unskip is just to trial the verification step — may revert.

pull_requests.title: fix(phai): pass user context through AI query execution pipeline
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The AI query execution pipeline currently doesn't have access to the user context when executing queries. This limits the ability to track which user initiated a query or apply user-specific logic during query execution.

## Changes

- Added `TYPE_CHECKING` import and conditional `User` type import in `query_executor.py` to avoid circular imports
- Updated `AssistantQueryExecutor.__init__()` to accept an optional `user` parameter and store it as `self._user`
- Passed `user=self._user` when calling `execute_query()` in the `aexecute_query()` method
- Updated `execute_and_format_query()` function signature to accept an optional `user` parameter and pass it to `AssistantQueryExecutor`
- Updated `InsightContext` to accept and store an optional `user` parameter, passing it through to `execute_and_format_query()`
- Updated both SQL execution tools (`execute_sql/mcp_tool.py` and `execute_sql/tool.py`) to pass `user=self._user` when creating `InsightContext` instances

## How did you test this code?

This is a straightforward refactoring that threads user context through the existing query execution pipeline. The changes are additive (optional parameters with defaults) and maintain backward compatibility. Existing tests should continue to pass as the `user` parameter defaults to `None`.

## Publish to changelog?

No

pull_requests.title: feat(sandbox): Add dynamic skill installation to sandbox containers
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

PostHog agent skills need to be dynamically injected into sandbox containers at build time. Currently, there's no mechanism to download and install skills from GitHub releases into the container environments where agents run.

## Changes

- **New script**: `pull-skills.sh` - Downloads PostHog agent skills from GitHub releases and installs them to both Claude Code plugin discovery (`/scripts/plugins/posthog/skills/`) and Codex agent discovery (`~/.agents/skills/`) directories
  - Handles flexible zip archive structures (skills at root or nested)
  - Falls back gracefully if download fails or no skills found
  - Creates required `plugin.json` configuration
  - Validates installation by counting `SKILL.md` files

- **Updated Dockerfiles**: Both `Dockerfile.sandbox-base` and `Dockerfile.sandbox-notebook` now execute `pull-skills.sh` during image build to inject skills before agent startup

- **Updated CI workflow**: Added `products/*/skills/**` to the change detection filter in `cd-sandbox-base-image.yml` so that skill updates trigger sandbox image rebuilds

## How did you test this code?

The changes are straightforward infrastructure additions:
- Script syntax is valid bash with proper error handling (`set -euo pipefail`)
- Dockerfile integration follows existing patterns in both images
- CI filter addition is a simple configuration change
- The script includes defensive checks for missing/malformed archives and gracefully continues on download failures

## Summary

Adds a `SERVICE_MODE` env var to the feature-flags Rust service to support splitting into two independent fleets: one for `/flags` + `/decide` traffic and one for `/flags/definitions` traffic.

pull_requests.title: fix(hogai): preserve layouts in upsert_dashboard updates
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Users updating dashboards via `upsert_dashboard` were getting unintended layout mutations.
When they only added or removed insights, existing tiles were resized/repositioned, which broke carefully arranged dashboard templates and forced manual re-layout.
Reorder flows also had edge cases where rows were over-compacted and could pull in extra tiles unexpectedly.

## Changes

Added explicit layout strategy to `upsert_dashboard` updates:

- `layout_mode="preserve_existing"` (default): update active insight without mutating existing layout.
- `layout_mode="reflow_all"`: explicitly opt in to recompute layouts when the user asks to reorder/rearrange.

Updated preserve behavior:

- Existing insight tiles keep their `layouts` unchanged.
- Newly created insight tiles are created with empty `layouts` so client placement can occur without mutating existing tile geometry.

Updated reflow behavior (row-based):

- Preserve insight order from `insight_ids`.
- Use existing `sm` `w/h` as baseline (with defaults when missing).
- Normalize each row height to tallest tile.
- Equalize widths when a row is not exactly 12 columns.
- Add overflow adaptation for insert/reorder cases (including “insert between two”).
- Prevent over-compaction by not pulling a 4th tile into an already full 3-tile row.
- Keep full-width single-tile rows stable.
- Respect fixed non-insight tile vertical bands (for example, text tiles) to avoid overlap during reflow.

## How did yo

pull_requests.title: fix(ds): handle MySQL zero dates ('0000-00-00') in data warehouse imports
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

This PR fixes ArrowInvalid: Failed to parse string: '0000-00-00' as a scalar of type date32[day] errors when importing MySQL tables that contain zero dates.

## Changes

  - Added _safe_convert_date and _safe_convert_datetime custom PyMySQL converters that return None for invalid/zero dates instead of passing raw strings through
  - Added _MYSQL_SAFE_CONVERSIONS dict overriding FIELD_TYPE.DATE, FIELD_TYPE.DATETIME, and FIELD_TYPE.TIMESTAMP
  - Applied conv=_MYSQL_SAFE_CONVERSIONS to both pymysql.connect() calls in mysql_source()
  - Forced date/datetime/timestamp columns to always be nullable in MySQLColumn.to_arrow_field(), since MySQL zero dates are semantically null
  - Added unit tests for safe converters and date column nullability

Test pass

pull_requests.title: perf(ci): pin setup-uv cache-dependency-glob to uv.lock
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- **Fix `setup-uv` spending ~50s scanning `node_modules`** when computing the cache key. The default `cache-dependency-glob` in `astral-sh/setup-uv` recursively scans the entire workspace for `**/*requirements*.txt`, `**/pyproject.toml`, `**/uv.lock`, etc. When `node_modules` is already installed (pnpm runs before uv in several jobs), this traversal crawls through thousands of nested directories.
- **Pin `cache-dependency-glob: uv.lock`** across all 19 `setup-uv` instances that have `enable-cache: true`. The root `uv.lock` is the canonical lockfile for the main project — it produces the same cache key without the expensive filesystem scan.

### Evidence

[Run 22902446195 / job 66452504981](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/22902446195/job/66452504981?pr=50469): `Install uv` step took **113s** (vs typical ~4s) because the glob spent ~50s traversing `node_modules` after pnpm had already installed.

### Impact

Saves ~50s per job on any workflow where pnpm installs before uv (product tests, migration checks, turbo, MCP, etc.). Across all CI runs this adds up to significant compute savings.


---

> [!NOTE]
> Created by [Mendral](https://mendral.com). Tag @mendral-app with feedback or questions.

pull_requests.title: chore: kafka-deduplicator normalize remote layout
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Checkpoint metadata.json and store files (SSTs, MANIFEST, etc.) live under different S3 path prefixes. Metadata is at `<s3_key_prefix>/<topic>/<partition>/<checkpoint_id>/metadata.json` (unhashed), while object files are at `<hash>/<s3_key_prefix>/<topic>/<partition>/<checkpoint_id>/<filename>` (hashed). This splits each checkpoint across two S3 "folders" and concentrates all metadata list operations on a single unhashed prefix, creating a hot-prefix risk under load.

Additionally, `list_recent_checkpoints` recursively lists every file across all checkpoints in the import window, then filters down to just metadata.json keys, wasting S3 LIST API calls.

## Changes

- Move metadata.json under the same hashed prefix as store files, so each checkpoint lives entirely under `<hash>/<s3_key_prefix>/<topic>/<partition>/<checkpoint_id>/`
- Replace recursive `list_with_offset` with shallow `list_with_delimiter` in `list_recent_checkpoints`, reducing the listing from paginating through all objects to a single delimiter-based call that returns only checkpoint folder names

## How did you test this code?

<!-- Briefly describe the steps you took. -->
<!-- Include automated tests if possible, otherwise describe the manual testing routine. -->
<!-- If you are an agent writing this, do NOT include manual tasks you have NOT completed. You can clearly outline you're simply an agent and you haven't tested this manually except for code-based unit/integration tests -->

pull_requests.title: fix: use freeform pricing type in services catalog
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Stripe's services sync rejects `"type": "custom"` as a paid pricing type. The [APP spec](https://github.com/agentic-provisioning/posthog-spec/blob/master/spec.md) defines valid types as `"freeform"` or `"stripe_price"`.

Error from Stripe: `"Provider paid pricing type is invalid: custom"`

## Changes

Changed `"type": "custom"` to `"type": "freeform", "freeform": "Usage-based pricing"` in both the pay_as_you_go plan and the analytics deployable component pricing.

## How did you test this code

Verified the current services endpoint returns the updated pricing format. Stripe confirmed the sync was failing with the "custom" type error.

## Summary

- DuckLake hosts (`*.postwh.com`) resolve to internal/private IPs but are PostHog-managed and safe to connect to
- Adding a postgres source with a `postwh.com` hostname (e.g. `entirely-chief-wildcat.us.postwh.com`) was failing with "Hosts with internal IP addresses are not allowed" because DNS resolution returned private addresses
- Adds a whitelist check in `_is_host_safe()` for hostnames ending in `.postwh.com`, which is the shared codepath used by both the SQL editor source creation (`posthog/hogql/direct_connection.py`) and the temporal data imports pipeline

## Test plan

- [x] Added parameterized tests for various `postwh.com` subdomains (US, EU, bare)
- [x] Added test confirming fake suffixes like `evil.postwh.com.evil.example.com` are still blocked
- [x] All 34 mixin tests pass

reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR updates the cloud host-safety validation used by Temporal data imports and direct Postgres connections to allow PostHog-managed DuckLake hosts under `*.postwh.com`, even though they resolve to private/internal IPs.

**Changes:**
- Whitelist hostnames ending in `.postwh.com` inside `_is_host_safe()` to bypass internal/private IP blocking on cloud.

pull_requests.title: fix(dwh): prevent SSL cert permission error in SQL editor postgres connections
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Fixes "could not open certificate file `/root/.postgresql/postgresql.crt`: Permission denied" when connecting to external Postgres sources via the SQL editor
- libpq automatically probes `~/.postgresql/` for client certs when `sslmode` is `prefer` or `require`. In containers where `$HOME=/root/` but the process runs as non-root, this fails with a permission error
- The data imports code (`posthog/temporal/data_imports/sources/postgres/postgres.py`) already sets `sslcert`/`sslkey`/`sslrootcert` to dummy paths on every connection, but the SQL editor's direct query path in `posthog/hogql/query.py` only did this for DuckLake hosts (`.us.postwh.com`)
- Moves the SSL cert overrides into the base connection kwargs so they apply to all connections

## Test plan

- [ ] Connect to an external Postgres source via the SQL editor and verify the connection succeeds without the certificate permission error
- [ ] Verify DuckLake connections still work (they still get `sslmode=require` override)

pull_requests.title: chore(personhog): better observability
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The personhog-router → personhog-replica service is experiencing latency spikes, but current metrics are too coarse to isolate the root cause. The existing instrumentation measures end-to-end request time and total DB query duration, but can't distinguish between:

- Time waiting for a DB connection from the pool vs actual query execution
- Primary pool latency vs replica pool latency
- gRPC transport/serialization overhead between router and replica
- Connection pool pressure and saturation
- Error categories (connection failures vs pool exhaustion vs query errors)
- Query result cardinality issues

This makes it impossible to build a Grafana dashboard that pinpoints where latency is being introduced in the request path.


## Changes

**Pool identification** — Set `pool_name` on both `PoolConfig` instances (`"primary"` / `"replica"`) so the existing `db_connection_created_total` counter from common-database can distinguish connection churn per pool.

**Error type counters** — Added `personhog_replica_storage_errors_total` counter with `error_type` (connection/pool_exhausted/query) and `operation` labels in `log_and_convert_error`. Previously errors were only logged.

**Pool health gauges** — New `pool_monitor.rs` spawns a background task polling pool stats every 10s (configurable via `POOL_MONITOR_INTERVAL_SECS`), reporting `personhog_replica_db_pool_size`, `personhog_replica_db_pool_idle`, and `personhog_replica_db_pool_max` per pool.

**Po

pull_requests.title: fix(workflows): Don't send empty HTML body for plaintext emails
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Customers sending plaintext-only emails via workflows receive blank emails. When the email editor's "Plain text" tab is used (setting `html: ""`), SES still receives an empty `Body.Html` field. SES wraps this in a `multipart/alternative` MIME message and injects its own tracking pixel into the HTML part. Email clients like Gmail prefer the HTML part per RFC 2046, rendering just a hidden 1x1 pixel - a blank email.

Confirmed via Gmail "Show original" on a production email - the `text/html` MIME part contains only an AWS tracking pixel.

## Changes

- Skip `Body.Html` in the SES request when `params.html` is empty, so SES sends a single-part `text/plain` email
- Same treatment for the maildev (local dev) path
- Added test asserting `Body.Html` is undefined for plaintext-only emails

## How did you test this code?

- Verified the root cause by inspecting raw MIME source of a production email in Gmail
- Sent a plaintext-only email via SES (dev account) with Body.Html omitted — Gmail source confirms single-part Content-Type: text/plain with no HTML part
- Added a unit test (should send plaintext-only email when html is empty) that mocks the SES client and asserts Body.Html is undefined

## Publish to changelog?

No

pull_requests.title: chore(devex): require `update-snapshots` label for snapshot auto-updates
pull_requests.body:
**Problem**
Snapshot auto-updates run on every human PR by default. When flaky snapshots update on unrelated changes, devs get confused by unexpected commits and noise in their PRs.

**Change**
Storybook and Playwright CI now default to check mode — snapshots must match or the test fails. To opt in to auto-updates, add the `update-snapshots` label and re-run CI (or just push a new commit with the label applied).

When tests fail in check mode, a PR comment explains the two options:
- Add label if the snapshot changes are intentional
- Ignore if flaky — wait for fixes on master

Only mendral-app[bot] still gets update mode automatically (fully automated, no human in the loop).

**Note:** the `update-snapshots` label needs to be created in the repo settings.

pull_requests.title: fix(session-recordings): add tier to snapshot throttle scope for prom metrics
pull_requests.body:
## Changes

The `rate_limit_exceeded_total` Prometheus counter's `scope` label was always `snapshots_burst` or `snapshots_sustained` regardless of org tier, making it impossible to tell from Grafana/metrics which tier was hitting rate limits.

After `_apply_tier_rates` resolves the tier, `self.scope` is updated to include it — e.g. `snapshots_burst_free`, `snapshots_sustained_paid`, `snapshots_burst_enterprise`. This flows into both the Prometheus `scope` label and the DRF throttle cache key.

The cache key becoming tier-specific is intentional: different tiers have different rates and should have separate rate limit buckets.

For non-personal-API-key requests (session auth) where tier lookup is skipped, the scope is unchanged.

pull_requests.title: chore(auth): deprecate legacy personal API key NULL scopes
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Personal API keys created before scope management was introduced have `scopes=NULL` in the database. This was treated as implicit all-access, which is error-prone.

For example, if saving the scopes ever runs into a serialization error, a common default is to use `None`. But in this case, `None` means all-access which is counter to expectations.

The existing code has been marked with "TRICKY". I don't like tricky code. We can make the code boring with a simple migration.

## Changes

- Adds a migration to backfill all `scopes=NULL` personal API keys to `scopes=["*"]` (explicit all-access)
- Removes the `scopes IS NULL → all access` special case from `APIScopePermission` — NULL is now treated as no access
- Updates the API serializer to return `[]` instead of `["*"]` when scopes is NULL
- Updates activity logging to use `or []` instead of `or ["*"]`
- Removes the `Q(scopes__isnull=True)` branch from the team access cache flag evaluation query

## How did you test this code?

pull_requests.title: fix(propdefs): cache eviction bug in PropertyDefinition for group props
pull_requests.body:
## Problem
When a batch write of `PropertyDefinition` records exhausts retries and has failed, we attempt to evict the batch entries from the pod-local cache. This ensures the next time we see the property in the event stream for this team, it gets another chance at being written.

But! Due to our unfortunate caching strategy (optimistically cache then evict on write fail) this fails because we resolve groups after we push default entries (of `event_type: Unresolved(...)`) into the `PropertyDefinition` local cache, but a failed write will contain an identical post-resolution entry of `event_type: Resolved(..., N)` so will not be evicted.

This means many group properties never get the chance to self-heal when the next corresponding event is viewed, until the pod cache LRU evicts the entry, or the pod is cycled.
<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Does this fix an issue? Uncomment the line below with the issue ID to automatically close it when merged -->
<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->

## Changes
Touch up `PropertyDefinition.entry_type` values just prior to eviction, after a batch write fail.

Long term fix is to restructure the caching to happen _after_ successful writes, not before 🤦 but this is higher lift and this small fix will stop the bleeding in the meantime
<!-- If there are frontend changes, please include screenshots. -->
<!-- If a reference design was involved, include a link to the relevant Figma frame! -

pull_requests.title: fix(cdp): Klime destination - add userTraits for identify
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The Klime destination template gates **all** property/trait inclusion behind the `include_all_properties` boolean. When this is `false` (the default), three things break:

1. **Track events** send no event properties at all -- custom properties like `plan`, `amount`, `currency` are silently dropped
2. **Group events** send no group traits -- `$group_set` data (name, plan, etc.) is lost
3. **Identify events** send no user traits -- email, name, and any other person properties are missing

This means customers using the default configuration receive events in Klime with empty properties/traits, making the data nearly useless for segmentation and filtering.

## Root cause

The `include_all_properties` toggle was designed as a safety valve for `person.properties` iteration, which triggers an expensive DB lookup in HogVM and causes timeouts for persons with many properties. However, the same gate was incorrectly applied to:

- **`event.properties`** (track) -- this is already in-memory, cheap, and bounded
- **`event.properties.$group_set`** (group) -- this is a small in-event object, also in-memory

Only `person.properties` iteration (identify) actually needs the gate, but even there, iterating all person properties is not a viable approach due to the timeout risk. The `include_all_properties` toggle is effectively obsolete and should not be relied upon.

## Changes

### Track events
Removed the `include_all_properties` gate from `event.properties

pull_requests.title: feat(flags): pre-compute flag dependency metadata in hypercache
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The Rust feature-flags service builds a full petgraph-based dependency graph on every request to resolve flag dependency ordering. This is redundant work — the flag list changes only when the Django hypercache is refreshed, not per-request.

## Changes

Adds pre-computed dependency metadata to the Django hypercache payload so downstream consumers (the Rust service) can skip graph construction at request time.

- **Django (`flags_cache.py`)**: Adds `_compute_flag_dependencies()` using Kahn's algorithm (layered topological sort) to pre-compute `evaluation_metadata` — dependency stages, flags with missing/cyclic deps, and transitive deps — and includes it in the hypercache payload alongside `flags`.
- **Docs**: Updates `flag-evaluation-engine.md` and `hypercache-system.md` to describe the new metadata format and evaluation flow.

The hypercache payload now includes an `evaluation_metadata` field alongside `flags`. For a chain `flag_a → flag_b → flag_c`:

```json
{
  "flags": [ ... ],
  "evaluation_metadata": {
    "dependency_stages": [[3], [2], [1]],
    "flags_with_missing_deps": [],
    "transitive_deps": {
      "1": [2, 3],
      "2": [3],
      "3": []
    }
  }
}
```

- `dependency_stages`: flags grouped by evaluation order — stage 0 has no deps, stage 1 depends only on stage 0, etc.
- `flags_with_missing_deps`: flag IDs with missing, cyclic, or transitively broken dependencies (fail closed).
- `transitive_deps`: full transitive closure per flag, used for key-

pull_requests.title: fix(flags): validate filter field types to prevent serde failures
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The Rust flag evaluation service uses strict serde deserialization for flag definitions. When the Django API allows non-conforming types into the `filters` JSON column (e.g., boolean `false` instead of `null`, string `"100"` instead of integer `100`), the Rust service fails to parse the entire team's flag cache, returning 500 errors on every `/flags` and `/decide` request for the affected team.

## Changes

Add strict type validation in `FeatureFlagSerializer.validate_filters()` that rejects non-conforming types with 400 errors instead of silently accepting them:

- `aggregation_group_type_index`: reject non-integer types (bool, string, float)
- `group.variant`: reject non-string types (bool, int)
- `group.rollout_percentage`: reject non-numeric types (string, bool) and non-finite values (NaN, Infinity)
- `group.properties[].group_type_index`: reject non-integer types (bool, string, float)
- `multivariate.variants[].rollout_percentage`: reject non-numeric types, non-finite values, and null (non-optional in Rust model)

## How did you test this code?

22 parameterized unit tests covering all bad-value types across the validated fields, plus positive tests verifying valid values are preserved.

## Publish to changelog?

no

## Docs update

No docs changes needed.

pull_requests.title: fix(batch-exports): handle memory errors in backfill estimation and optimize persons query
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The backfill estimation query can sometimes fail with a `ClickHouseMemoryLimitExceededError`, which causes the entire backfill workflow to fail. The backfill should still proceed even if we can't estimate the record count.

Additionally, the persons count query scans more data than necessary because it doesn't pre-filter rows by timestamp before the GROUP BY.

## Changes

- Wrap the backfill estimation calls in `get_backfill_info` with a try/except for `ClickHouseMemoryLimitExceededError`. When caught, log a warning and return `total_records_count=None` so the workflow continues without an estimate.
- Add a `lower_bound_condition` (`_timestamp >= start_at`) to the WHERE clauses in the persons count query's `new_persons` and `distinct_ids` CTEs. This pre-filters rows before the GROUP BY/HAVING, reducing memory usage. Only the lower bound is used (not the upper bound) to avoid incorrectly excluding person rows that have versions both inside and outside the backfill range.

## How did you test this code?

- Existing unit tests.
- Query performance was tested in production on a team with a lot of persons:
  - For a large backfill range, memory usage was reduced by around 15%
  - For a smaller backfill range, memory usage was reduced by around 90% (since the pre-filtering has much more of an effect)
  - Row counts in each case were identical

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

N/A

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored with Claude Code.
pull_requests.author: rossgray

pull_requests.title: feat: view-level query coalescing
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Does this fix an issue? Uncomment the line below with the issue ID to automatically close it when merged -->
<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->

I added query coalescing at the query runner level here: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/49295. However, this approach is brittle because it involves storing the leader's exceptions message in Redis and then wrapping it in a general QueryCoalescingError for the followers to return. To preserve the status code, followers would need to reconstruct the original exception from Redis. This approach wouldn't work if we wanted to extend it to exceptions that are defined at runtime. 

## Changes

Move query coalescing to the Django view level. This uses the same mechanism as before, where a signal in Redis indicates if the leader has completed or errored and a heartbeat to extend the leader lock TTL. Remove the query runner level coalescing. There still is a dry run mode that will verify how many queries can be coalesced. 

These are the four code paths
- Success: leader completes and sets the key in Redis. Followers see the signal, fall through to normal execution, and hit the freshly written cache.
- Error: leader gets a 4xx/5xx response, stores the rendered HTTP response (status + body) in Redis. Followers see the error signal, read the stored response, and replay it.
- Timeout (heartbeat alive): leader is still running (lock ex

pull_requests.title: feat(flags): hypercache flag provider
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Every Django process (web workers, Celery workers, Temporal workers) independently polls the `/api/feature_flag/local_evaluation/` endpoint every 90 seconds to fetch feature flag definitions. Multiplied across all workers, this creates significant unnecessary load on team 2 (PostHog's own project). The monolith already has a HyperCache infrastructure that maintains flag definitions in Redis (updated via Django signals on flag changes + periodic refresh tasks), but the SDK's module-level API doesn't use it.

## Solution

Create a read-only `HyperCacheFlagProvider` that implements the SDK's `FlagDefinitionCacheProvider` protocol, reading flag definitions directly from the existing HyperCache instead of polling the API. The provider:

- Returns `False` from `should_fetch_flag_definitions()` so the SDK never polls the API
- Reads from `flag_definitions_hypercache.get_from_cache(team_id)` on each evaluation cycle
- Falls back gracefully — if the cache is empty and no flags are loaded yet, the SDK's emergency fallback logic fetches from the API using `personal_api_key`

## Changes

**`posthog/feature_flags/sdk_cache_provider.py`** (new)
- `HyperCacheFlagProvider` class implementing `FlagDefinitionCacheProvider`
- Lazy-imports `flag_definitions_hypercache` to avoid circular imports
- All errors caught and logged — returns `None` to let the SDK handle fallback

**`posthog/apps.py`**
- Configures `posthoganalytics.flag_definition_cache_provider` with `H

pull_requests.title: fix(oauth): add logging and better error messages for authorization failures
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When users click "Authorize" on the OAuth consent screen (POST /oauth/authorize/), failures show a generic "Something went wrong" toast with no details. There's no backend logging either, making it impossible to diagnose authorization failures for DCR/MCP clients.

This came up via a support ticket where a Gumloop user consistently gets this error when trying to connect to PostHog's MCP server. We found `$exception` events in PostHog showing `Non-OK response [POST /oauth/authorize/] (status 400)` but the actual error reason is lost.

## Changes

**Backend** (`posthog/api/oauth/views.py`):
- Log `oauth_authorize_validation_error` with client_id and serializer errors when validation fails
- Log `oauth_authorize_invalid_client` when client_id doesn't match any application
- Log `oauth_authorize_toolkit_error` when OAuthToolkitError is raised during authorization

**Frontend** (`oauthAuthorizeLogic.ts`):
- Extract error detail from `error.detail`, `error.data.error`, or `error.data.error_description`
- Show the actual error message in the toast instead of the generic fallback

## How did you test this code?

I'm an agent - I have not manually tested this. The changes are minimal logging additions and a frontend string change. Existing OAuth tests should continue to pass as no behavior is changed.

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

N/A

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored by Claude Code while investigating a support ticket about OAuth authorization failures for D

pull_requests.title: fix(batch-exports): coerce string types from EncryptedJSONField in batch export inputs
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

`BatchExportDestination.config` is an `EncryptedJSONField` that doesn't lways preserve Python types — boolean and integer values can be stored/retrieved as strings (e.g. `"True"` instead of `True`, `"5432"` instead of `5432`). When `sync_batch_export` unpacks these config values into workflow input dataclasses via `**destination_config`, fields like `use_variant_type` on Databricks receive strings instead of bools, causing Temporal workflow errors at runtime.

Some dataclasses had ad-hoc `__post_init__` workarounds (S3, Postgres, BigQuery), but Databricks and AzureBlob didn't, and it's easy to forget to add these.

Wonder if in future it makes sense to use Pydantic models rather than Dataclasses to provide better type safety/validation...

## Changes

- Added a generic `__post_init__` to `BaseBatchExportInputs` that introspects all `bool` and `int` typed fields (including `int | None` unions) and coerces string values to their declared types

## How did you test this code?

- Added unit tests in `products/batch_exports/backend/tests/test_service.py` covering bool and int coercion for Databricks, S3, Postgres, BigQuery, AzureBlob, including edge cases (case insensitivity, `None` preservation, actual booleans preserved)
- Added integration tests in `posthog/management/commands/test/test_update_batch_export_schedules.py` that create real Temporal schedules with string-typed config and verify the decoded workflow args contain proper Python types (Databr
pull_requests.author: rossgray

pull_requests.title: chore(flags): support ProjectSecretAPIKey for Flags in Rust
pull_requests.body:
Closes #51090 

## Problem

The `/flags/definitions` Rust endpoint authenticates `phs_` tokens only against the legacy `posthog_team.secret_api_token` column. PostHog now has a managed `ProjectSecretAPIKey` model (table `posthog_projectsecretapikey`) that stores hashed secret keys per-team with scopes, so Rust must also check this table so managed project secret API keys work on the Rust endpoint.

Django's `ProjectSecretAPIKeyAuthentication` currently only looks up the legacy `Team.secret_api_token`. The `find_project_secret_api_key()` method on the model exists but isn't wired into Django's auth flow yet. This Rust implementation is intentionally ahead of Django.

## Changes

**Auth flow for `phs_` tokens** (in `authenticate_flag_definitions`):
1. Try `posthog_projectsecretapikey` (hashed lookup with scope validation) → metric `("method", "project_secret_api_key")`
2. If not found, fall back to `posthog_team.secret_api_token` (legacy) → metric `("method", "secret_api_key")`
3. Non-`phs_` tokens → PAK path (unchanged)

**`auth.rs`:**
- Renamed `hash_personal_api_key()` → `hash_key_value()` — shared by PAK and project secret key (same `sha256$<hex>` format, verified identical to Python's `hash_key_value(token, mode="sha256")` with pinned test values)
- Added `validate_project_secret_api_key_for_team()` — hashes token, queries `posthog_projectsecretapikey` by `secure_value`, validates scopes and `team_id`

**`test_utils.rs`:**
- Added `create_project_secret

pull_requests.title: fix(llma): isolate LLM gateway unit tests from CI environment
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The LLM gateway CI has 6 persistent test failures:

**3 unit test failures** in `test_model_registry.py`: The `mock_settings` fixture patches `get_settings()` to control which providers are "configured", but `_get_configured_providers()` also checks env vars directly. CI intentionally sets `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` and `FIREWORKS_API_KEY` for integration tests, but the unit tests weren't isolating themselves from these, causing openrouter/fireworks models to appear available when the mock settings say they shouldn't be.

**3 integration test failures** in `test_fireworks_sdk.py`: The Fireworks deployment auto-scales to zero, so cold-start 503s exceed the 2-retry window.

## Changes

- Add a module-level `clear_env_api_keys` autouse fixture to `test_model_registry.py` so unit tests only reflect mock settings, not the CI environment
- Remove the now-redundant class-scoped duplicate in `TestProviderFiltering`
- Mark Fireworks and OpenRouter integration tests as `xfail(strict=False)` so cold-start failures don't block CI

## How did you test this code?

Ran `test_model_registry.py` with `OPENROUTER_API_KEY=fake-key FIREWORKS_API_KEY=fake-key` set — all 36 tests pass (previously 3 failed).

## Publish to changelog?

no

## Docs update

no

pull_requests.title: feat(recordings): add tier-based rate limiting for snapshot API
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Adds tier-based rate limiting to the recording snapshots API for personal API key requests
- Free-plan orgs: 60/min burst, 300/hr sustained
- Paid (scale/boost): 90/min burst, 500/hr sustained
- Enterprise: 120/min burst, 600/hr sustained
- Tier detected from `Organization.available_product_features` (SAML/SCIM = enterprise, has features = paid, empty = free)
- Result cached per team for 12 hours to avoid DB lookups on every request
- Only applies to personal API key requests — session auth and sharing tokens skip tier lookup entirely
- Falls back to free-tier rates on lookup error, missing team_id, or unknown tier
- Guards against falsy/non-dict entries in `available_product_features`

## Test plan

- [x] Parameterized unit tests for tier detection from features (none/empty/paid/enterprise/falsy entries)
- [x] Cached org tier lookup tests (cache hit, cache miss, nonexistent team)
- [x] Rate config validation tests for all tier/scope combinations
- [x] Unknown tier fallback test (exercises throttle with `_apply_tier_rates("mystery_tier")`)
- [x] Error path test (patches `get_cached_org_tier` to raise, asserts free-tier rates applied)
- [x] Auth gating test (non-personal-API-key requests skip tier lookup entirely)
- [x] Missing team_id test (applies free-tier rates explicitly)

pull_requests.title: perf: Related persons query
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

  The related persons query (used to show people related to a group) joins through `person_distinct_id2` via a subquery on every request. This can be optimized by querying `person_distinct_id2` directly with
  an `IN` subquery on event distinct_ids, avoiding the full table join. The optimization is gated behind a feature flag (`optimized-related-people-query`) for experimentation and safe rollout.

Part of https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/40472
  ## Changes

  - Add an optimized query path in `RelatedActorsQuery` behind a feature flag
  - Create test file for related actors query
  - Add 4 parametrized behavioral tests (8 test cases) that run against both control and optimized paths:
    - Basic inclusion/exclusion of related people
    - Soft-deleted `person_distinct_id2` rows are excluded
    - Reassigned distinct_ids resolve to the new person
    - Multiple distinct_ids mapping to the same person are deduplicated

## How did you test this code?
Unit tests and checking in the UI if the query was working as expected
pull_requests.author: arthurdedeus

pull_requests.title: feat(subscriptions): add delivery outcome tracking
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

We need success and failure events to build a success-rate percentage graph in PostHog. However, subscription deliveries run as Temporal activities. When a pod OOMs, metrics emitted from within the activity may not make it out of the pod. 

## Changes

- Add a new lightweight `emit_subscription_delivery_outcome_events_activity` that batch-loads team IDs and emits `subscription_delivery_succeeded` / `subscription_delivery_exhausted` PostHog events

The event emit activity runs from the workflowlevel (not inside the delivery activity), so Temporal's server-side state persistence ensures it executes even if the delivery worker OOMs.

## How did you test this code?
- 3 new tests added:
  - Successful deliveries emit `subscription_delivery_succeeded` events
  - Failed deliveries emit `subscription_delivery_exhausted` events
  - Mixed success/failure - one failing subscription doesn't block others from delivering

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

N/A

pull_requests.title: chore(flags): migration for new format of holdout_groups
pull_requests.body:
**Part 3 of #47931** 

## Summary

- Backfills the new `holdout` field on feature flags that were created previously and only have the legacy `holdout_groups` format
- Parses `holdout-{id}` from the variant string and maps `rollout_percentage` to `exclusion_percentage`
- Skips flags that already have `holdout` (idempotent)
- Data-only migration (`elidable=True`), safe to skip on fresh installs

## Context

Phases 1 (Django dual-write) and 2 (Rust reads new format with fallback) are merged. All newly created/updated holdouts already write both `holdout_groups` and `holdout` in their flag filters. This migration backfills the ~20 flags created before Phase 1 so Phase 4 can eventually remove the `holdout_groups` fallback.

### Production scope

```sql
SELECT
    count(*) AS flags_with_holdout_groups,
    count(DISTINCT team_id) AS teams
FROM posthog_featureflag
WHERE filters::jsonb ? 'holdout_groups'
  AND filters::jsonb -> 'holdout_groups' != 'null'::jsonb
  AND jsonb_array_length(filters::jsonb -> 'holdout_groups') > 0
  AND deleted = false;
```

| Region | flags_with_holdout_groups | teams |
  |--------|--------------------------|-------|
  | US     | 12                       | 9     |
  | EU     | 8                        | 7     |

  ### Why no explicit cache invalidation

  `bulk_update` doesn't fire `post_save` signals, so the flags cache won't be immediately refreshed. This is fine because Phase 2's Rust reader already falls back to `hold

pull_requests.title: fix(phrocs): kill process group on stop to prevent zombie processes
pull_requests.body:
**Problem:** when phrocs exits, `Stop()` sends SIGTERM to the direct bash child only. The actual process (tsx watch, vite, node) is a grandchild that never receives the signal and orphans. People keep having to manually kill zombie node processes via Activity Monitor.

**Fix:** set `Setpgid: true` on spawn so each child gets its own process group, then `syscall.Kill(-pgid, SIGTERM)` in `Stop()` to terminate the entire tree atomically. Same pattern as `bin/start-go-service`.

mprocs has the same issue ([pvolok/mprocs#76](https://github.com/pvolok/mprocs/issues/76)) but since it's third-party there's no clean fix — this is phrocs-only.
pull_requests.author: webjunkie

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR updates `phrocs` subprocess lifecycle management so that stopping a process terminates the entire spawned process tree (not just the immediate `bash` child), addressing orphaned `node/tsx/vite` processes after phrocs exits.

**Changes:**
- Spawn each managed command in its own process group (`SysProcAttr.Setpgid = true`).
- Update `Stop()` to send `SIGTERM` to the whole process group via `syscall.Kill(-pid, SIGTERM)`.
- Expand inline documentation explaining why

pull_requests.title: fix(cdp): group team mapping entries by team ID and autofill remainder percentages
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The getProducerTeamMapping function splits config strings by comma and processes each entry independently. This means configs like `79155:*:kafka,79155:hog:postgres-v2:0.001` fail - the second entry overwrite the first, and then getProducerMapping throws because there's no * default.

## Changes

- Group team mapping entries by team ID before parsing, so multiple entries for the same team are parsed together
- Add requireDefault option to getProducerMapping so team mappings can omit the * default (inherited from global)
- Auto-fill remainder percentages from the team's * default or the global * default (e.g., hog:postgres-v2:0.001 gets kafka:0.999 appended)
- Merge each team's routing on top of the global mapping so unspecified queues fall through

## How did you test this code?

Automated tests covering:
- E2E routing for 79155:*:kafka,79155:hog:postgres-v2:0.001 (percentage roll, non-hog queues, other teams unaffected)
- E2E routing for global fallback (team with no *) and global queue inheritance
- Unit tests for parsing, remainder filling, validation errors, and edge cases

## Publish to changelog?

No

pull_requests.title: feat(devex): use orval JS API for in-process type generation
pull_requests.body:
**Problem**

`hogli build:openapi` spawns a child process per product module for Orval code generation — 44 for frontend types, 12 for MCP schemas. Each `pnpm exec orval` incurs ~0.6s of Node/pnpm startup overhead, making the Orval phase ~11s for frontend and ~5s for MCP despite the work itself being ~30ms per module.

**Changes**

Call Orval's `generate()` JS API directly instead of spawning subprocesses. Config objects are passed inline instead of written to temp config files. Bounded concurrency worker pool (default 10) in `@posthog/openapi-codegen` shared by both scripts.

**Results**

Frontend type generation: ~11s → ~3.3s
MCP schema generation: ~4.8s → ~3.7s

MCP generated schemas now correctly emit Zod 4 syntax (`zod.iso.datetime`) because Orval resolves the Zod version from the output target's nearest `package.json`. The subprocess approach resolved from repo root where Zod 3 lives.

pull_requests.title: feat: Continous commit metadata.json
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The kafka-deduplicator's checkpoint system uses a two-tier approach for disaster recovery: local RocksDB + metadata.json, and S3 uploads. 
However, metadata.json was only written during S3 checkpoint exports, which are infrequent (every few minutes). This meant that after a pod restart, even when local RocksDB data was perfectly intact, the service had no way to know its last committed offset and had to fall back to an expensive S3 import or start from scratch.

Additionally, the store directory layout used `<base>/<topic>_<partition>/<timestamp>/` which created new timestamped subdirectories on every import, making local restore impossible and accumulating orphan directories.

## Changes

- Simplify the store directory layout from `<base>/<topic>_<partition>/<timestamp>/` to `<base>/<topic>/<partition>/`, enabling in-place local restore across restarts
- Write metadata.json continuously on every Kafka offset commit via a fire-and-forget `OnCommit` callback trait, keeping the `updated_at` timestamp fresh for staleness detection
- Add local restore as the first restore strategy during rebalance: if metadata.json exists and is within the configurable staleness window (`local_checkpoint_max_staleness`, default 2h, probably needs tuning), open RocksDB directly and skip S3 import entirely
- Seed `OffsetTracker` from restored metadata so the Kafka consumer seeks to the correct position and checkpointing reflects the pre-restart state
- Decouple `BatchConsum

pull_requests.title: feat: Personhog-leader PoC
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

PersonHog currently only supports reads through personhog-replica (Postgres). To support low-latency writes and strong consistency reads for person data, we need a leader component that serves as a per-partition in-memory cache backed by Kafka, coordinated through etcd. This PR adds an initial naive cache only implementation for the leader component

## Changes

- Add `personhog-leader` crate with per-partition person cache 
- Implement gRPC service implementing `GetPerson` and `UpdatePersonProperties`, and person property update logic (`$set`, `$set_once`, `$unset`)
- Add `PersonHogLeader` proto service definition with `LeaderGetPersonRequest` and `UpdatePersonPropertiesRequest` RPCs
- Add `LeaderBackend` to personhog-router that routes strong consistency reads and writes to leader pods using the etcd routing table for partition-to-pod resolution, with gRPC client caching and retries
- Update routing logic to send person data writes and strong reads to the leader instead of returning errors
- Add `LeaderHandoffHandler` that integrates personhog-leader's cache with personhog-coordination's handoff lifecycle (warm/release partitions)
- Add integration tests for personhog-router (8 tests covering routing behavior, error cases, and non-person data) and personhog-leader (4 tests covering etcd coordination lifecycle, partition ownership, rebalancing, and pod crash recovery)

## How did you test this code?

<!-- Briefly describe the steps you took. -->

- **Fail on zero matches** — `--sync-all` now exits with code 1 and prints actionable warnings when a product YAML has no matching OpenAPI operations, so missing tags get caught early

## Summary

Fixes snapshot drift on master for two failing Storybook visual regression tests:

- **`Scenes-App/Insights/Trends › TrendsBar › play-test`** (shard 6/16) — 1.12% pixel mismatch (1,291 differing pixels) after a recent UI change merged without updating the baseline.
- **`Components/EditorFilters › WithLongFilterValue › smoke-test`** (shard 2/16) — 32px height mismatch (snapshot was 1079×1591, rendered as 1079×1623) after a layout change in the filter editor.

## Approach

Deleted the four outdated snapshot PNG files (`TrendsBar` light/dark, `EditorFilters WithLongFilterValue` light/dark). The Storybook CI will run in **UPDATE mode** (since `mendral-app[bot]` is a trusted bot), detect the missing snapshots, regenerate them from the current UI, and commit the new baselines back to this PR.

Closes the snapshot drift reported in the incident: _Storybook snapshot drift: TrendsBar and EditorFilters fail on master (shards 2/16 and 6/16)_.

pull_requests.title: fix(mcp): Gate AI-powered tools behind organization consent
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Organizations need control over whether AI data processing features are available to their users. Some tools (like HogQL generation) invoke LLMs internally and should only be accessible when the organization has explicitly approved AI data processing.

## Changes

- Added `requires_ai_consent` field to tool definitions to mark tools that require AI data processing approval
- Updated `getToolsFromContext()` to fetch the organization's `is_ai_data_processing_approved` setting and pass it to tool filtering
- Modified `getToolsForFeatures()` to exclude tools marked with `requires_ai_consent` when `aiConsentGiven` is `false`
- Marked `query-generate-hogql-from-question` tool as requiring AI consent
- Updated tool configuration schema and documentation to support the new field
- Updated tool generation script to include the new field in generated definitions

The filtering logic treats undefined/missing consent as allowing tools (defaulting to enabled), while explicitly denying consent (`false`) excludes AI-powered tools. This ensures backward compatibility while providing organizations with control over AI features.

## How did you test this code?

Added comprehensive unit tests covering:
- Excluding AI consent tools when `aiConsentGiven` is `false`
- Including AI consent tools when `aiConsentGiven` is `true` or `undefined`
- Preserving non-AI tools regardless of consent status
- Combining AI consent filtering with feature-based filtering
- Integration tests verifying

pull_requests.title: fix: allow missing API-Version header on agentic oauth/token endpoint
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Stripe does not send the `API-Version` header on the OAuth token exchange call (`POST /api/agentic/oauth/token`) since it's a standard OAuth endpoint, not a provisioning protocol endpoint. Confirmed by Stripe (Alexander) that this is intentional.

PostHog's `verify_stripe_signature` rejected the request with `{"error": {"code": "invalid_api_version"}}` because it treated a missing header the same as an invalid one. This was the root cause of the "Something went wrong" error on Stripe's callback page after the user successfully authorized.

Confirmed in production logs - multiple `invalid_api_version` errors on `/api/agentic/oauth/token` with `api_version: ""`.

## Changes

Added `require_api_version` parameter to `verify_stripe_signature` (default `True`). The `oauth_token` view passes `require_api_version=False` via the region proxy decorator. All other endpoints keep the version check.

## How did you test this code

Reproduced against production:
- Without `API-Version` header (what Stripe sends): `400 invalid_api_version` (the bug)
- With `API-Version: 0.1d` (what our test script sends): `400 invalid_grant` (expected, fake code)

Full e2e test script passes with all 17 tests (account request, authorize, token exchange, provisioning, deep links, credential rotation, refresh tokens, error cases).

🤖 Generated with [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code)

pull_requests.title: feat(toolbar): add more events
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Stacked on #51066.

The toolbar has ~20 existing capture events but significant gaps in observability: no API response time tracking, no auth flow timing, no menu usage data, and no way to tell how users launched the toolbar or whether they were already authenticated.

## Changes

- **API request tracking**: instrument `toolbarFetch` with `toolbar api request` event — captures `method`, `pathname`, `status`, `duration_ms`, and `did_token_retry` for all 13+ toolbar API endpoints
- **Menu usage**: add `toolbar menu opened` / `toolbar menu closed` events in `setVisibleMenu` listener, tracking which menu and what was previously open
- **Auth flow timing**: add `toolbar oauth exchange` and `toolbar token refresh` events with `status` and `duration_ms`
- **Toolbar load**: measure time from `ph_load_toolbar` to initialization complete (`load_duration_ms`)
- **Launch context**: add `source` (`url` / `localstorage`) to the `toolbar loaded` event — distinguishes PostHog-launched from auto-loaded toolbars. `is_authenticated` already tells us if the user had valid tokens at load time
- **Telemetry docs**: add `TELEMETRY.md` at the toolbar root documenting all 31 events with their properties and source files

## How did you test this code?

- Manually verified new events appear in PostHog with correct properties
- Tested both `url` and `localstorage` source values
- Confirmed `duration_ms` values are reasonable for API requests and auth flows

## Publish to changelog?

No.

## Problem

Stacked on #51065.

When toolbar errors or warnings occur, we capture exceptions but have no structured logs to correlate with. Console logs are lost when the page navigates away, and there's no way to search or filter them.

## Changes

- Add `toolbarLogger` — a structured logger that sends OTLP-formatted log records to PostHog's `/i/v1/logs` endpoint while also logging to the browser console
- Include `posthog.distinct_id` as a resource attribute so logs are attributed to the user

pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Feature flags need to support realtime/behavioral cohort membership lookups in addition to the existing static cohort membership checks. Static cohorts use pre-computed membership stored in `posthog_cohortpeople`, while realtime cohorts require dynamic queries to the behavioral cohorts database based on recent user activity.

## Changes

- **Added realtime cohort membership support**: Introduced `CohortMembershipProvider` trait and implementations for querying behavioral cohorts database
- **Extended flag evaluation state**: Added `person_uuid` field and merged cohort membership caching to handle both static and realtime results
- **Enhanced cohort evaluation logic**: Modified `prepare_flag_evaluation_state` to fetch realtime cohort memberships after static ones, with graceful error handling
- **Added new metrics**: Introduced `FLAG_REALTIME_COHORT_QUERY_TIME` and `FLAG_REALTIME_COHORT_QUERY_ERROR_COUNTER` for monitoring
- **Updated canonical logging**: Added fields for tracking realtime cohort queries and evaluation counts
- **Integrated provider into matcher**: Added `cohort_membership_provider` parameter to `FeatureFlagMatcher` constructor and evaluation context

The implementation maintains backward compatibility by falling back to `NoOpCohortMembershipProvider` when behavioral cohorts database is unavailable, treating all realtime cohorts as non-members.

pull_requests.title: feat(data-warehouse): add MCP tools for materialized views
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Add 9 MCP tools for managing data warehouse saved queries (views): list, get, create, update, delete, materialize, revert materialization, run, and run history
- Add `help_text` to serializer fields so descriptions flow through to MCP tool parameter docs
- Fix 3 bugs found during local end-to-end testing (see below)

## Bug fixes

1. **Column inference broken for API clients**: `DataWarehouseSavedQuery.get_columns()` passed `{"query": "SELECT ..."}` to `process_query_dict` without the `kind` discriminator required by `QuerySchemaRoot`. This caused column inference to always fail for any API client that doesn't send explicit `types`. The frontend masked this by always sending types from the SQL editor. Fix: wrap the query as `{"kind": "HogQLQuery", "query": "..."}` when `kind` is missing.

2. **`run` action missing `required_scopes`**: Personal API keys got 403 Forbidden on the run endpoint because it had no `required_scopes` declared. Fix: add `required_scopes=["warehouse_view:write"]`.

3. **Update always failed via MCP**: `edited_history_id` was excluded from MCP update params, but the backend requires it when changing the `query` field. Without it, updates always rejected with "The query was modified by someone else." Fix: expose `edited_history_id` in the MCP partial-update tool with clear instructions.

## MCP tools enabled

| Tool | Operation | Scope |
|------|-----------|-------|
| `warehouse-saved-queries-list` | List all views | `warehouse_view:read` |
