pull_requests.title: chore: replace axum::async_trait re-export with direct async_trait import
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Axum 0.8 removes the `async_trait` re-export. Several Rust crates in the workspace (`feature-flags`, `cymbal`, `batch-import-worker`, `kafka-deduplicator`) import `async_trait` via `axum::async_trait`, which will break on upgrade.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This PR doesn't upgrade us to Axum 0.8. It's just preparing us for such an upgrade in the future.

## Changes

- Replace all `use axum::async_trait` imports with `use async_trait::async_trait` across 31 `.rs` files
- Add `async-trait = { workspace = true }` to `cymbal` and `feature-flags` `Cargo.toml` (the other two crates already had it)
- Remove one redundant function-local import in `feature-flags/src/utils/test_utils.rs`

This is a no-op — axum 0.7 simply re-exports the same crate.

## How did you test this code?

`cargo check -p feature-flags` passes. The change is a mechanical import path substitution with no behavioral difference.

No publish to changelog.

pull_requests.title: chore(devex): surface review thread resolution in PR approval agent
pull_requests.body:
**Problem**
The PR approval agent lists inline review comments without indicating whether they've been resolved or are outdated. This causes the LLM reviewer to flag old comments that the author already addressed — either by resolving the thread or by pushing a fix commit.

**Changes**
- Switch from REST (`/pulls/{n}/comments`) to GraphQL `reviewThreads` for fetching inline comments — gives us `isResolved` and `isOutdated` per thread
- Add `__typename` check to correctly identify bot users (copilot, greptile) that have `authorAssociation: CONTRIBUTOR` instead of `BOT`
- Annotate each comment in the LLM prompt with `[resolved]` or `[outdated]` tags
- Update the system prompt to treat resolution status as a signal, not gospel — the LLM still skims resolved comments for serious unaddressed concerns, and checks whether follow-up commits addressed unresolved ones before flagging

**Test plan**
- Dry-run against PRs with resolved/outdated threads, verified `is_resolved`/`is_outdated` fields populate correctly and tags appear in prompt

pull_requests.title: fix(sources): ArrowInvalid for decimals
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

[Ticket](https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/52614): 

Looks like PyArrow's Postgres connector is reading numeric(10,2) from Postgres but mapping it to Decimal128(38, 2) in some batches (38 is PyArrow's default max precision for decimal types). The Delta table was originally created with Decimal128(10, 2), and when the pipeline tries to append data with Decimal128(38, 2), it's rejected, schema overwrite isn't supported for appends.

## Changes

There was a lot of refactoring here, but the main changes are described below. The rest of the method's functionality was not changed.

- PyArrow's Postgres connector maps `numeric(10,2)` to `decimal128(38,2)` in some batches. When the Delta table was created with `decimal128(10,2)`, appending `decimal128(38,2)` data fails because delta-rs rejects precision widening on appends.
- Fixed `_evolve_pyarrow_schema` in `posthog/temporal/data_imports/pipelines/pipeline/utils.py` with two changes:
  1. **Downcast guard** (L221-234): when incoming and delta decimals share the same scale but the incoming precision is inflated by the connector, try casting back to the Delta type first. If values fit, no schema change needed.
  2. **Minimal merged precision** (L244-245): when reconciling mismatched decimals, compute `merged_precision = max_int_digits + merged_scale` instead of always defaulting to `decimal128(38, scale)`.
- Refactored the function for clarity: renamed variables to `incoming_*` / `delta_*`, colla

pull_requests.title: fix(ci): remove redundant OpenAPI type generation in check-migrations
pull_requests.body:
**Problem**
The OpenAPI step ran `hogli build:openapi` twice — once on the merge commit to detect drift, then again after checking out the actual branch to commit. Added in f1ec28bd01 when auto-commit was introduced, out of concern that the merge commit and branch HEAD could produce different output.

**Fix**
Checkout the actual branch ref at the earlier PR checkout step (used for migrations) instead of the default merge commit. The single OpenAPI generation then runs against branch code directly and can be committed without a second checkout + regeneration.

Saves ~30s of redundant codegen on the unhappy path (when types are out of date). No behavior change on the happy path.

pull_requests.title: fix(early-access): persist "Registered" state for concept-stage features
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

PR #49888 correctly changed concept-stage early access features to not enable their feature flag (concept is for gauging interest, not enabling functionality). However, the frontend derived the "Registered" button state from `featureFlags[flagKey]`, which is now intentionally `false` for concept features.

This causes the "Get notified" → "Registered" button to flash briefly on click, then revert back to "Get notified" when posthog-js re-fetches flags from the server and gets `false` back.

### Before

![2026-03-14 13 15 00](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/b9001a14-b9ad-4fa9-979c-476946242d69)

### After

![2026-03-15 18 27 22](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/93b85b9c-c7be-42ac-8712-efb60136969f)

## Changes

- Added a `conceptEnrollments` kea reducer to track concept-stage enrollments locally for immediate reactivity within the session
- Updated the `earlyAccessFeatures` selector to also read `$stored_person_properties` from posthog-js localStorage, which persists the `$feature_enrollment/{flagKey}` person property across page reloads
- No posthog-js changes required — the SDK already persists enrollment as person properties in both localStorage and on the server

## How did you test this code?

- Added 3 unit tests for the `conceptEnrollments` reducer (concept tracking, non-concept exclusion, multiple enrollments)
- All 9 existing `featurePreviewsLogic` tests pass
- Created local concept-stage early access features f

pull_requests.title: feat(early-access): add option to ignore opt-outs when promoting to GA
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When an early access feature is promoted to General Availability, users who previously opted out still receive `false` for the feature flag. This is because the `super_groups` condition checks the enrollment property first, so opted-out users remain opted out even after GA promotion. The workaround (manually editing the flag to 100% rollout) exists but isn't discoverable.

Closes #50980

## Changes

- Added a "Roll out to all users — include users who previously opted out" checkbox to the GA promotion confirmation dialog
- When checked: clears the feature flag's `super_groups` (removing opt-in/opt-out conditions) and sets rollout to 100%
- When unchecked (default): preserves existing behavior where opted-out users remain opted out
- Backend handles the new `rollout_to_all` parameter in `EarlyAccessFeatureSerializer.update()`
- All three frontend GA promotion entry points (save button, stage dropdown, info panel select) pass the value through

## How did you test this code?

Added two backend tests:
- `test_promote_to_ga_with_rollout_to_all_clears_super_groups_and_sets_100_percent` — verifies super_groups are cleared and rollout is 100%
- `test_promote_to_ga_without_rollout_to_all_preserves_super_groups` — verifies default behavior is unchanged

All 35 early access feature tests pass. I'm an agent and haven't tested this manually.

## Publish to changelog?

Yes

## Docs update

The early access features documentation should be updated to d

When working on bigger dashboards, it becomes different to move existing items on the dashboard because because scrolling can be slow or difficult when there are many items and you're trying to move them from top to bottom. This was noted in replays, in customer feedback, in GitHub issues. 

## Changes

Adds a new zoom button when editing layouts. The zoom button will zoom out the items into the grid so that they're smaller, which makes it easier to move around while still maintaining their grid positions.

pull_requests.title: fix(llma): upgrade labeling agent to gpt-5.4
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- The previous PR (#50776) changed the labeling agent model from `gpt-5.2` to `gpt-5.3`, but `gpt-5.3` doesn't exist as an OpenAI model (they went from 5.2 → 5.4)
- This caused all labeling agent calls to silently fail, resulting in every cluster getting fallback "Label not generated by agent" labels since March 12
- Upgrades to `gpt-5.4` (OpenAI's latest frontier model) — modest cost increase ($1.75 → $2.50/1M input tokens, $14 → $15/1M output tokens) but 2.5x context window (400K → 1.05M)

## Changes

- Update `LABELING_AGENT_MODEL` from `gpt-5.3` to `gpt-5.4` in clustering constants
- Add `gpt-5.4` to `OpenAIConfig.SUPPORTED_MODELS` and `SUPPORTED_MODELS_WITH_THINKING`
- Add `llma_labeling` product to LLM gateway config with `gpt-5.4` allowlisted
- Update READMEs

## Test plan

- [ ] Verify cluster labeling works by triggering a manual clustering workflow for team 2
- [ ] Check Temporal worker logs for `cluster_labeling_agent_completed` (not `cluster_labeling_agent_error`)
- [ ] Confirm clusters in the UI show generated labels instead of "Label not generated by agent"

pull_requests.title: fix(flags): honor edit URL param on initial mount
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

This is a papercut that long has bothered me and I finally got off my rear to fix it.

Clicking "Edit" from the feature flags list page hamburger menu navigates to `/feature_flags/:id?edit=true`, but the detail page renders in view mode instead of edit mode. The `?edit=true` URL parameter is ignored on initial page load.

## Changes

kea-router fires `urlToAction` with `method='POP'` and `initial=true` on first mount, so the existing `method === 'PUSH'` guard in `featureFlagLogic` skipped the `editFeatureFlag` call entirely.

The `editFeatureFlag` call now fires on both `initial` mount and `PUSH` navigation, while keeping the data-loading logic PUSH-only to avoid duplicating `afterMount`'s cache-optimized loading. This matches the pattern used in `insightSceneLogic`.

## How did you test this code?

- Verified TypeScript compiles with no new errors
- Manually tested it.

## Change Log

no

pull_requests.title: chore(llma): auto-install sentiment deps when llm_analytics intent is enabled
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Developers working on LLM Analytics locally need to manually install the sentiment Python dependencies (`torch`, `optimum`, `transformers` ~2GB) and download the ONNX model separately. The flox activation hook runs `uv sync` (without `--group sentiment`) which strips these packages on every restart, requiring repeated manual reinstallation.

## Changes

Adds `uv_groups` support to the hogli intent resolution system, parallel to the existing `docker_profiles` pattern:

- **`devenv/intent-map.yaml`**: New `llm_analytics_sentiment` capability with `uv_groups: [sentiment]`, added to the `llm_analytics` intent
- **`common/hogli/devenv/resolver.py`**: `uv_groups` field on `Capability` and `ResolvedEnvironment`, collected during resolution like `docker_profiles`
- **`common/hogli/devenv/generator.py`**: `_add_uv_groups` method prepends `uv sync --group sentiment --inexact && bin/download-sentiment-model` to the temporal-worker shell command when the capability is active
- **`bin/download-sentiment-model`**: Fixed to use `python3` instead of `python` (which doesn't exist in flox)

When `llm_analytics` is enabled via `hogli dev:setup`, the temporal-worker startup automatically installs the sentiment packages and downloads the ONNX model. No impact on developers who don't use `llm_analytics`. Packages are cached by uv so re-installs after the first are fast (~1-2s).

## How did you test this code?

- All 37 existing devenv tests pass (`pytest common/hogli/tests/test_devenv.
pull_requests.author: andrewm4894

pull_requests.title: fix(notebook):  Notebook unable to show different funnel views
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Changing funnel visualization type (e.g. to "Historical trends") in a notebook's settings panel doesn't update the chart, showing the bar chart instead of the expected line graph. 
The `propsQuery` selector in `insightDataLogic` uses kea's `(_, props) => props` pattern, which doesn't re-evaluate on prop-only changes because reselect memoization only tracks Redux state changes.

## Changes
 
1. Add `syncQueryFromProps` action that updates `internalQuery` reducer without triggering `insightVizDataLogic.setQuery` listener
2. Extend `propsChanged` handler to detect query changes from props and dispatch `syncQueryFromProps`, ensuring downstream selectors (`querySource` → `funnelsFilter` → `funnelVizType`) re-evaluate via Redux state
3. Add tests verifying `syncQueryFromProps` updates state without side effects and that `propsChanged` triggers it on query changes

pull_requests.title: chore(mcp): tree-shakeable generated zod schemas
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Generated zod schemas in the MCP service are not tree-shakeable — esbuild bundles all schemas even when only a subset is imported, increasing bundle size.

## Changes

- Generate only the zod schemas needed for enabled tool inputs (not full response schemas)
- Remove unused generated schemas
- Add `/* @__PURE__ */` annotations to top-level exported zod expressions so esbuild can tree-shake unused ones

## How did you test this code?

Verified generated output contains `@__PURE__` annotations on all exported zod constants. Confirmed the annotation regex runs after Orval generation.

pull_requests.title: chore(mcp): skip zod schemas for disabled mcp tools
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

MCP Orval schema generation currently includes:
1. operations from tools marked `enabled: false` in YAML definitions
2. response-side OpenAPI schemas

That generates extra Zod schemas we do not use for MCP request validation, increasing generated code size and memory footprint.

## Changes

- Updated MCP Orval input filtering to only include enabled tools:
  - `services/mcp/scripts/generate-orval-schemas.mjs`
  - `parseToolDefinition` now ignores tools without `enabled: true`
  - `exclude_params` are only applied for enabled tools
  - logging now reports enabled operation counts
- Extended shared OpenAPI filtering utility:
  - `tools/openapi-codegen/src/schema.mjs`
  - `filterSchemaByOperationIds(fullSchema, operationIds, options = {})`
  - new option: `includeResponseSchemas` (default `true`)
  - when `false`, selected operations are transformed so each response keeps only `{ description }` (removing `content`, `headers`, and `$ref`) before `$ref` collection
- Wired MCP Orval generation to use request-side schemas only:
  - `filterSchemaByOperationIds(..., { includeResponseSchemas: false })`
- Added tests for the new behavior:
  - `tools/openapi-codegen/tests/schema.test.mjs`
  - verifies default behavior includes response refs
  - verifies `includeResponseSchemas: false` drops response refs while preserving request refs
  - verifies response status keys remain with description-only objects

## How did you test this code?

Executed:

pull_requests.title: feat(flags): record PAK last_used_at in Rust flags definitions service
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The Rust flags definitions service (`/api/feature_flags/definitions`) does not record `last_used_at` for personal API keys (PAKs), meaning that PAK usage through this endpoint goes untracked. This matters for surfacing stale or unused keys to users.

## Changes

This employs the same pattern that the `/local_evaluation` Django endpoint does. It debounces writes to `last_used_at` for one hour. One hour granularity is plenty.

- `auth.rs`: `validate_personal_api_key_with_scopes_for_team` now returns the PAK `id` (String) on success instead of `()`, so callers can use it for usage tracking
- `flag_definitions.rs`: after successful PAK auth, spawns a background task to record usage without blocking the request path
- `pak_usage.rs` (new): implements `record_pak_last_used` with Redis SET NX EX debouncing (1-hour window matching Django) to avoid hammering the DB on high-frequency callers

## How did you test this code?

Unit tests in `pak_usage.rs` cover:
- Skipping the DB write when the Redis debounce key already exists
- Proceeding with the DB write when the debounce key is new (mocked; real DB write verified by the mock call count)
- Graceful handling of Redis errors (warns and skips the DB write)

Manually verified that `last_used_at` is written to the DB on first request and debounced on subsequent requests within the 1-hour window.

## Publish to changelog?

Do not publish to changelog.

## Docs update

No docs update needed.

pull_requests.title: chore(mcp): support multi-server Grafana MCP setup
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Switching between US and EU Grafana regions requires restarting the MCP client each time, since the region is read at startup from `~/.grafana-region`. Engineers who regularly query both regions lose time on restarts.

## Changes

- **Wrapper script**: Add `GRAFANA_REGION` env var support (takes precedence over `~/.grafana-region` file), enabling two MCP server instances to run simultaneously with different regions
- **Migration script** (`grafana-migrate-multi`): Automate the conversion from single-server to multi-server setup. Scans both global (`~/.config/claude-code/.mcp.json`) and project (`.claude.json`) MCP configs, clones the `grafana` entry as `grafana-eu`, pins regions via env var, and duplicates permissions in `~/.claude/settings.json`. Supports `--dry-run` for preview and `--slim` to normalize disable-flags to the recommended smaller set
- **AGENTS.md**: Add conditional Grafana section instructing agents to default to US and use EU only when available
- **README**: Restructure docs with single-server vs multi-server setup sections, add migration instructions

## How did you test this code?

Tested the multi-server setup by running both `grafana` (US) and `grafana-eu` (EU) MCP servers simultaneously and querying Loki logs across both regions. Verified the migration script's `--dry-run` output and idempotency.

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

N/A — internal tooling only.

pull_requests.title: feat(ws): make HubSpot source resumable
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

HubSpot endpoints don't support incremental queries (they have some filter, but it's limited to a certain number of records, which could cause data loss). When our customer's'' accounts become too big, they face more issues while syncing data.

## Problem

The MCP worker's startup time was ~400ms due to eagerly evaluating the `@/tools` module tree (all Zod schemas, tool definitions) at the top level.

| Version | Avg startup |
|---|---|
| Static import (before) | ~404 ms |
| Dynamic import (after) | ~148 ms |

~63% reduction.

pull_requests.title: fix(batch-exports): handle DST transitions in daily/weekly interval calculations
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Batch exports with daily/weekly intervals configured with a timezone that uses daylight savings time (DST) (e.g. `US/Eastern`) produces incorrect data intervals during DST transitions.

Hourly and sub-hourly intervals are unaffected since they use fixed UTC durations.

## Changes

- **`get_data_interval`** (`products/batch_exports/backend/temporal/batch_exports.py`): Added `timezone` parameter. For day/week intervals, converts to local time before subtracting so `timedelta(days=1)` preserves wall clock time across DST transitions.
- **`backfill_range`** (`products/batch_exports/backend/temporal/backfill_batch_export.py`): Added `timezone` parameter. For steps >= 1 day, steps in local time and yields UTC results. Extracts timezone from the existing `schedule_handle.describe()` call — no new API calls.
- **`BaseBatchExportInputs`** (`posthog/batch_exports/service.py`): Added `timezone: str = "UTC"` field (backwards-compatible default). Populated from `batch_export.timezone_info` in `sync_batch_export`.
- All 9 destination workflows + 1 test updated to pass `inputs.timezone` to `get_data_interval`.
- Simplified existing `posthog/api/test/batch_exports/test_create.py` test

## How did you test this code?

- Added 4 new tests covering spring-forward and fall-back for both `get_data_interval` and `backfill_range`.
  - These tests reproduced the issue before the fix was implemented.

## Post-deployment

Will need to sync Temporal schedules in order t

pull_requests.title: fix(devex): replace celery autoreload fork with in-process watchman thread
pull_requests.body:
**Problem**
Django's `autoreload.run_with_reloader()` forks a full Django process as the parent file watcher — ~146 MB per celery process. With worker + beat that's significant memory sitting idle just to detect .py changes.

**Fix**
Run a pywatchman subscription as a daemon thread inside the celery process itself. On file change, `os.execv()` re-execs in-place (same PID, fresh interpreter). No fork, no subprocess — single process per celery type.

- Watches `posthog/`, `ee/`, `products/` for .py changes via the already-running watchman daemon (provided by flox)
- Gracefully degrades when watchman is unavailable (CI) — logs a warning and runs without reload
- Prod is unaffected — uses `bin/docker-worker-celery`, not this command

pull_requests.title: fix: typeless exceptions
pull_requests.body:
In some cases it is possible that customer will ingest an exception with manually set fingerprint but improper structure. Such events fail our ET ingestion pipeline but are still ingested with `$cymbal_errors` attached.

Usually you can't see such exceptions in ET tab. You can only see them in activity tab with explanation why they failed to ingest into ET. 

But there is a special case where customer sets fingerprint manually so it gets "assigned" to an existing ET issue. When you then enter that issue, entire page crashes because it tries to attach usual metadata to improperly formatted exception event.

This PR makes it so that we will only include exceptions which have issue id set (they "passed" ET ingestion pipeline)

pull_requests.title: feat(analytics-platform): Add limited support for cohorts in real-time filters
pull_requests.body:
## Problem



See 
* https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C09B0SSQEDA/p1769702335556119?thread_ts=1769591314.825779&cid=C09B0SSQEDA
* https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C076E99B152/p1773337517732949?thread_ts=1773334304.885789&cid=C076E99B152

Some queries are over 2x slower than they need to be due to internal/test filters. This is because, for every event, they must read the host and email properties (or whichever properties are configured). This can account for most of the data read from disk for the query!

The solution to this is to use a small cohort, containing the internal/test users, and set the filters to NOT IN that cohort.

The problem with this solution is that it would break real-time CDP functions that use internal/test filters, as these cannot access the DB, so cannot use cohorts.

## Changes

This PR adds limited support for cohorts in real-time CDP functions. Specifically, if the cohort is a `dynamic` cohort that only uses `person` properties, we convert the cohort filter to equivalent person property filters.

## How did you test this code?

Added many tests, including a test for the intended common use case where the customer has 2 cohorts: internal users and test users

## Publish to changelog?

<!-- For features only -->

pull_requests.title: fix(devex): preserve 4-space indent when auto-updating hogli manifest
pull_requests.body:
`auto_update_manifest()` uses PyYAML's `yaml.dump()` which defaults to 2-space indentation. The committed manifest uses 4-space (matching `.oxfmtrc.json` tabWidth), so every time hogli found missing entries it rewrote the entire file with 2-space indent — causing a full-file dirty diff that blocked branch checkouts and merges.

Adds `indent=4` to the `yaml.dump()` call.

pull_requests.title: fix(ds): reduce memory usage in postgres data warehouse source to prevent OOM
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

OOM errors when syncing wide and big Postgres tables with big text columns (like 100k+ character strings) by reducing peak memory usage during data import

## Changes

  * Changed `pg_column_size(t)` to `octet_length(t::text)` in chunk size estimation to measure decompressed row size instead of TOAST-compressed size
  * Removed unnecessary `list()` copy in `table_from_iterator` — `batch` is already a list
  * Materialized dicts from cursor rows and freed the original tuples (`del rows`) before passing to `table_from_iterator`, reducing peak memory by avoiding two copies coexisting

## How did you test this code?

Test pass
Local run ⬇️ 

### Local run results:

We went from a memory usage of:

15:09:52.571 RSS: 5542.9 MB VSZ: 429381.9 MB

pull_requests.title: feat(llma): add quota limiting and event restrictions to OTel endpoint
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The OTel ingestion endpoint bypasses quota limiting and event restrictions that the main capture handler enforces. This means teams over their LLM event quota or with active event restrictions can still ingest data through the OTel path.

## Changes

- Add quota limiting with all-or-nothing batch semantics — if any span would be dropped by the quota limiter, the entire request is rejected with a non-retryable 400 (per OTLP spec, only 429/502/503/504 are retryable)
- Add event restriction checks with the same all-or-nothing semantics — non-drop flags (force_overflow, skip_person_processing, etc.) are OR’d across all spans and applied uniformly
- Extract quota/restriction/event-building logic into `filtering.rs` to keep the handler lean
- Add `AppliedRestrictions::merge()` for combining non-drop restriction flags across spans
- Register the scoped LLM quota limiter in test setup (was missing)

## How did you test this code?

Integration tests covering:
- LLM quota exceeded → 400, no events produced
- Global quota exceeded with scoped LLM limiter retaining events → 200
- Both quotas exceeded → 400
- Partial quota drop (one span type filtered) → entire batch rejected
- Each restriction type (drop, force_overflow, skip_person_processing, redirect_to_dlq, redirect_to_topic)
- Filtered drop restriction on a multi-span batch → entire batch rejected

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

No docs changes needed.

pull_requests.title: fix(replay): remove body from 204 delete response to fix 502 protocol error
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Recording deletion consistently returns 502 on production with `upstream connect error or disconnect/reset before headers. retried and the latest reset reason: protocol error`
- Browser timing shows ~524ms server wait time (not a timeout — the server actively rejects)
- Root cause: `Response({"success": True}, status=204)` sends a JSON body on an HTTP 204 response. HTTP 204 means "No Content" and MUST NOT include a body. Under Nginx Unit ASGI + HTTP/2, envoy enforces this strictly and resets the connection as a protocol error
- Fix: `Response(status=204)` — no body

## Test plan

- [ ] Deploy and verify single recording deletion returns 204 without 502
- [ ] Verify bulk delete (uses 200 with body — unaffected) still works
- [ ] Verify deleted recording disappears from listing after refresh

pull_requests.title: fix(cdp): handle binary responses correctly in webhook HTTP responses
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

  - Fix binary response corruption in CDP source webhook HTTP responses. The pixel template (and any custom source returning binary data) was sending base64-decoded binary through a string response path, causing `charset=utf-8` to be appended to the content-type and corrupting the payload.
  - Add `isBase64Encoded` and `contentType` fields to `HogFunctionWebhookResult`, allowing Hog sources to return base64-encoded binary that gets decoded server-side in Node.js where binary handling is correct.
  - Update the pixel template to use the new `isBase64Encoded: true` flag instead of calling `base64Decode()` in Hog.

  ## How it works

  When `isBase64Encoded` is `true`, the CDP API decodes the base64 string into a `Buffer` and sends it with `res.send(buffer)`, which avoids Express adding `charset=utf-8` to the content-type. Non-binary string responses continue to work as before.

  ## Closes

https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/47729

pull_requests.title: feat(error-tracking): evaluate suppression rules server-side
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Error tracking suppression rules currently only work client-side. We need server-side evaluation of suppression rules in the Cymbal ingestion pipeline so that matching events are dropped before issue linking, reducing noise and unnecessary processing.

## Changes

**Django model + migrations**
- Added `bytecode`, `disabled_data`, and `sampling_rate` fields to `ErrorTrackingSuppressionRule`
- API compiles filter rules to HogVM bytecode on create/update
- Sampling rate validation (0.0-1.0)
- Generic error responses for invalid filters

**Rust Cymbal pipeline**
- New `SuppressionRule` struct with `load_for_team`, `try_match` (HogVM bytecode evaluation), and `disable` (auto-disables broken rules with diagnostic data)
- `RuleSuppression` operator in the linking stage, evaluated before issue linking
- `SuppressedByRule` error variant propagated through consumer and HTTP pipelines
- Suppression rule caching in `TeamManager` with configurable TTL and size limits
- Sampling rate support in rule evaluation
- Metrics: events suppressed, rules tried/found/disabled, processing time
- Disabled rule cache TTLs in local dev for faster iteration

## How did you test this code?

- Unit tests for `SuppressionRule::try_match` covering match, no-match, and invalid bytecode
- Backend API tests for suppression rule CRUD and validation

## Publish to changelog?

no

pull_requests.title: fix(storybook): add waitForSelector to experiment stories
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Add `data-attr="metrics-chart-tick-labels"` to the `TableHeader` SVG element and configure all 10 experiment stories to use `testOptions.waitForSelector` to wait for SVG tick labels to render before snapshotting
- Increase `makeDelay` from 500ms to 1000ms as a safety margin for initial data loading

## Problem

The `ExperimentFrequentistTwoVariants` (and other experiment) visual regression snapshots are flaky because secondary metrics column titles (SVG tick labels) sometimes aren't rendered when the snapshot is taken. This is caused by a race condition between:
1. The test runner's snapshot timing
2. The async `ResizeObserver`/`useEffect` in `TableHeader` that determines SVG dimensions and tick label rendering

The previous `makeDelay(500)` was a time-based wait that didn't guarantee the SVG tick labels had rendered. The test runner's `.Spinner` wait and content stability check don't cover async updates inside SVGs.

## Solution

1. **`TableHeader.tsx`**: Added `data-attr="metrics-chart-tick-labels"` to the SVG element containing tick labels — provides a reliable DOM selector
2. **All 10 experiment stories**: Added `testOptions.waitForSelector: '[data-attr="metrics-chart-tick-labels"]'` — the test runner now waits for the SVG to be present in the DOM before proceeding to snapshot
3. **Increased delay**: `makeDelay(500)` → `makeDelay(1000)` as belt-and-suspenders for initial data loading

## Files Changed

- `frontend/src/scenes/experiments/MetricsView/new/Table

pull_requests.title: fix(test): add default property values mocks
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Add empty-response mocks for all property values API endpoints to the default MSW handlers in `handlers.ts`, preventing "Failed to load property values" error toasts from appearing in Storybook visual regression snapshots.

## Problem

The default MSW mock handlers don't include mocks for property values endpoints (`/api/event/values/`, `/api/person/values/`, etc.). When Storybook stories render components that load property values, the API calls fail and `propertyDefinitionsModel` shows a `lemonToast.error('Failed to load property values')` toast, which appears in visual regression snapshots as false diffs.

## Changes

Added 7 property values endpoint mocks to `defaultMocks.get` in `frontend/src/mocks/handlers.ts`:

| Endpoint | Purpose |
|----------|---------|
| `/api/event/values/` | Event property values |
| `/api/person/values/` | Person property values |
| `/api/group/values/` | Group property values |
| `/api/environments/:team_id/sessions/values/` | Session property values |
| `/api/projects/:team_id/flag_value/values/` | Feature flag values |
| `/api/projects/:team_id/groups/property_values/` | Group property values (project-scoped) |
| `/api/environments/:team_id/data_warehouse/property_values/` | Data warehouse property values |

All return empty arrays (`[]`), matching the expected API response format. Individual stories can still override these with specific mock data using `mswDecorator()` or `useStorybookMocks()`.

pull_requests.title: fix(flags): check Redis before negative cache in ReadThroughCache
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The `ReadThroughCache::get_or_load` method checks the negative cache before Redis. The negative cache exists to protect against repeated calls to the expensive loader function for keys known not to exist — not to protect Redis. This means a valid Redis entry could be blocked by a stale negative cache entry.

The scenario we want to avoid is a signal creates a cache entry, but a request comes in before the cache entry is ready. We don't want to block the next 5 minutes (or whatever) of requests because of the negative cache entry. The negative cache entry is more difficult to invalidate dynamically, so we use a TTL. Eventually, we may want to implement a proper approach to negative cache invalidation. But this should work for now.

## Changes

- Reorder cache lookups to Redis → negative cache → loader so a valid Redis entry is returned immediately
- Move negative cache check from `get_or_load` into `handle_cache_miss`, where it gates the loader across all error paths (miss, corruption, Redis-unavailable)
- Deduplicate `handle_true_cache_miss` and `handle_corrupted_cache` into a single `load_and_cache` method parameterized by `CacheSource` variants
- Include `CacheSource` in cache write failure warning messages for better diagnostics
- Fix `_key` parameter naming in `handle_redis_unavailable` (parameter was actually used)
- Add test verifying Redis hit takes priority over negative cache

## How did you test this code?

All 24 existing + new unit tes
pull_requests.author: haacked

pull_requests.title: chore(flags): remove PBKDF2 personal API key support from Rust service
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The Rust feature-flags service supports three personal API key hashing modes (`SHA256`, `PBKDF2` with 260k iterations, `PBKDF2` with 390k iterations), trying each sequentially on every auth request. This adds unnecessary complexity and a slow-path dependency (`pbkdf2` crate) to a service that hasn't shipped yet.

Since Feb 2024, Django auto-upgrades any `PBKDF2` key to `SHA256` on use. Production data confirms zero `PBKDF2` keys have been used in 2+ years across US (~6,750 keys) and EU (~1,750 keys). Every actively-used key has already been upgraded.

## Changes

- Remove `hash_legacy_pbkdf2_key()` and multi-mode hashing logic from `auth.rs`
- Simplify `hash_personal_api_key()` to SHA256-only (now `pub(crate)` for reuse)
- Flatten `validate_personal_api_key_with_scopes_for_team()` from a 3-iteration loop to a single hash + query
- Remove `pbkdf2` crate dependency from `Cargo.toml`
- Remove `PERSONAL_API_KEY_HASH_MODE_COUNTER` metric (meaningless with one mode)
- Reuse `hash_personal_api_key()` in `test_utils.rs` instead of duplicating inline SHA256 hashing

## How did you test this code?

- `cargo check -p feature-flags` — compiles clean
- `cargo test -p feature-flags -- api::auth` — all 5 auth tests pass
- `cargo clippy -p feature-flags -- -D warnings` — zero warnings

## Publish to changelog?

No

## Docs update

N/A

pull_requests.title: fix(replay): mobile recordings show black screen
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Mobile session recordings show a black screen during playback. This is a regression from removing the `REPLAY_SNAPSHOT_STORE` feature flag (#50211).

`SnapshotStore.canPlayAt()` requires a full snapshot before allowing events to be fed to rrweb, but mobile recordings don't have full snapshots in the raw data — they're synthesized later by `processAllSnapshots`. This causes `isWaitingForPlayableFullSnapshot` to stay true and the player to never receive events.

The `LoadingScheduler` is also affected — `findNearestFullSnapshot()` returns null, so the scheduler searches backward through every source trying to find a full snapshot that will never exist.

## Changes

- Add `syncFullSnapshotTimestamps()` to `SnapshotStore` — after `processAllSnapshots` runs, scans the processed result for full snapshot events and updates entries whose timestamps changed
- Call it from the coordinator's `processSnapshotsAsync` after processing completes, triggering `storeUpdated()` so `isWaitingForPlayableFullSnapshot` re-evaluates
- This fixes both `canPlayAt()` and the `LoadingScheduler`

## How did you test this code?

- Unit tests for `syncFullSnapshotTimestamps`: syncs synthetic timestamps, makes `canPlayAt` return true, no-op when unchanged, skips unloaded entries
- All existing SnapshotStore, coordinator, and snapshotDataLogic tests pass (55 tests)

Supersedes #50991

pull_requests.title: chore(flags): read new holdout field in Rust
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

Second task of the `holdout_groups` → `holdout` migration #47931 . The Rust feature flag service now reads the new `holdout` field with a fallback to the legacy `holdout_groups` format.

Django (first task) already writes both formats when saving experiment holdouts. This PR makes Rust prefer the new format when present.

### How it works

- If `holdout` is present, then use `exclusion_percentage` and construct variant as `holdout-{id}`
- Else if `holdout_groups` is present → use the legacy path (unchanged)
- Else → no holdout

### Logic changes

|------|--------|
| `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/flag_models.rs` | Added `Holdout` struct (`id`, `exclusion_percentage`) and `holdout: Option<Holdout>` field to `FlagFilters` |
| `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/flag_matching.rs` | Gate checks both fields; `is_holdout_condition_match` prefers new format with `else if` fallback to legacy (legacy path unchanged) |
| `rust/feature-flags/src/flags/test_flag_matching.rs` | Two new tests: new format only, and both formats present (new takes precedence) |

pull_requests.title: fix(devex): pr-approval-agent tuning and label retention
pull_requests.body:
**CI no longer blocks.** Failing checks are not a hard gate anymore — CI is its own gate and the agent is often triggered before checks finish.

**Zero reviews → escalate.** If there are no review comments at all, the agent escalates. Authors should request reviews (Codex, Claude, Copilot, Greptile, or human) before stamphog.

**Faster on obvious cases.** Gate denials and T1a-trivial PRs use low effort + 3 max turns instead of the full 20-turn deep exploration.

**Label stays on approval.** Only removed on failure. Branch protection's "dismiss stale reviews on push" handles revocation for free.

**Misc.** Disable session persistence, plain-language denial reasons (no internal gate codes), CODEOWNERS-soft for team-devex.

pull_requests.title: feat(mcp): add reverse proxy management tools
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Reverse proxy setup is one of the most commonly recommended steps for improving data accuracy (recovering 20-45% of blocked users). Currently, MCP conversations dead-end at "go check the UI" when users need to manage proxies. This adds full proxy lifecycle management through MCP.

## Changes

- Add 5 MCP tools: `proxy-list`, `proxy-get`, `proxy-create`, `proxy-retry`, `proxy-delete`
- Add YAML tool definitions in `services/mcp/definitions/proxy-records.yaml`
- Add `@extend_schema` annotations and `help_text` to the proxy record serializer/viewset so descriptions flow through the OpenAPI → MCP codegen pipeline
- Add explicit `retrieve` endpoint to `ProxyRecordViewset` (was missing, only `list` existed)
- Add `organization:write` to `OAUTH_SCOPES_SUPPORTED`
- Fix MCP codegen (`generate-tools.ts`) to handle org-scoped operations that don't have `project_id` in their path — previously it always added `.omit({ project_id: true })` which crashes with Zod v4's strict validation

## How did you test this code?

- All 203 MCP unit tests pass
- All 16 Django proxy record API tests pass (including 2 new tests for `retrieve` and cross-org 404)
- Full end-to-end testing through MCP with local Django + Temporal stack:

## Problem

The LemonTable component stories lack examples demonstrating how the table behaves with horizontal and vertical overflow scenarios. This makes it harder for developers to understand and test the table's scrolling behavior in constrained containers.

## Changes

Added three new Storybook stories to `LemonTable.stories.tsx`:

1. **WithHorizontalOverflow** - Demonstrates a table with many columns in a horizontally constrained container (max-width: 120)
2. **WithVerticalOverflow** - Demonstrates a table with many rows in a vertically constrained container (max-height: 60)
3. **WithHorizontalAndVerticalOverflow** - Demonstrates a table with both many columns and rows in a doubly constrained container

Also added supporting test data:
- `MANY_PEOPLE` - Array of 12 mock person objects for populating the table
- `WIDE_COLUMNS` - Array of 7 column definitions with various rendering logic to create a wide table layout

## How did you test this code?

pull_requests.title: fix(hogql): support nested join key expressions
pull_requests.body:
Solving a support ticket.

### Motivation

- Data warehouse joins were silently skipped when `source_table_key`/`joining_table_key` used nested HogQL function expressions (for example `toInt(ifNull(properties.asset_class, 1))`), causing queries to fail with unresolved join aliases.
- The parsing logic only handled simple `Field` nodes or a single `Call` wrapping a `Field`, so nested wrappers prevented extracting the underlying field chain.

### Description

- Added a recursive helper ` _extract_field_chain` to unwrap `Alias` and nested `Call` nodes and return the underlying `Field` chain. (`posthog/hogql/database/utils.py`).
- Updated `get_join_field_chain` to use the new helper and preserve the existing `capture_exception` behavior for unsupported expressions. (`posthog/hogql/database/utils.py`).
- Added a regression test `test_database_warehouse_joins_persons_poe_v2_source_key_nested_ast_call` that uses `toString(ifNull(properties.email, ''))` to ensure nested expressions register the join and allow query planning. (`posthog/hogql/database/test/test_database.py`).

### Testing

- Ran targeted `pytest -q posthog/hogql/database/test/test_database.py -k "source_key_ast_call or nested_ast_call"`, which failed in this environment due to missing Django dependencies. (failed)
- Attempted to run via `flox` per repo guidance, but `flox` is not available in this container so the test run could not proceed. (failed)
- Pre-commit hooks ran `bin/hogli lint:python:fix` a

pull_requests.title: fix(mcp): fix schema exclusions to prevent shared schema mutation
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

  `applyNestedExclusions` resolves `$ref` pointers by following them to the shared component schema and then mutating it in place. When a schema like `ActionStep` is referenced by multiple operations, excluding a field from one operation's request body silently strips it from every other reference too — responses, other endpoints, anything sharing that component.

  ## Changes

  - Switch `applyNestedExclusions` to a clone-on-write strategy: when a `$ref` is encountered, the referenced component is deep-cloned and the `$ref` replaced with the clone before any mutation, leaving the original component schema untouched
  - Move `applyNestedExclusions` from `services/mcp/scripts/lib/` into the shared `@posthog/openapi-codegen` package so it can be reused by other codegen consumers
  - Add a CI workflow for the `openapi-codegen` package

  ## How did you test this code?

  - 11 unit tests in `tools/openapi-codegen/tests/exclusions.test.mjs`, including 2 new tests that explicitly verify shared component schemas are not mutated across operations and through nested `$ref` chains
  - `pnpm --filter=@posthog/openapi-codegen test` — all passing

  ## Publish to changelog?

  No
pull_requests.author: VojtechBartos

pull_requests.title: fix(persons): skip properties timeline when no person exists
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When expanding a person row in the insight actors modal, the frontend requests the `properties_timeline` endpoint. If the person UUID exists in ClickHouse but has no corresponding record in PostgreSQL, this returns a 404 and shows an error toast: "Load result failed: Not found."

This happens in three scenarios:
- **Personless mode**: events ingested with `$process_person_profile: false`, cookieless tracking, or the `person_processing_opt_out` team setting intentionally skip Person record creation in PostgreSQL
- **Person merges**: the old person UUID is orphaned after distinct IDs are moved to the merged target
- **Person deletions**: the Person record is soft-deleted but ClickHouse events still reference the UUID

## Changes

Skip the `PropertiesTimeline` request when `actor.created_at` is falsy (indicating no PostgreSQL Person record exists) and fall back to the static `PropertiesTable` instead, which correctly shows empty properties for these actors.

## How did you test this code?

I haven't tested manually, but the code is minimal and the change seems sound.

Here's how it could be tested:
1. Find an insight with persons where one of the three scenarios above applies (easiest: use personless mode with `$process_person_profile: false`)
2. Click into a data point to open the persons modal
3. Expand a person row that has no properties or display name
4. Confirm no error toast appears and the properties tab shows an empty properties table

pull_requests.title: fix(variables): allow clearing list variable selection
pull_requests.body:
Add `allowClear` to LemonSelect for list-type HogQL variables in view/dashboard mode. This lets users clear a selected list variable back to null, which is needed for optional variables (e.g. filtering by channel type).

## Problem

When using a list-type HogQL variable in a SQL insight, there's no way to clear a selected value once one has been chosen. This is fine for required variables (e.g. granularity in a time series), but for optional variables (e.g. channel type to segment conversion rates) users have no way to return to an unfiltered/non-segmented view. The current workaround is to use a string variable instead.

## Changes

Both changes are in `VariableComponent` in `frontend/src/queries/nodes/DataVisualization/Components/Variables/Variables.tsx`:

**Insight view / dashboard mode** (inline dropdown, no popover):
- Added `allowClear` prop to `LemonSelect` so users can click X to clear the selection
- Changed `value` to respect `variable.isNull` — when null, the dropdown shows the empty/placeholder state instead of falling back to `default_value`
- Changed `onChange` to derive `isNull` from whether a value is selected (`!value`)

**Edit mode** (popover with "Set to null" toggle):
- No changes — edit mode already supports setting a list variable to null via the existing "Set to null" toggle, consistent with all other variable types

## How did you test this code?

**Insight view mode (primary fix):**
1. Create a SQL insight with a list-type variabl

pull_requests.title: feat: error tracking v2 query
pull_requests.body:
## In short

We are currently querying two databases. We don't want to do that. In some cases it becomes very inefficient. In some cases it gives wrong number of results.

This PR adds a switch to ET list UI hidden behind a feature flag which lets you choose v1/v2 query. This is for posthog employees only.

V2 query queries Postgres through CH so it is one big query. Always correct results

## Changes

### Connector

We have a postgres connector inside HogQL so we are able to query postgres. Not all tables tho. They are registered under `system.<some-table>` in some place. Registering new table does not require migration. It is a simple, reversible change in the code.

First thing to do was to make sure that tables we want are accessible via this `system` prefix:
- issues - this was already there,
- fingerprints - easy peasy,
- issue assignments - this could not be accessed by that connector because HogQL applies `team_id` filter on everything and that particular table had no `team_id`. So:
  - I added a migration which adds this field (NULLable),
  - made sure that when we create assignments, we add this field,
  - added a backfill migration which fills this data for all previous assignments.
  
### V2 query
  
This was LLM heavy. I iterated quite a lot and chose a query which offered the best performance. I wasn't able to fully test it because we don't have access to all tables we want on prod. I then asked LLM to convert it to AST format.

### Eve

pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Currently, the analytics properties only capture the full referer URL. To enable more granular analytics and filtering, we need to extract the host and pathname components separately from the referer URL.

## Changes

- Modified `get_request_analytics_properties()` to parse the referer URL and extract `host` (netloc) and `pathname` components
- Added `urllib.parse.urlparse` import to handle URL parsing
- Added two new analytics properties: `$host` and `$pathname`
- Updated all relevant test cases to expect the new properties

## How did you test this code?

The changes are covered by existing unit tests in `test_event_usage.py`. All test cases have been updated to verify:
- Correct extraction of host and pathname from valid URLs
- Proper handling of None values when referer is missing
- Graceful handling of edge cases (malformed URLs, missing components)

The test suite validates the new properties across multiple scenarios including API requests with and without referer headers.

pull_requests.title: feat(insights): prevent display settings leaking across insight tabs
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

Fix insight tab switching so trends-only display settings no longer leak into other insight types.

## Changes

When switching from a trends insight with a custom display to funnels, the shared query-property cache was incorrectly reapplying `display` onto `funnelsFilter`. That regression came from the recent `insightNavLogic` refactor that replaced helper-based merging with direct property spreading - https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/49824.

This change scopes cached visualization properties by target insight type:
- `display` now only carries across trends and stickiness
- `showPercentStackView` now only carries across trends
- `showValuesOnSeries` only carries to insight types that support it

I also restored the explanatory retention comment in `insightNavLogic.tsx`.

pull_requests.title: fix(trends): cache key collision for unstacked bar and pie chart
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Cache keys for the pie chart and the newish unstacked bar chart collide, as the `else` path in `grouped_chart_display_types` would treat any new chart types as "total value".

## Changes

- Fixes the issue for existing chart type (see LLM analysis below)
- Makes the default path throw an error and uses exhaustiveness checks to flag them early
---
From the actual trends execution paths, the safe cache buckets are:

- Standard time series: `Auto`, `ActionsLineGraph`, `ActionsBar`, `ActionsUnstackedBar`, `ActionsStackedBar`, `ActionsAreaGraph`, `TwoDimensionalHeatmap`
  Reason: these are grouped under the standard time-series cache key in [`posthog/schema_helpers.py`](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/blob/HEAD/posthog/schema_helpers.py#L157-L175). The generic trends runner only has special execution branches for cumulative, total-value, calendar heatmap, and box plot displays. The frontend semantic-equality map is aligned in [`frontend/src/scenes/insights/utils/queryUtils.ts`](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/blob/HEAD/frontend/src/scenes/insights/utils/queryUtils.ts#L131-L154).

pull_requests.title: feat(flags): add warn-then-enforce rate limiting with per-token overrides
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The Rust feature flags service needs a gradual rollout path for rate limiting. Currently, rate limiting is binary (log-only or enforce), which makes it risky to enable in production. Operators need the ability to warn customers before hard-blocking, and specific high-traffic tokens need custom rate limits.

## Changes

- Add two-tier warn-then-enforce rate limiting model for the `/flags` IP and token limiters:
  - **Warn tier**: Requests exceeding the warn capacity proceed but include a warning header (`X-PostHog-Rate-Limit-Warning`) so callers can react proactively
  - **Enforce tier**: Requests exceeding the enforce capacity are blocked with 429
- Auto-derive the warn tier as a configurable percentage of enforce capacity via `FLAGS_WARN_CAPACITY_RATIO` (default 0.8), so operators only need to set the enforce capacity and get warnings for free
- Add per-token custom rate limit overrides via `FLAGS_TOKEN_RATE_LIMIT_OVERRIDES` env var (JSON map of token → rate string), allowing specific tokens to have custom limits
- Separate metric labels into two orthogonal dimensions:
  - `mode`: `log_only` (observe-only) or `enforcing` (actually blocking) — reflects how the limiter is configured
  - `action`: `warned` (crossed warn tier) or `blocked` (crossed enforce tier) — reflects which threshold was crossed
  - In log-only mode, `action="blocked"` shows what _would_ be blocked without actually rejecting requests
- Unify log_only and enforcing modes to use identical threshol

pull_requests.title: fix: Scope first-party tokens by organization instead of team
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The first-party OAuth auto-approve flow (used by Code, MCP, etc.) was scoping tokens to all teams with `scoped_teams = [every team ID]` and `scoped_organizations = []`. The APIScopePermission.check_team_and_org_permissions check sees non-empty scoped_teams and tries to resolve view.team, but org-level endpoints like `/api/organizations/{id}/projects/` don't have a team, so it falls into the exception handler and raises a **403: "API keys with scoped projects are only supported on project-based endpoints."**

This meant scoped OAuth tokens could only hit project-level endpoints, never org-level ones. In practice, there was no way to list all projects across orgs without either:
  - Calling `/api/projects/{id}/` individually for every project (N+1 — 70+ requests for the PostHog org)
  - Using switch organization to patch the active org on `/@me`, which mutates shared state across us.posthog.com and code

The fix scopes first-party tokens by organizations instead of teams. `scoped_teams = []` (falsy) skips the team permission check entirely, and `scoped_organizations = [all user org IDs]` passes the org-level check. Project-level access is still enforced via queryset filtering by org membership.

Ref: https://github.com/PostHog/code/issues/1101

## Changes

The change is semantically equivalent in access scope, first-party tokens previously had `scoped_teams = [all teams]` (derived from org membership) and now have `scoped_organizations  = [all orgs]` (

pull_requests.title: chore(prop-defs): rm persons db connection
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The `property-defs-rs` service maintained a dual-path architecture for resolving group type mappings: it could read from either the shared cloud Postgres database or a separate persons database, with a rollout percentage controlling traffic to the personhog gRPC service. This added significant complexity — two connection pools, a fallback chain (personhog -> DB), rollout gating logic, and extra metrics to track each source.

Now that the personhog service is stable and fully rolled out, the DB-based resolution paths and rollout machinery are dead code.


## Changes

- **Removed the persons DB connection pool** (`persons_pool`) from `AppContext` — the service no longer opens a second Postgres connection for group type reads
- **Removed DB-based group type resolution** (`resolve_via_db`) and the `should_use_personhog` rollout gate from `GroupTypeResolver` — personhog is now the sole resolution path
- **Removed config flags**: `read_groups_from_persons_db`, `database_persons_url`, and `personhog_rollout_percentage`
- **Removed associated metrics**: `GROUP_TYPE_READS`, `GROUP_TYPE_RESOLVE_SOURCE`, `PERSONHOG_RESOLVE_FALLBACKS`, `DB_RESOLVE_DURATION`
- **Simplified error handling**: if personhog fails, the error is propagated instead of silently falling back to the DB
- **Updated tests**: converted from `#[sqlx::test]` (which required a real Postgres instance and test migrations) to plain `#[tokio::test]` since no DB access is needed. Removed `seed_group_

pull_requests.title: fix(test): Fix mock URL patterns
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Fix 6 stale mock URL patterns in `CohortEdit.test.tsx` `calculation status` tests that used the old `:team` pattern (no trailing slash) instead of the current `:team_id/` convention, causing mocks to never match and the retry button test to fail with `Unable to find an element with the text: /Calculation failed:/`.

## Details

The testing-library upgrade (`e08fa955db`) migrated all `beforeEach` mock handlers to `:team_id` with trailing slashes, but the `calculation status` test cases (added the same day in `2172fcef5c`) still used the old `:team` pattern. This caused the cohort API mocks to silently fall through, so `errors_calculating` was never set and the error banner never rendered.

**Change:** `/api/projects/:team/cohorts/${cohortId}` → `/api/projects/:team_id/cohorts/${cohortId}/` in all 6 mock URLs across the `calculation status` describe block.

pull_requests.title: fix: use initial_current_url for initial channel type URL rules
pull_requests.body:
Custom rules on URL (e.g. 'URL contains login') were evaluated against $initial_url which the SDK does not set; first-touch URL is stored in $initial_current_url. Use coalesce($initial_current_url, $initial_url) so initial channel type rules match correctly.

## Problem

Users who define custom channel type rules on "URL" (e.g. "URL contains login") for **Initial channel type** saw "Direct" instead of their custom channel, even when the first-touch URL matched the rule. Session-based channel type worked because it uses session entry URL; initial channel type was using a different property.

## Changes

- **`posthog/hogql/database/schema/channel_type.py`**: For initial channel type, the URL used in custom rules now comes from `coalesce($initial_current_url, $initial_url)` instead of only `$initial_url`. pull_requests.title: fix(replay): use asyncio.run() for recording deletion to fix 502 errors
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Recording deletion consistently returns 502 (`upstream_reset_before_response_started{protocol_error}`) on production
- Root cause: `async_to_sync()` from asgiref doesn't work correctly with aiohttp's `ClientSession` under Nginx Unit ASGI mode
- The recording-api successfully processes the delete (key is shredded), but the Django worker connection resets before sending the response back to the browser
- Switches to `asyncio.run()` which matches the working pattern used by the snapshot block-fetch endpoint (`_stream_blob_v2_to_client`)

## Investigation findings

- Recording-api logs: all deletes complete successfully (0 failures)
- Django logs: no delete-related messages at all (no success, no failure, no exception handler reached)
- Nginx Unit stderr: `RuntimeError: failed to send body` errors
- Production config: `NGINX_UNIT_PYTHON_PROTOCOL=asgi`

## Test plan

- [ ] Deploy to production and verify single recording deletion returns 204 (not 502)
- [ ] Verify bulk delete also works
- [ ] Confirm deleted recordings disappear from the listing after refresh

**Billable flag filtering** (`flag_definitions.rs`):

pull_requests.title: fix: pass org owner to BillingManager in Vercel uninstall to fix 403
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When a user uninstalls the PostHog integration from Vercel, the webhook calls `delete_installation` which needs to deauthorize billing. The `BillingManager` was created without a user, so the JWT token sent to the billing service had no `organization_role` (defaulting to `UNKNOWN`, role level 0). The billing service's `uninstall` endpoint requires `IsOrgAdmin` (role level 8+), causing a 403 and blocking the uninstall.

This has been failing since March 6 with 16 occurrences (error tracking issue: `019cc326-4f04-7992-9dcf-9933010a41d7`).

## Changes

- Query for an organization owner and pass their user to `BillingManager` so the JWT includes an admin-level `organization_role`
- Raise `RuntimeError` if no owner is found (instead of silently falling back to the broken `user=None` behavior)
- Update test assertion to expect the new `user=` kwarg

## How did you test this code

- All existing Vercel integration tests pass (15/15 delete-related tests)
- The `test_delete_installation_calls_billing_deauthorize` test now verifies the owner user is passed to `BillingManager`

## Changelog

No — this is a bug fix for an internal integration flow.

pull_requests.title: feat(django): more personhog callsite replacements
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The Django service currently queries the `posthog_person` PostgreSQL table directly via the ORM for several person-lookup patterns. We want to migrate these reads to the `personhog` gRPC service so that the Django web tier no longer needs direct access to the persons database. This is a step toward fully decoupling person storage from the web service.

The call sites migrated here are:

- **`get_persons_by_uuids`** (`posthog/models/person/util.py`) — used by the actor base query to resolve person UUIDs from insight results
- **`get_persons_by_distinct_ids`** (`posthog/models/person/util.py`) — used by 4 callers (event API, person API batch endpoint, events query runner, session summary patterns) to resolve persons from distinct IDs
- **`get_people`** (`posthog/queries/actor_base_query.py`) — used by trends/funnels/paths/stickiness to load and serialize person actors
- **`SessionRecording.load_person`** (`posthog/session_recordings/models/session_recording.py`) — used to load the person associated with a single session recording

## Changes

### Core: proto-to-model converter (`posthog/personhog_client/converters.py`)

Added `proto_person_to_model()` which converts a proto `Person` message into an unsaved Django `Person` model instance. This allows existing serializers and property accessors (like `person.distinct_ids`) to work without modification. The converter sets `_distinct_ids` on the model so the `distinct_ids` property returns them without hi

The `POST /api/agentic/provisioning/deep_links` endpoint generates URLs pointing to `/login/stripe?token=...` but no view existed to handle them — visiting the URL returned a 404. This blocks the Stripe APP deep link flow.

## Changes

pull_requests.title: fix(ci): increase Node.js heap size for mobile-replay schema build
pull_requests.body:
## Summary
- Fix OOM crash in `ts-json-schema-generator` during the "Check if mobile replay schema.json is up to date" CI step by setting `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192`
- The process was exhausting the default ~4GB Node.js heap limit, likely due to increased type complexity after the recent TypeScript 5.4.5 → 5.5.4 upgrade (PR #49824) combined with accumulated type growth from new products and dependency updates

## Context
- **Insight:** [Frontend typechecking OOM: ts-json-schema-generator heap exhaustion](https://app.mendral.com/insights/01KKHD6ZGDSJGH299VC2AWDFSJ)
- The `typescript:check` step in the same job already uses `--max-old-space-size=16384` — this change applies a similar (but smaller) allocation to the schema build step
- `ts-json-schema-generator` is at pre-release `2.4.0-next.6` which may not be optimized for TS 5.5; longer-term it may be worth upgrading or investigating memory usage

## Change
Single-line addition of `NODE_OPTIONS: --max-old-space-size=8192` env var to the mobile-replay schema build step in `.github/workflows/ci-frontend.yml`

pull_requests.title: fix(toolbar): replaceState bugs
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Toolbar OAuth authentication is broken for users on sites with SPA routers. Three related bugs:

1. **SPA router destroys toolbar during mount**: `cleanToolbarAuthHash()` calls `history.replaceState()` synchronously in `afterMount`, which triggers SPA routers (React Router, Angular, etc.) to re-render the page. The toolbar is killed mid-initialization before the OAuth code exchange can run. The PKCE verifier is never consumed and the auth params remain in the URL.

2. **Duplicate params on re-authentication**: The `authenticate` handler strips `__posthog=` from the hash before building the redirect URL, but not `__posthog_toolbar=`. Re-authenticating appends new params without removing old ones, and the backend's `normalize_and_validate_app_url` also didn't strip `__posthog_toolbar=`.

3. **No fallback when code exchange fails with stored tokens available**: If stale `__posthog_toolbar=` params survive in the URL (browser crash, navigation before cleanup), the toolbar attempts a doomed code exchange (expired PKCE/stale code) and leaves the user unauthenticated — even though valid OAuth tokens exist in `_postHogToolbarOAuth` localStorage.

## Changes

**Frontend (`utils.ts`):**
- Split `cleanToolbarAuthHash()` into `readToolbarAuthHash()` (read-only, returns params) and `cleanToolbarAuthHash()` (void, modifies URL). Reading is synchronous in `afterMount`; URL cleanup is deferred via `setTimeout(500)` to avoid triggering SPA routers.
- `cleanToolbarAuthHash` regex n

## Problem

The PostHog MCP server has no tools for managing CDP hog functions (destinations, transformations, site apps, webhooks) or browsing hog function templates. This means AI agents can't create, configure, or inspect pipeline integrations — a core part of the product.

This is also prerequisite for the adding workflows MCP tools.

## Changes

- **CDP serializer improvements** — add `scope_object` to the template viewset, `scope_object_write_actions` to `HogFunctionViewSet`, `@extend_schema` annotations on `invocations` and `rearrange` actions, and a dedicated `HogFunctionRearrangeSerializer` so drf-spectacular produces accurate request/response schemas
- **Template filtering** — add `template_id` filter to the hog function templates API via a new `FilterSet`
- **MCP tool definitions** — add `functions.yaml` (list, create, retrieve, partial-update, delete, invocations, rearrange) and `function_templates.yaml` (list, retrieve) with scopes, annotations, and detailed descriptions
- **Generated handlers and Orval schemas** — run the codegen pipeline to produce TypeScript tool handlers, Zod validation schemas, and API client code for both new tool groups
- **readOnly-in-required fix** — add `stripReadOnlyFromRequired` to the Orval schema generator so server-computed fields (bytecode, transpiled, order) are not required in request validation
- **Validation error improvement** — annotate `AnyInputField` with `@extend_schema_field` so drf-spectacular doesn't produce a broken s

pull_requests.title: test(storybook): update drifted visual regression snapshots
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Remove 8 outdated Storybook snapshot PNGs that are causing visual regression failures on master (shards 2/16 and 4/16). CI will regenerate them in UPDATE mode.

## Root Cause Analysis

All 4 failing stories share a common pattern: recent feature PRs modified shared UI components but were merged without rebasing on latest master, causing snapshot drift:

| Story | Shard | Failure | Root Cause |
|-------|-------|---------|------------|
| InsightPanelActions › UnsavedInsight | 2/16 | 1.12-1.88% pixel diff | `feat(customer-journeys)` [#50404](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/50404) |
| EditorFilters › WithLongFilterValue | 2/16 | Size mismatch (32px height) | `react-grid-layout v2` [#50732](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/50732) |
| ExperimentFrequentistTwoVariants | 4/16 | 1.94% pixel diff | Shared UI component changes |
| Funnels › FunnelWithInlineEventsEdit | 4/16 | 1.13-2.23% pixel diff | Shared UI component changes |

Failing run: https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/23007988479

## How this works

The Storybook CI workflow detects `mendral-app[bot]` as a trusted bot and runs in **UPDATE mode**. When snapshots are missing, the test runner regenerates them and the `handle-snapshots` job commits the fresh PNGs back to this branch.

pull_requests.title: fix(flags): classify local evaluation auth methods explicitly
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The local evaluation auth metric (`posthog_local_evaluation_auth_total`) used a binary classification — `secret_api_token` or `personal_api_key` — which misclassified any non-secret-token auth (session, OAuth, JWT) as `personal_api_key`. This is because `TeamAndOrgViewSetMixin.get_authenticators()` always appends default authenticators (session, OAuth, JWT, personal API key), so the endpoint accepts more auth methods than intended.

We need accurate per-method metrics to understand which auth methods are actually used before deciding whether to restrict the endpoint.

## Changes

**Python (`posthog/api/feature_flag.py`):**
- Replace binary `is_authenticated_via_project_secret_api_token` check with explicit `isinstance` checks against each authenticator class
- Add `_classify_auth_method()` helper with a dict-based lookup mapping each authenticator to a label: `secret_api_key`, `personal_api_key`, `oauth`, `jwt`, `session`, or `other`
- Rename label from `secret_api_token` to `secret_api_key` to match the authentication class (`ProjectSecretAPIKeyAuthentication`), distinguishing it from the permission class (`ProjectSecretAPITokenPermission`)

**Rust (`rust/feature-flags/src/api/flag_definitions.rs`, `src/metrics/consts.rs`):**
- Rename label from `secret_api_token` to `secret_api_key` for consistency with Python

## How did you test this code?

This is a metrics-only change with no behavioral impact. The metric was just added in #50753, so no existing dashboards r

pull_requests.title: fix(devex): Only run cyclotron migrations when CDP selected
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Does this fix an issue? Uncomment the line below with the issue ID to automatically close it when merged -->
<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->

When running `nodejs` with a subset of products selected that don't add `cdp_workflows` to the `NODEJS_CAPABILITY_GROUPS` (like `product_analytics` or `llm_analytics`), cyclotron migrations are unselected -> `run/bin/migrate-cyclotron-node` won't run -> `run_cyclotron_node_migrations` won't run -> `sqlx database create -D "$CYCLOTRON_NODE_DATABASE_URL"` won't run.

When `cdpCyclotronV2Janitor` is enabled, it attemps (and fails) to connect to the `cyclotron_node` database. pull_requests.title: fix(marketing-analytics): fix Meta Ads double counting conversions when pixel + CAPI are both configured
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/52246

When users configure both Meta Pixel (frontend) and Conversions API/CAPI (server-side) events, PostHog was double counting conversions. Meta already deduplicates conversions across channels in their aggregated action types (e.g., `lead`), but our code was summing **both** the aggregated type (`lead`) and the channel-specific type (`offsite_conversion.fb_pixel_lead`) together, resulting in exactly 2x the real conversion count.

For example, a user reported Meta showing 5 conversions while PostHog showed 10.

## Changes

Restructured Meta conversion action types from a 2-tier to a **3-tier priority system**:

1. **Omni** (`omni_lead`, `omni_purchase`, etc.) — fully deduplicated by Meta (preferred)
2. **Aggregated** (`lead`, `purchase`, etc.) — already deduplicated across pixel + CAPI by Meta
3. **Specific** (`offsite_conversion.fb_pixel_lead`, etc.) — channel-specific breakdowns, only used as last resort

Previously tiers 2 and 3 were combined into a single "fallback" list, causing the double count.

**Files changed:**
- `posthog/schema.py` — Split `MetaAdsConversionFallbackActionTypes` (now only aggregated) + new `MetaAdsConversionSpecificActionTypes`
- `frontend/src/queries/schema/schema-general.ts` — Same split in TS schema
- `frontend/src/queries/schema.json` — Regenerated
- `products/.../constants.py` — Import and expose new enum
- `products/.../adapters/meta_ads.py` — 3-tier `if omni >

pull_requests.title: fix(insights): restore optional null chaining HogQLBoldNumber
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The TypeScript 5.5 upgrade (#49824, merged March 6) removed optional chaining from `response.result` and `response.results` accesses in `HogQLBoldNumber`, causing `TypeError: Cannot read properties of null (reading '0')`.

This happens when an `InsightModel` with `result: null` is passed as `cachedResults` to `dataNodeLogic` — e.g. on dashboards before query results are loaded. The `'result' in response` check passes because the key exists on the object, but the value is `null`, and `null[0]` throws.

183 occurrences across 21 users since March 7. Error tracking issue: https://us.posthog.com/error_tracking/019cc6e6-ef93-7392-bc8a-3faef56e8665

## Changes

Restored optional chaining (`?.`) on `response.results` and `response.result` accesses in `HogQLBoldNumber` (lines 269-270 of `BoldNumber.tsx`).

## How did you test this code?

This restores the pre-existing safe access pattern that was accidentally removed during the TS upgrade. I didn't test this manually.

👉 _Stay up-to-date with [PostHog coding conventions](https://posthog.com/docs/contribute/coding-conventions) for a smoother review._

## Publish to changelog?

No

## 🤖 LLM context

Fix authored with Claude Code.

pull_requests.title: feat(mcp): support for nested exclude_params
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

We do not have a way how to exclude nested params


## Summary

  - Extend `exclude_params` to support dot-notation paths for nested field exclusion (e.g., `steps.*.selector_regex`)
  - All `exclude_params` — both top-level and nested — are now applied to the OpenAPI spec **before Orval runs**, so the generated Zod schemas never contain
  excluded fields. This means `generate-tools.ts` simply skips excluded fields instead of generating `.omit()` calls against them, preventing runtime errors
  from trying to omit nonexistent keys.

  ## What changed

  - **`schema-exclusions.mjs`** (new) — `applyNestedExclusions()` walks dot-notation paths through OpenAPI schemas, resolving `$ref` transparently, and
  deletes targeted properties + updates `required` arrays
  - **`generate-orval-schemas.mjs`** — replaced regex-based `collectOperationIdsFromFile` with YAML-parsing `parseToolDefinition` that extracts
  `exclude_params` per operation; calls `applyNestedExclusions` instead of the deleted `applyMcpExtensions`
  - **`generate-tools.ts`** — removed `applyMcpExtensions` import/call and `x-mcp-*` interface fields; skips excluded fields in body schema iteration
  (they're already gone from the Zod schema)
  - **Generated files** — `actions/api.ts` lost 26 lines (excluded fields removed from schemas), `cohorts/api.ts` lost 174 lines (same cleanup from
  regeneration)
  - **`schema-exclusions.test.ts`** (new) — 9 tests covering wildcard paths, deep nesting

pull_requests.title: fix(sig): stop filling buffers forever
pull_requests.body:
Ended up with a 3 workflow setup:
- `emit`, which runs a unique workflow per new signal, and tries to push into a bounded buffer in `buffer` (implemented via polling, which is meh, but fine, least annoying way to do this here I think)
- `buffer`, which runs once per team, and accepts signals from `emit` workflows, packages them up for grouping, and then writes them to s3
- `grouping-v2`, which runs once per team, accepts references to those bundled up s3 objects, and does the batch group processing workflow

could _arguably_ skip the writing to s3, and just make the grouping workflow a bounded buffer, but since the grouping workflow is so slow compared to the buffer packaging/writing, we'd end up with hundreds/thousands of workflows hanging around in temporal constantly polling the grouping workflow for multiple minutes/hours at a time.
pull_requests.author: oliverb123

reviews.author: copilot-pull-request-reviewer
reviews.body:
## Pull request overview

This PR replaces the original single-workflow signals pipeline (which could grow its buffer indefinitely) with a 3-workflow architecture to bound the buffer size:

1. **`emit` (SignalEmitterWorkflow)**: short-lived per-signal workflow that submits a signal to the buffer via a polling activity with backpressure.
2. **`buffer` (BufferSignalsWorkflow)**: long-running per-team workflow that batches signals and flushes them to S3 when full or on a timer.
3. **`grouping-v2` (

pull_requests.title: fix: guard if result is `null` so that we don't try to `.map` a bad result
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Fix for https://us.posthog.com/project/2/error_tracking/019cc3c2-fd9e-7893-85d6-45d9156d346a, and reported in https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/53009, if `result` is a `null` value, we try to `.map()` it, causing the error to be seen.

## Changes

Adds an additional guard to check if the final value of `data` is `null`, in which case replace it with an empty array.

## How did you test this code?

Quick sanity check with js fiddle

```js
response = {
  "msg": "hello",
  "response": "abc",
  // "results": [1,2,3],
  "result": null,
}

const data_before = 'results' in response ? response.results : 'result' in response ? response.result : []
const data_after = ('results' in response ? response.results : 'result' in response ? response.result : null) ?? []

console.log(data_before)
console.log(data_after)
```

results in 

```
> null
> []
```

## Publish to changelog?

<!-- For features only -->

no
