pull_requests.title: fix(endpoints): set hasMore to false when no more
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When query results are returned without pagination enabled, the `hasMore` field is not being set in the response. This can cause frontend consumers to be uncertain whether there are additional results available or if pagination simply wasn't applied.

## Changes

Added logic to set `hasMore` to `False` in the result when:
- Pagination is not enabled (or `pagination` is falsy)
- The result contains a "results" field

This ensures that all responses with results consistently include the `hasMore` field, indicating whether additional results are available.

## How did you test this code?

This is a straightforward conditional addition that sets a response field. The change is covered by existing query execution tests that verify response structure. CI should pass with this modification.

## Publish to changelog?

no

pull_requests.title: feat(llma): add Vercel AI SDK OTel middleware and make pipeline extensible
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The OTel ingestion pipeline only supported Pydantic AI spans. Vercel AI SDK uses different attributes (`ai.operationId`, `ai.prompt.messages`, `ai.response.text`, etc.) that weren't being mapped, so spans from Vercel AI SDK users were either misclassified or missing data. Additionally, the pipeline wasn't structured for adding more library middlewares.

## Changes

**Rust (`capture/src/otel/`):**
- Extracted event name classification into `event_name.rs` (following the same pattern as `identity.rs`)
- Data-driven `EventClassifier` registry with `ai.operationId` support for Vercel AI SDK span classification
- `gen_ai.operation.name` takes precedence when both attributes are present
- Filter noisy OTel resource attributes (`host.*`, `process.*`, `os.*`, `telemetry.*`, `service.*`) from event properties while preserving user-set attributes like `user.id` and `posthog.ai.debug`

**Node.js (`nodejs/src/ingestion/ai/otel/`):**
- Created `middleware/` directory with an `OtelLibraryMiddleware` interface where each middleware owns its detection via `matches(event)` (Express.js-style, supports stacking)
- Extracted existing Pydantic AI logic into `middleware/pydantic-ai.ts` (no behavioral changes)
- Added `middleware/vercel-ai.ts` that maps `ai.*` attributes to PostHog's standard `$ai_*` properties, handles tool calls, and strips SDK-specific keys
- Strip unmapped Vercel AI span attributes (`ai.response.reasoning`, `gen_ai.request.max_tokens`, `gen_ai.response.finish_reason

  - Django app configuration and models

  - Comprehensive test structure

pull_requests.title: fix(logs): add time_bucket where filter to logs queries
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

due to the size of our skip indexes queries could be very slow with certain filters (e.g. full text search) as loading the skip index for all the potential granules needed a huge amount of data

this is unfortunate because clickhouse doesn't prioritise pruning with small indexes (e.g. the timestamp minmax index) before loading the larger indexes

adding time_bucket filtering to the primary key will prune a significant number of granules before secondary skip indexes are loaded

## Changes

add time_bucket where clause to logs queries

## How did you test this code?

verified it works locally. No change in behaviour so existing tests should cover it

## Publish to changelog?
no

pull_requests.title: fix(proxy): clear stale warning message when proxy becomes valid
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When a managed proxy setup encounters a transient issue (e.g. Cloudflare proxying detection during DNS validation), a warning message is written to the ProxyRecord. If the customer fixes the issue and the setup completes successfully, the status updates to VALID/Live but the old warning message persists in the UI — confusing customers into thinking there's still a problem.

## Changes

Pass `message=""` when updating the ProxyRecord status to VALID in the create workflow, clearing any stale messages from earlier retries. This matches what the monitor workflow already does (line 445 of `monitor.py`).

## How did you test this code?

Verified the monitor workflow already uses `message=""` when setting VALID status. The create workflow was the only place missing it. The `update_record` function correctly handles empty strings (the check is `if message is not None`).

## Publish to changelog?

No

pull_requests.title: fix(test): wait for portal content in side panel before clicking delete
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Fixes the "Deleting an insight from dashboard redirects back" E2E test which fails **~23% of the time** (119/513 commits in the last 7 days) with a 90s timeout waiting for the delete button inside `#side-panel`.

## Problem

`openInfoPanel()` returns as soon as `#side-panel` is visible, but the insight panel content is rendered via `createPortal` into a target registered by `SidePanelInfo`'s `useEffect` — which runs *after* render. During this gap, the delete button either doesn't exist yet (insight data still loading) or is portaled into the hidden inline panel.

Three previous fix attempts:
| PR | Approach | Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| #49970 | Added waits but didn't scope locator | ❌ Button found in hidden inline panel |
| #50057 | Waited for `.scene-panel-actions-section` globally (`attached`) | ❌ Never merged — global selector still matches hidden container |
| #50276 | Scoped delete button locator to `#side-panel` | ⚠️ Fixed wrong-container but not timing — 23% failure rate persists |

## Fix

Add a wait for `#side-panel .scene-panel-actions-section` to be **visible** in `openInfoPanel()`. This combines:
1. **Scoping** to `#side-panel` (avoids matching the hidden inline panel — the mistake #50057 made)
2. **Timing** — waits for the portal content to actually render inside the side panel before returning

This ensures both the portal target switch and insight data loading are complete before `clickDeleteInsight()` tries to find the button.

## Insight
https:/

pull_requests.title: chore(ci): increase SETUP_OVERHEAD_SECONDS from 20 to 60
pull_requests.body:
Bumps `SETUP_OVERHEAD_SECONDS` from 20 to 60 in `turbo-discover.js` and updates the stale inline comment.

## Why

Many products have 0s in `.test_durations` (new/uncaptured). With OH=20, each adds only 20s to the packing budget, so the bin-packer crams them into mega-buckets that massively overshoot the 10-min target.

## Proof — actual CI wall-clock times

**Master (OH=20)** — 7 matrix entries, worst bundled bucket 18.3 min (11 products), second worst 16.6 min (15 products):
- [Run 22965245681](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/22965245681)
- [Run 22964762832](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/22964762832)
- [Run 22964354570](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/22964354570)

**This PR (OH=60)** — 10 matrix entries, worst bundled bucket 13.7 min (5 products), no bucket larger than 8 products:
- [Run 22968179986](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/22968179986)

**Result:** Worst bundled bucket drops from **18.3 → 13.7 min**. The two mega-buckets (11 and 15 products) get split into five balanced buckets (2–8 products each). Tradeoff is 3 more parallel jobs.

## Problem

The data warehouse source wizard has a multi-step flow (credentials, schema selection, confirmation, sync progress) that's too heavy when we already know exactly which tables we need - like in the Signals Inbox where each source only needs one specific table.

## Changes

Adds a `requiredTables` prop to `sourceWizardLogic`. When set, the wizard skips the schema selection step and confirmation dialog entirely - it auto-configures the required tables with default sync settings and creates the source directly. On success with `onComplete` provided, it calls that callback instead of showing the sync progress step.

## How did you test this code?

Manual testing of the signals setup flow end-to-end.

## Publish to changelog?

No

pull_requests.title: fix(ci): add pr-approval-agent to Python CI triggers and baseline mypy errors
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- **Add `tools/pr-approval-agent/**` to the `ci-python.yml` path filter** so that Python code quality checks (ruff, mypy) trigger when the approval agent files are changed in PRs. Previously, changes to `tools/pr-approval-agent/` did not trigger Python CI, which allowed 39 mypy violations to slip through in PR #49727.
- **Add 39 existing mypy violations to `mypy-baseline.txt`** to unblock the Python code quality check on master. The violations are pre-existing in the agent code and are now baselined so they don't block CI.

## Root Cause

PR #49727 added new Python files to `tools/pr-approval-agent/` but the `ci-python.yml` workflow's path filter didn't include `tools/pr-approval-agent/**`. This meant Python checks never ran on the PR. The PR's CI also failed for a separate reason (missing uv version pin), so the mypy errors went completely unnoticed. The follow-up fix (d814b14) resolved the uv pin, allowing mypy to run — which then surfaced the type errors on master.

## Changes

1. **`.github/workflows/ci-python.yml`**: Added `tools/pr-approval-agent/**` to the `dorny/paths-filter` configuration
2. **`mypy-baseline.txt`**: Added 39 baseline entries for the existing violations (`union-attr`, `call-overload`, `var-annotated`, `arg-type`, `misc`)

pull_requests.title: fix(ci): fix pr-approval-agent trigger + harden reviewer
pull_requests.body:
Fixes the approval agent not triggering (#50749) and addresses review feedback from #49727.

**Trigger fix**
- Remove `author_association` check — GitHub webhooks return inconsistent values (`COLLABORATOR` vs `MEMBER`) for org members. Write access is already required to apply the `stamphog` label.

**Reviewer hardening**
- Explicitly disallow `Agent`, `WebFetch`, `WebSearch` tools (defense in depth on top of `dontAsk` mode)
- Move diff path and analysis instruction out of the untrusted content block to reduce prompt injection surface
- Reinforce JSON output requirement in system prompt (`output_format` alone doesn't reliably constrain agent SDK output)

**CI cleanup**
- Switch to blobless checkout (`filter: blob:none`) instead of full clone
- Use `ubuntu-latest` instead of depot runner

pull_requests.title: feat(signals): Add SignalSourceConfig-based gating for DW-based sources
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Signal emission for data import sources (GitHub, Linear, Zendesk) was gated behind the team-level `proactive_tasks_enabled` flag. This is too coarse - users should be able to enable/disable each signal source independently from the Inbox.

## Changes

Each signal emitter now declares its own `source_product`/`source_type` identity (matching the `SignalSourceConfig` model choices). At job creation time, instead of checking `proactive_tasks_enabled`, we look up whether a `SignalSourceConfig` row exists with `enabled=True` for the specific source. This means signal emission only happens when the user has explicitly opted in for that source via the Inbox UI.

The viewset also gains lifecycle hooks: enabling a data-import-backed source triggers a sync of the relevant schema, and disabling a clustering source cancels its Temporal workflow.

## How did you test this code?

Existing tests updated to use `SignalSourceConfig` rows instead of `proactive_tasks_enabled`. Added a test case for when no config row exists at all.
pull_requests.author: Twixes

pull_requests.title: fix(ci): scope pnpm installs to only necessary packages per workflow
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

Redo of #50536 (reverted in #50612) with fixes for the issues that caused the original to break.

### What went wrong with #50536

1. **Scoped install missed products/\***: `--filter=@posthog/mcp...` only installs declared npm deps (`@posthog/mosaic`, `@posthog/openapi-codegen`), but MCP imports from 8 products via `tsconfig.json` path aliases — invisible to pnpm's dependency graph.

2. **CI didn't catch it**: The `ci-mcp.yml` path filter had a typo on line 37 (`mcp-ci.yml` instead of `ci-mcp.yml`), so changes to the workflow file itself never triggered MCP tests. The PR only touched workflow files, so MCP jobs were skipped entirely.

3. **MCP tsconfig missing `dom` lib**: The tsconfig specified `lib: ["es2021"]` but UI apps use DOM APIs (`document`, `window`). This accidentally worked because `@types/jsdom` (hoisted from unrelated workspace packages) pulled in `lib.dom.d.ts` via a triple-slash reference. With scoped installs, `@types/jsdom` isn't present.

### Fixes in this PR

| Fix | File |
|-----|------|
| Add `--filter='./products/*'` to MCP install commands | `ci-mcp.yml`, `ci-mcp-ui-apps.yml` |
| Fix self-trigger typo (`mcp-ci.yml` → `ci-mcp.yml`) | `ci-mcp.yml` |
| Add `dom` lib to MCP tsconfig | `services/mcp/tsconfig.json` |
| Scope oauth-proxy install | `ci-oauth-proxy.yml` |
| Scope update-openapi install | `ci-backend.yml` |

### Filters per workflow

| Workflow | Filter |
|----------|--------|
| `ci-mcp.yml` (3 jobs) | `--filter=@posthog/mcp... --fil

## Problem

We want to understand adoption patterns as we migrate authentication methods for local evaluation / flag definitions requests. Currently there is no metric tracking whether a request uses a secret API token vs a personal API key.

## Changes

- **Python (`posthog/api/feature_flag.py`)**: Add `posthog_local_evaluation_auth_total` Prometheus counter with a `method` label (`secret_api_token` or `personal_api_key`), incremented on each `local_evaluation()` request using the existing `is_authenticated_via_project_secret_api_token` helper.
- **Rust (`rust/feature-flags/src/metrics/consts.rs`)**: Add `FLAG_DEFINITIONS_AUTH_COUNTER` constant (`flags_flag_definitions_auth_total`).
- **Rust (`rust/feature-flags/src/api/flag_definitions.rs`)**: Increment the counter after each successful auth path in `authenticate_flag_definitions()`.

## How did you test this code?

- `ruff check` passes with no lint errors
- `cargo check -p feature-flags` compiles successfully
- No behavioral changes — only metric emission added, so existing tests remain valid
- For Rust: `RUST_LOG=info ENABLE_METRICS=true cargo run -p feature-flags` and then `curl http://localhost:3001/metrics`

## Publish to changelog?

Do not publish to changelog.

pull_requests.title: feat: Add email notification for reverse proxy provisioning
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When users set up a reverse proxy for PostHog, they need to know when the provisioning process is complete so they can update their SDK configuration. Currently, there's no notification system to inform users that their proxy is ready to use.

## Changes

- Added email notification functionality for when reverse proxies are successfully provisioned
- Created new email template `proxy_provisioned.html` that informs users their proxy is ready and provides a link to proxy settings. This email is not used by default, we usually use customer.io email instead (see below)
- Added Customer.io template mapping for the new email type
- Integrated email sending into the proxy creation workflow as a non-blocking operation
- Email failures do not prevent the proxy provisioning workflow from completing successfully

![CleanShot 2026-03-11 at 17.25.16.png](https://app.graphite.com/user-attachments/assets/6421e083-1a23-4152-bec7-332568daddaf.png)

## Publish to changelog?

No - this is an internal improvement to the proxy provisioning system that enhances user experience but doesn't introduce new user-facing features.

pull_requests.title: fix(llma): use campaign_groups instead of campaigns for LinkedIn Ads
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

LinkedIn Ads has an inverted hierarchy compared to other platforms:
- **Other platforms**: `campaigns` = what users manage and name
- **LinkedIn**: `campaign_groups` = what users manage, `campaigns` = subdivisions (ad sets)

The adapter was pointing to `campaigns` + `campaign_stats`, but should use `campaign_groups` + `campaign_group_stats`. Both tables are already synced by the data import pipeline.

## Changes

- Updated `schema-general.ts` LinkedIn config to use `campaign_groups` / `campaign_group_stats` table names
- Changed adapter validation strings and JOIN FK from `campaign_id` to `campaign_group_id`
- Created new test CSVs (`campaign_groups.csv`, `campaign_group_stats.csv`) with 3 campaign groups aggregating the previous 5 campaigns
- Updated test expectations (3 results instead of 5, same totals)
- Removed old `campaigns.csv` and `campaign_stats.csv` test files
- Regenerated OpenAPI schemas

## How did you test this code?

All 66 adapter tests pass:
```
pytest products/marketing_analytics/backend/hogql_queries/test_adapters.py -x -k "linkedin" -v
```

no

pull_requests.title: chore(prop-defs): migrate to personhog
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

`property-defs-rs` resolves group type mappings by querying the `posthog_grouptypemapping` table directly via SQL against either the shared cloud DB or the persons DB. We want to migrate this to the personhog gRPC service (via the router) so that group type resolution is centralized through personhog like other person/group data access. This needs to be rolled out gradually with a fallback to the existing DB path on failure.

## Changes

- **`Cargo.toml`** — Added `personhog-proto` and `tonic` dependencies, plus `tokio-stream` as a dev-dependency for tests.
- **`config.rs`** — Added three env-var-driven config fields:
  - `PERSONHOG_ADDR` (default `""`) — gRPC address; empty = disabled entirely.
  - `PERSONHOG_TIMEOUT_MS` (default `5000`) — per-request timeout.
  - `PERSONHOG_ROLLOUT_PERCENTAGE` (default `0`) — percentage of batches routed through personhog (0 = off, 100 = all).
- **`metrics_consts.rs`** — Added five metric constants for observability: resolve source (`db`/`personhog`/`db_fallback`), personhog error count, fallback count, and duration histograms for both paths.
- **`group_type_resolver.rs`** (new) — Extracted all group type resolution logic into a self-contained `GroupTypeResolver` struct that owns the cache, personhog client, and rollout config. Contains:
  - `resolve()` — public entry point: cache check, dispatch, apply results.
  - `should_use_personhog()` — percentage-based rollout gate.
  - `resolve_via_db()` — existing SQL pat

pull_requests.title: feat: Replace reverse proxy onboarding step with project notice banner
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Ad blockers can silently drop 10-25% of events from web applications. While PostHog offers a reverse proxy solution to route data through custom domains to prevent this, users weren't being made aware of this important feature after completing onboarding.

## Changes

- **Removed reverse proxy onboarding step**: Eliminated the dedicated reverse proxy configuration step from the onboarding flow, along with its associated logic, stories, and snapshots
- **Added project-level reverse proxy notice**: Introduced a new `missing_reverse_proxy` notice that appears in the main navigation when no proxy records exist, informing users about the benefits of setting up a reverse proxy
- **Enhanced proxy settings page**: Added SDK setup instructions directly on the proxy settings page to help users update their configuration after creating proxy records
- **Improved code snippet generation**: Updated `OnboardingDocsContentWrapper` to support showing reverse proxy domains in code snippets when `useReverseProxy` prop is enabled
- **Refactored project notices**: Extracted notice definitions into a reusable `NOTICES` configuration object and created a `Notice` component with Storybook stories for better testing and documentation
- **Updated navigation logic**: Modified the project notice priority system to show reverse proxy notices when appropriate, with session-based dismissal to avoid showing multiple notices consecutively

The reverse proxy feature is now surfaced as a contextua

pull_requests.title: fix(test): raise averageDuration threshold
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Raise the `averageDuration` threshold from 130ms to 175ms in the `sessionRecordingDataCoordinatorLogic` performance test to eliminate false-positive CI failures caused by runner variability.

## Context

The performance test `loads all data` fails intermittently when `averageDuration` exceeds 130ms. The most recent failure was 138.47ms (6.5% over threshold) on a commit that only modified CI workflow YAML files — confirming this is CI runner noise, not a code regression.

The 175ms threshold provides ~35% headroom above the observed failure while still catching real regressions (a 2x slowdown would be ~260ms, well above the new limit). This follows the same pattern as the previously resolved `stdDev` threshold fix in the same file.

## Changes

- `frontend/src/scenes/session-recordings/player/sessionRecordingDataCoordinatorLogic.performance.test.ts:136`: `expect(averageDuration).toBeLessThan(130)` → `expect(averageDuration).toBeLessThan(175)`

pull_requests.title: fix: prevent RawPostDataException in stripe signature verification
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Production 500 errors on `/api/agentic/` endpoints caused by `RawPostDataException`. DRF's default throttle classes (`BurstRateThrottle`/`SustainedRateThrottle`) call `PersonalAPIKeyAuthentication.find_key_with_source()` during `check_throttles()`, which accesses `request.data`. This consumes the WSGI input stream (sets `_read_started=True` without caching `_body`), so when `verify_stripe_signature()` later accesses `request.body`, Django raises `RawPostDataException`.

Not reproducible locally by default because `RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED` defaults to `False` in dev — throttles never run, stream is never consumed. Reproduced by setting `RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=True` in instance settings.

## Changes

Two-layer fix:
- **Primary**: `@throttle_classes([])` on all 8 agentic provisioning view functions — prevents DRF from running throttle classes that consume the stream
- **Defense-in-depth**: `_get_raw_body()` helper in `signature.py` — if the stream IS consumed, returns `b""` instead of crashing (401 signature mismatch instead of 500)
- Regression tests simulating DRF stream consumption

## How did you test this code

- Reproduced locally by enabling `RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=True` in instance settings, confirmed `RawPostDataException`
- Verified fix prevents the exception
- All 103 tests in `ee/api/agentic_provisioning/test/` pass
- New regression tests: `TestVerifySignatureAfterDRFParsing` with stream-consumed, stream-not-consumed, and body-cached scenarios

## Changelog

No
pull_requests.author: MattBro

reviews.author: rafaeelaudibert
reviews.body: Why are the DRF throttlers consuming the stream? I believe a better fix is to prevent them from doing that 🤔

reviews.author: rafaeelaudibert
reviews.body:
We should add a test proving that the stream is *NOT* being consumed at all.

We can probably achieve that by adding a test that sets `RATE_LIMIT_ENABLED=True` in that test, and guarantee we can actually process the request.

It's not fully future-proof, though. We should make sure we have enough observabiliyy here to learn whether these errors start happening in the future.

(We need that observability for the launch day anyways, can be a follow-up)

pull_requests.title: fix: remove next_url from redirect params to prevent DOM-based XSS
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The Vercel connect flow had a DOM-based XSS vulnerability (VERIA-212). The backend's `_validate_next_url` correctly rejects non-allowlisted URLs by returning `""`, but JavaScript treats empty string as falsy. The frontend used `sessionInfo?.next_url || nextUrl` which fell through to the raw, unvalidated `next` URL query parameter — allowing `javascript:` URIs to reach `window.location.href`.

**Stacked on #50544** (Tom's CSRF session binding fix).

## Changes

pull_requests.title: feat(onboarding): add simplified single-screen product selection
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The current onboarding flow uses a two-step process (choose use case → pick products) that adds unnecessary friction. We want a simpler, more delightful single-screen experience where users pick one product to start with, behind the `ONBOARDING_SIMPLIFIED_PRODUCT_SELECTION` feature flag.

## Changes

Replaces the two-step product selection with a single-screen arc wheel carousel:

**Carousel with spring physics**
- Spring-based animation engine (slightly underdamped ζ≈0.87) for smooth, natural-feeling transitions
- Coast friction model for spin-and-release behavior
- Velocity-capped spring so clicking distant products shows each intermediate product along the way
- 1:1 drag-to-visual mapping — grabbing a product moves it exactly with your cursor
- Dead zone (5px) to distinguish clicks from drags

**Design**
- Arc wheel layout with colored glow on the active product
- Page-level color wash that shifts with the selected product
- Large (180px) animated hedgehog area with per-product hedgehog personalities
- Social proof badges ("Used by 185K+ teams") and setup effort indicators
- User-centric copy ("What should we build first?", capability bullets)

**Easter egg**
- Nauseous hedgehog triggered by rapid back-and-forth shaking or fast spinning
- Agitation accumulator with hysteresis (trigger at threshold, release at 50%) to prevent flickering during normal browsing

**Tracking events**
- `onboarding_product_browsed` — fires when user settles

pull_requests.title: fix(proxy): handle Cloudflare CNAME flattening in DNS validation
pull_requests.body:
## Problem\

Customers whose domains use Cloudflare nameservers get a false positive "Cloudflare proxying enabled" error during managed proxy setup, even when their CNAME record has proxy disabled (grey cloud/DNS 
This is because Cloudflare automatically flattens CNAME records into A records at the authoritative DNS level, so the `wait_for_dns_records` activity sees Cloudflare IPs without a visible CNAME and
incorrectly blames the customer.
## Changes

In the `wait_for_dns_records` activity's `NoAnswer` handler, before showing the Cloudflare proxying error, we now resolve the target CNAME to its A records and compare them with the customer's A records. 
the IPs match, the setup is valid — the CNAME is just being flattened — and we treat it as a successful validation. If the IPs differ, the customer genuinely has proxy enabled on their zone and we show the
existing error message.
## How did you test this code?

Validated with real DNS lookups against domains in both states:
- Domain with Cloudflare nameservers + DNS only: A records match target CNAME's A records → correctly treated as valid
- Domain with proxy enabled: A records differ from target → correctly shows error
- Domain with non-Cloudflare nameservers: CNAME visible → works as before (no change in behavior)

pull_requests.title: feat(workflows): Hide advanced fields in email by default
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The email step configuration in hogflows always shows optional fields (Reply-To, Preheader) even though they're rarely used. As more optional fields are added in the future (CC, BCC), this will create unnecessary visual clutter. Users configuring a simple email (From, To, Subject, body) shouldn't need to scroll past empty optional fields.

## Changes

Adds a show/hide pattern for advanced email fields:

- Reply-To and Preheader are hidden by default behind + Reply-to / + Preheader buttons
- Clicking a button reveals the field inline, a dismiss (X) button clears and hides it
- Fields with existing values are auto-revealed on load so no data is hidden
- Fields are marked with isAdvancedField on the existing EmailMetaField type - adding CC/BCC later requires only a field definition, no UI changes

![advanced-fields-hidden](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/7e259ab9-5b3c-4f69-9706-3b834efbe0e6)

## How did you test this code?

Added unit tests for the kea logic covering: advanced fields hidden by default, reveal/hide round-trip with idempotency, and auto-reveal on mount for fields with existing values. Tested manually in the hogflow email step inline panel, the email template editing dialog, and the email destination configuration - verifying fields are hidden by default, revealing/dismissing them, and confirming fields with existing values auto-show on load.

pull_requests.body:
## Problem

After making Klaviyo resumable (#50629), production imports started failing with ReadTimeout errors because the raw external_requests.get call had no retry handling. DLT's REST client previously handled this transparently.

## Changes

  - Added fetch_page() with tenacity retry decorator (5 attempts, exponential backoff + jitter) for ReadTimeout, ConnectionError, 429, and 5xx responses
  - Moved should_yield() check inside the for item in items loop to drain the batcher before it overflows, matching the Stripe source pattern
  - Moved next_url computation before the item loop so resume state can be saved on mid-page yields

pull_requests.title: fix(flags): pass paired group identifiers instead of cartesian product
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When evaluating feature flags that involve multiple group types, the Rust flag evaluation service fetched group properties using two independent `ANY` filters:

```sql
WHERE group_type_index = ANY($2) AND group_key = ANY($3)
```

The parameters came from separate `HashSet<GroupTypeIndex>` and `HashSet<String>` collections, so the pairing between each group type index and its key was lost before the query was built. The query expressed a cartesian product: match any row whose index is in the first set **and** whose key is in the second set.

### Example

A team has two group types and three group rows:
- `organization` (index 0), key `"acme"`
- `company` (index 1), key `"acme"` ← same key, different group type
- `company` (index 1), key `"globex"`

A request comes in for `groups: {"organization": "acme", "company": "globex"}`. We want rows for exactly `(0, "acme")` and `(1, "globex")`.

**Old query** (`ANY/ANY`): `group_type_index IN (0, 1) AND group_key IN ('acme', 'globex')` — returns **3 rows**, including the false match `(1, "acme")` because index 1 is in the first array and `"acme"` is in the second. The flag evaluator loads the wrong company's properties.

**New query** (`UNNEST` paired join): matches only the exact `(index, key)` pairs requested — returns the correct **2 rows**.

This isn't theoretical — many teams in production have group keys shared across multiple group type indexes.

### Additional issues with the old approach

**Changes:**
- Replace group property SQL lookup from independent `ANY(type_indexes) AND ANY(keys)` filters to a paired join via `UNNEST(type_indexes[], keys[])`.

pull_requests.title: feat(mcp): restore OAuth client_name on analytics events and forward as header
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The OAuth-verified `client_name` (from token introspection) was added to `mcp tool call` analytics events in #48366 but was accidentally dropped in #48770 when `getBaseEventProperties()` was removed during an instructions refactor. This left us with only the client-reported `mcp_client_name` (from the MCP `initialize` handshake), which is less reliable — clients can self-report anything, and some just say "node".

Context: https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C02E3BKC78F/p1773144953063849

## Changes

- Restore `client_name` from the Durable Object cache (populated by OAuth introspection) on `mcp tool call` / `mcp tool response` events in `trackEvent()`
- Forward the OAuth client name as a new `x-posthog-mcp-oauth-client-name` HTTP header to the PostHog API, so it also appears on CRUD events (`dashboard created`, `insight created`, etc.) via `report_user_action`
- Read the new header in `get_mcp_properties()` in `event_usage.py` as `mcp_oauth_client_name`

## How did you test this code?

- All 27 `posthog/test/test_event_usage.py` tests pass, including updated cases for the new `mcp_oauth_client_name` property

pull_requests.title: fix(mcp): preserve tool ordering and operation IDs in scaffold-yaml
pull_requests.body:
Fixes repeated "chore: update OpenAPI generated types" auto-commits on unrelated PRs (seen on #50492 and #50237).

**Root cause** — `scaffold-yaml --sync-all` had two bugs in `mergeWithExisting()`:
1. Rebuilt the tools map in OpenAPI iteration order instead of preserving the existing YAML order, causing non-deterministic reordering diffs on every CI run
2. Overwrote hand-authored `operation` values (e.g. `feature_flags_retrieve_2`) with dedup-normalized base names (`feature_flags_retrieve`), undoing intentional variant choices

**Fix** — iterate existing YAML tools first (preserving order), only append genuinely new operations at the end. Never overwrite the `operation` field on existing entries.

Also cleans up stale workflows tool definitions that were removed upstream.

pull_requests.title: fix: correct stripe app manifest provisioning base_url
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The Stripe APP spec appends `/provisioning/services`, `/provisioning/health`, etc. to the `provisioning.base_url` in the manifest. Our `base_url` was set to `https://us.posthog.com/api/agentic/provisioning/`, which caused Stripe to call URLs like `https://us.posthog.com/api/agentic/provisioning/provisioning/services` (double `/provisioning/`), resulting in 404s.

## Changes

Changed `provisioning.base_url` in `services/stripe-app/stripe-app.json` from `https://us.posthog.com/api/agentic/provisioning/` to `https://us.posthog.com/api/agentic/` so Stripe correctly calls `https://us.posthog.com/api/agentic/provisioning/services`.

The dev manifest (`stripe-app.dev.json`) does not override `provisioning.base_url` so no change needed there.

## How did you test this code?

This is a config-only change. Verified by reading the [Stripe APP provisioning spec](https://docs.stripe.com/stripe-apps/build-provisioning-api) which confirms Stripe appends `/provisioning/*` paths to the base_url.

## Publish to changelog?

No

## 🤖 LLM context

Co-authored by Claude Opus 4.6.

pull_requests.title: feat(data-warehouse): add optional names filter to get_schemas
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

`get_schemas` on every data import source fetches metadata for **all** tables/endpoints, even when a caller only needs a subset. For SQL-backed sources this means running `information_schema` queries (and `COUNT(*)` for row counts) against every table in the database schema — wasteful when only a few specific tables are needed.

## Changes

Adds `names: list[str] | None = None` to `_BaseSource.get_schemas` and all 43 source implementations:

- **SQL database sources** (postgres, redshift, mysql, mssql) — filter pushed directly into the SQL query with `AND table_name = ANY(%(names)s)` / `AND table_name IN %(names)s`. For postgres/redshift row count queries, the initial table listing is filtered so `COUNT(*)` only runs on requested tables. Foreign key queries are similarly filtered.
- **Snowflake, BigQuery, MongoDB** — filter applied after fetching (driver/API limitations make in-query filtering less ergonomic).
- **Static/API endpoint sources** (stripe, hubspot, shopify, salesforce, etc.) — filter applied in Python on the result list; no change to fetch logic since these are cheap hardcoded lists or lightweight API calls.
- **Stubs** (ashby, customer_io) — signature updated only.

All existing callers omit `names` and get unchanged behaviour (all schemas returned).

## How did you test this code?

I am an agent and have not tested this manually. The change is additive (new optional parameter defaulting to `None` which preserves existing behaviour). Existing tests p

pull_requests.title: fix(ci): pass PYTEST_ADDOPTS through turborepo to product tests
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- **Root cause**: Turborepo 2.x strict env mode filters out `PYTEST_ADDOPTS` (including `--snapshot-update`) from product test processes because it's not listed in the `backend:test` task's `env` or `passThroughEnv` keys in `turbo.json`
- This causes snapshot mismatches to fail tests instead of auto-updating, and then the snapshot patch/upload steps are skipped because the test step failed (no `if: always()` guards)

## Changes

1. **`turbo.json`** — Add `PYTEST_ADDOPTS` to `passThroughEnv` for the `backend:test` task. Using `passThroughEnv` (not `env`) so it doesn't affect the turbo cache hash.
2. **`.github/workflows/ci-backend.yml`** — Add `always() &&` to the `if` conditions on the three snapshot steps (Verify, Generate, Upload) in the `turbo-tests` job, so they run even if the test step fails.

## Context

Reported in [Slack thread](https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0113360FFV) — product test snapshots (e.g. error-tracking) never auto-update in CI. [PR #49518](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/49518) added the snapshot infrastructure to turbo-tests but didn't account for Turborepo's strict env filtering. See [failed run](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/22951009003/job/66615520485?pr=50501) on PR #50501.

Insight: https://app.mendral.com/insights/01KKEFB67471T29S99AWYFFA47


---

pull_requests.title: fix(llma): optimize sentiment ClickHouse queries for high-volume teams
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Sentiment classification workflows are timing out for high-volume teams. A team with 1.4B `$ai_generation` events per week was hitting 80s+ ClickHouse query times on UUID-based lookups that should return only 5 rows.

Root cause: the `GENERATIONS_BY_UUID_QUERY` included a `length(properties.$ai_input) <= N` filter which translates to `JSONExtractRaw` on every scanned row in the date range. Combined with a 30-day default lookback and several frontend callers not passing date ranges at all, this created massive full-scan queries.

## Changes

**Backend:**
- Remove `length(properties.$ai_input)` filter from `GENERATIONS_BY_UUID_QUERY` -- it forced ClickHouse to decompress + parse JSON for every row in the scan (benchmarked 2.4x slower: 40s with vs 17s without on a 7-day window)
- Apply the size guard post-fetch in Python instead (before `json.loads`), where it only runs on the ~5 returned rows
- Reduce `QUERY_LOOKBACK_DAYS` from 30 to 7 as a safer fallback when callers omit date ranges

**Frontend:**
- Pass tight date windows (`createdAt` to `createdAt + 2 days`) from trace detail and session scene callers that were previously omitting date ranges entirely
- Extract `SENTIMENT_DATE_WINDOW_DAYS` constant to `sentimentUtils.ts` for consistency across callers
- The list view column renderers already passed the global date filter and are unchanged

## How did you test this code?

Benchmarked directly on production ClickHouse via the sentiment worker pod against the affec

`ExperimentExposuresQueryRunner` still scans the events table directly, even with precomputation is enabled. Since `ExperimentQueryRunner` caches exposures with a TTL, the Exposures tab and metrics can show different exposure counts until the cache refreshes.

- Parameterize all exposure tests with `(direct, precomputed)` to verify both paths produce identical results

## Summary

- Update 20 inline snapshot assertions across 3 GeoIP test files to reflect the current MaxMind MMDB postal code (`44199`) for IP `12.87.118.0` (Cleveland, Ohio), which changed from `44192`.

### Affected files
- `nodejs/src/cdp/templates/_transformations/geoip/geoip.template.test.ts`

The MaxMind GeoIP2 City database was updated and now maps IP `12.87.118.0` to postal code `44199` instead of `44192`. All other GeoIP fields (city, country, lat/lon, etc.) are unchanged. The snapshot bot only runs on PRs (not master pushes), so the stale snapshots were not caught before merge.

No production code changes — only test inline snapshots updated.

pull_requests.title: fix(mcp): build ui-apps before wrangler deploy
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The wrangler build command only ran `copy-instructions.ts` but never built the UI apps. Since `ui-apps-dist/` is gitignored, wrangler deploy would fail with ENOENT errors whenever new UI apps were added (survey, workflow, llm-costs, etc.) because the built HTML files didn't exist.

Additionally, building all 21 Vite+React apps in parallel caused OOM kills (exit code 137) in CI.

## Changes

- Chain `pnpm run build:ui-apps` before `copy-instructions.ts` in the wrangler build command so UI app HTML files are always generated before bundling
- Limit build concurrency to 4 parallel Vite processes to avoid OOM in CI

## How did you test this code?

Ran `npx wrangler deploy --dry-run` — all 21 UI apps built successfully and the dry-run completed without errors.

## Publish to changelog?

no

pull_requests.title: refactor: Separate interceptors by task queue
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

We have too many interceptors running in our temporal workers. This causes problems:
* Tracebacks are now extremely long as they traverse the whole interceptor chain. They can no longer render sometimes.
* Each interceptor adds overhead, which is unnecessary if a particular set of workflows/activities does not rely on it (e.g. a lot of interceptors check for activity name and return if not supported, which means they could just be removed).

<!-- Who are we building for, what are their needs, why is this important? -->

<!-- Does this fix an issue? Uncomment the line below with the issue ID to automatically close it when merged -->
<!-- Closes #ISSUE_ID -->

## Changes

This assigns interceptors one or more task queues, and checks which interceptors match the given queue when initializing a worker. Only interceptors that do match are simply are initialized and passed to the worker, otherwise no overhead is added.

Here is a breakdown of each interceptor and what they do/care about:
* `BatchExportsMetricsInterceptor`:
	* Only relevant in batch export queues, otherwise does nothing and can be removed.
* `PostHogClientInterceptor`:
	* Error tracking. Used by all queues.
* `DeleteRecordingsMetricsInterceptor`:
	* Only operates in delete recordings workflows which execute in the session replay task queue. Otherwise does nothing and can be removed.
* `EvalsMetricsInterceptor`:
	* Only operates on worfklow `run-evaluation` and its activities. This

pull_requests.title: fix(ci): scope pnpm store cache per workflow
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- 6+ CI workflows share the same `node-cache-Linux-x64-pnpm-{hash}` cache key via `actions/setup-node`'s built-in `cache: pnpm`, causing cross-workflow cache pollution
- Whichever workflow saves first stamps an incomplete pnpm store (from its filtered `--filter` subset). Since `setup-node` never saves on a cache hit, the incomplete store persists indefinitely
- ci-nodejs was downloading ~663 pnpm packages (33% of 2029) on every run despite cache hits

### Fix

Add `cache-dependency-path` that includes each workflow's own YAML file alongside `pnpm-lock.yaml`. This gives each workflow a unique cache key hash, preventing cross-pollution while keeping `setup-node`'s built-in cache machinery. On a miss, the shared `restore-keys` prefix still provides warm starts from other workflows' entries, and since it's not an exact hit, `setup-node` correctly saves a new per-workflow entry.

## Test plan

- [ ] PR CI: all jobs pass (cache miss expected — new key hashes)
- [ ] After merge: first master run seeds per-workflow caches; subsequent runs should show fewer downloads

Zsh single-quoted strings don't support backslash escapes — `\'` just ends the string early, causing `_hogli:150: unmatched '` on tab complete.

Two manifest commands have apostrophes in their descriptions (`dev:api-key` and `posthog:hobby-installer-linux`). Switched to the POSIX `'"'"'` idiom.

pull_requests.body:
**Problem**
The MCP scaffold script discovers operations by matching the product folder name as a URL substring (e.g. searching for `/workflows/` in API paths). This breaks for products whose API routes use a different slug — `workflows` owns `/hog_flows/` routes, so `scaffold-yaml --sync-all` silently skipped it with "no operations found."

The hand-authored YAML for workflows survived only because the skip path doesn't touch the file.

**Fix**
Use `x-explicit-tags` from the OpenAPI spec as priority 1 for operation discovery, same approach as the frontend type generator (`generate-openapi-types.mjs`). Falls back to URL matching for endpoints that don't have tags yet.

ViewSets in `products/<name>/backend/` are auto-tagged via module path. ViewSets living elsewhere (like `posthog/api/hog_flow.py`) need an explicit `@extend_schema(tags=["workflows"])` decorator — added here for both `HogFlowViewSet` and `HogFlowTemplateViewSet`.

Also skips deprecated endpoints now, so `/api/environments/` duplicates no longer leak into YAML files (was causing noise in cohorts and error_tracking).

**`feature` field**
Changed `feature: hog_flows` → `feature: workflows` to align with the product folder name. This field is used for runtime tool filtering via `?features=` query param. Open to discussion on whether `feature` should always equal the product dir name or if there are cases where it shouldn't — would appreciate input from folks who use the features param.

**Docs**
Updated the impleme

pull_requests.title: feat(phai): add pagination to TeamTaxonomyQuery for large orgs
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

`TeamTaxonomyQueryRunner` has a hard-coded `LIMIT 500` with no offset support. For large orgs, AI tools can't paginate through events efficiently. The system/ignored event filtering happens in Python post-processing, which breaks pagination semantics — filtered events consume pagination slots, meaning pages can return fewer results than requested.

## Changes

- Add `limit`/`offset` fields to `TeamTaxonomyQuery` and `hasMore`/`limit`/`offset` to the response schema
- Use `HogQLHasMorePaginator` for proper offset-based pagination in `TeamTaxonomyQueryRunner`
- Move ignored event filtering from Python post-processing to SQL via `NOT IN` clause using new `IGNORED_EVENT_NAMES` constant
- Add secondary sort by event name for deterministic pagination ordering
- Add `limit`/`offset` to `ReadEvents` model, threading pagination through to `format_events_yaml` with a "more available" hint
- Update MCP Zod schema with pagination fields on `ReadEventsQuerySchema`
- Add skill references: team taxonomy query example, skipped events documentation
- Update tool description with pagination guidance
- Regenerate all downstream types (schema.py, schema.json, tool-inputs.json)

## How did you test this code?

- `pytest posthog/hogql_queries/ai/test/test_team_taxonomy_query_runner.py` — 5 tests pass (including new `test_pagination_with_limit_and_offset`, updated `test_limit` to assert `hasMore=True`)
- `pytest ee/hogai/tools/read_taxonomy/test/` — 9 tests pass (including new paginatio

pull_requests.title: chore(personhog): update person protos w last seen at
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

A `last_seen_at` column was recently added to the `posthog_person` table,
but the personhog proto definitions and the replica/router services were never updated to include it.
This means any client reading persons through the personhog gRPC API cannot access this field.


## Changes

- **Proto**: Added `optional int64 last_seen_at = 11` to the `Person` message in `proto/personhog/types/v1/person.proto`.
  Uses millis-since-epoch, consistent with the existing `created_at` field.
- **Replica storage types**: Added `last_seen_at: Option<DateTime<Utc>>` to the storage `Person` struct
  and the Postgres `PersonRow` / `PersonWithDistinctIdRow` row types.
- **Replica SQL queries**: All `SELECT` statements in `storage/postgres/person.rs` now include `last_seen_at`.
- **Replica proto conversion**: Maps `last_seen_at` to millis via `timestamp_millis()` in the `From<storage::Person> for proto::Person` impl.
- **Router test fixtures**: Added `last_seen_at: None` to all test `Person` constructions so the router compiles.
- **Python stubs**: Regenerated via `bin/generate_personhog_proto.sh`.


## How did you test this code?

- `cargo check -p personhog-proto` — proto crate compiles cleanly
- `cargo check -p personhog-replica -p personhog-router` — both service crates compile cleanly
- Python proto stubs regenerated and verified to contain the new `last_seen_at` field in both `person_pb2.py` and `person_pb2.pyi`

pull_requests.title: chore(personhog): sqlx compile time type checking
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The personhog-replica crate uses runtime `sqlx::query_as::<_, RowType>(sql).bind(x)` for all database queries. This means SQL correctness is only verified at runtime — a typo in a column name, a schema change that renames a field, or a type mismatch between the Rust struct and the database column will only surface when the query actually executes. Additionally, every query requires a hand-written intermediate `Row` struct with `#[derive(FromRow)]` and a `From<Row> for DomainType` conversion to handle type widening (e.g. `i32` → `i64` for team_id), adding ~100 lines of boilerplate.


## Changes

Converts all 21 queries in personhog-replica's postgres storage layer to use sqlx compile-time checked macros (`query_as!`, `query!`, `query_scalar!`). These macros validate SQL against the real database schema at develop-time and cache the metadata in `.sqlx/` JSON files for offline CI builds.

**Removed boilerplate** (6 intermediate structs + 3 `From` impls):
- `PersonRow`, `PersonWithDistinctIdRow`, `From<PersonRow> for Person`
- `GroupRow`, `From<GroupRow> for Group`
- `GroupTypeMappingRow`, `From<GroupTypeMappingRow> for GroupTypeMapping`
- `DistinctIdRow`, `DistinctIdWithVersionRow`

**Type widening now handled in SQL** instead of Rust `From` conversions:
- `id::bigint as "id!"`, `team_id::bigint as "team_id!"` — SQL cast with forced non-null (cast expressions lose NOT NULL metadata)
- `CASE WHEN is_user_id IS NULL THEN NULL ELSE (is_user_id != 0) END

## Pull request overview

pull_requests.title: chore(flags): refactor FlagsCanonicalLogLine and split evaluation counters
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

`FlagsCanonicalLogLine` mixes request-level metadata with per-flag evaluation counters in a single flat struct. `merge_rayon_delta` must explicitly list every evaluation counter field to merge rayon thread-local deltas back into the request log. If someone adds a new eval counter and writes it via `with_canonical_log` inside `evaluate_single_flag` but forgets to add it to `merge_rayon_delta`, the counter silently stays at zero, re-introducing a silent data loss bug for that field.

Closes https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/issues/49255

## Solution

Extract the 6 evaluation counter fields into an `EvalCounters` sub-struct embedded in `FlagsCanonicalLogLine` as `pub eval: EvalCounters`. The `merge` method on `EvalCounters` uses an exhaustive destructuring pattern (no `..`), so adding a new field to `EvalCounters` without a corresponding merge rule is a **compile error**.

`merge_rayon_delta` becomes a one-liner that delegates to `self.eval.merge(&delta.eval)`.

## Changes

**`handler/canonical_log.rs`**
- New `EvalCounters` struct with `flags_device_id_bucketing`, `cohorts_evaluated`, `property_cache_hits`, `property_cache_misses`, `person_properties_not_cached`, `group_properties_not_cached`.
- `EvalCounters::merge()` with exhaustive destructuring for compile-time safety.
- `FlagsCanonicalLogLine` embeds `pub eval: EvalCounters` replacing the 6 flat fields.
- `merge_rayon_delta` delegates to `self.eval.merge(&delta.eval)`.
- `emit()` references

pull_requests.title: fix(llma): playground save fixes
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Two bugs in the playground save flow:

1. **Generic error messages**: When saving a prompt from the playground (e.g. "Save as new prompt"), backend validation errors (invalid name, duplicate name, etc.) were silently swallowed. The user only saw a generic "Failed to save prompt" toast with no indication of what went wrong.

2. **Wrong prompt saved**: When multiple prompts exist in the playground (e.g. duplicating a prompt for comparison), all save actions used `promptConfigs[0]` instead of the prompt the user clicked save on. This meant modifying the second prompt and saving would save the first prompt's content.

## Changes

- Export `getApiErrorDetail` from `llmPromptLogic` and use it in all four playground save catch blocks to surface actual API error details
- Add `promptId` parameter to all save actions (`saveToLinkedPrompt`, `saveToLinkedEvaluation`, `saveAsNewPrompt`, `saveAsNewEvaluation`) so the correct prompt config is used
- Extract `MessageActions` component outside the scrollable area so the add message button stays visible

## How did you test this code?

Tested locally.

## Publish to changelog?

no
pull_requests.author: richardsolomou

pull_requests.title: fix(flags): remove MAX_PROPERTY_VALUES limit on property filters
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

Remove the `MAX_PROPERTY_VALUES=1000` limit that was added in #24946. This limit is now redundant and overly restrictive.

## Context

The limit was originally added to prevent OOM issues in the Python `/decide` endpoint when evaluating feature flags with large property filter value arrays (e.g., `distinct_id IN [1000+ values]`).

Since then:

1. **Rust service handles evaluation** — The Rust feature-flags service handles all flag evaluation and doesn't have memory issues with large property filters. It uses efficient iterator-based short-circuit evaluation with O(n) performance and minimal memory overhead.

2. **Better limit exists** — `MAX_FEATURE_FLAG_FILTER_SIZE_BYTES` (512KB default, added in #47995) provides a more accurate safety net by limiting total payload size rather than arbitrarily constraining list length.

3. **Limit isn't enforced consistently** — Many existing flags already exceed 1000 values (created before the limit existed), so it's not providing meaningful protection anyway.

More internal discussion on this: https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C07Q2U4BH4L/p1772829719772819

## Changes

- Remove `MAX_PROPERTY_VALUES` constant
- Remove validation code that checks property value list length
- Remove associated test

## Test plan

- [x] Verified byte-size limit tests still pass (`test_per_flag_filter_size_limit_on_create`, `test_per_flag_filter_size_limit_on_update`)
- [x] Verified feature flag creation tests still pass

pull_requests.title: fix(llm-gateway): always throttle against auth user
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The LLM gateway was vulnerable to rate limit poisoning attacks where malicious users could inject victim user IDs through client-provided fields (`user` param, `standard_logging_object.end_user`, or `litellm_params.metadata.user_id`) to bill costs against other users' quotas or bypass rate limiting entirely.

## Changes

- Modified `_extract_end_user_id()` to always return the authenticated user's ID instead of trusting client-provided user identification
- Updated rate limiting logic to consistently use the authenticated user's ID for quota enforcement across both OAuth and API key authentication methods
- Added safeguard in `record_cost()` to prevent overriding existing end_user_id with client-supplied values
- Removed fallback logic that previously checked multiple client-controlled sources for user identification

## How did you test this code?

Added test coverage including:
- Unit tests verifying that all client-provided user fields are ignored in favor of authenticated user ID
- Poisoning prevention tests confirming attackers cannot bill costs to victim accounts
- Rate limiting tests ensuring users are properly limited by their own authenticated identity
- Context tests verifying end_user_id cannot be overridden once set from authentication

## Publish to changelog?
No

pull_requests.title: fix(ci): detect non-isolated products and trigger full test suite
pull_requests.body:
**Context:** PR #50469 only touched the signals product but CI only ran signals' own tests — Django and all other product tests were skipped. Signals isn't isolated (imports heavily from posthog/temporal, posthog/hogql, etc.), so its changes should trigger the full suite.

**Root cause:** all 45+ products had a no-op `backend:contract-check` echo script, making turbo's cache status meaningless for isolation detection.

**Fix:** use turbo's own mechanics. Only truly isolated products (those with a product-level `turbo.json` narrowing contract-check inputs, currently just `visual_review`) keep the `backend:contract-check` script. CI now diffs `backend:test` tasks against `backend:contract-check` tasks — products missing from the latter are non-isolated, and any change to them triggers all products + Django.

Also updates hogli product:lint and the bootstrap template so new products are non-isolated by default.

## Summary

- Limit `property-defs-rs` test threads to 4 to prevent intermittent `PoolTimedOut` failures in CI

## Evidence

- [Failed run with PoolTimedOut](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/actions/runs/22909845666): 4 tests failed at `sqlx-core-0.8.6/src/testing/mod.rs:226` during database setup

pull_requests.title: fix(hogql): remove double db call for group type mapping
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

`Database.create_for()` in the HogQL database initialization runs on every single query execution. It makes two uncached ORM queries to `posthog_grouptypemapping` per execution:

1. `GroupTypeMapping.objects.filter(team=team)` — loads all model fields, filtered by `team_id`
2. `GroupTypeMapping.objects.filter(project_id=team.project_id)` — loads all model fields

On the `posthog-query-django` deployment this generates ~830K queries/min (~1.66M total across both calls). Meanwhile, there is an existing Redis-cached function (`get_group_types_for_project`) with a 5-minute TTL that is already used elsewhere for the same data — but these two hot-path call sites bypass it entirely.


## Changes

- Replace both direct ORM queries in `posthog/hogql/database/database.py` with a single call to `get_group_types_for_project(team.project_id)`, which checks Redis cache first and only falls back to the database on cache miss.
- Update `_setup_group_key_fields()` to accept the cached `list[dict]` result instead of querying the ORM itself.
- Remove the now-unused `GroupTypeMapping` and `PERSONS_DB_FOR_READ` imports.


## How did you test this code?

- Ran existing HogQL database tests (`test_database_group_type_mappings`, `test_database_group_type_mappings_overwrite`) — both pass.
- Ran property types tests that exercise group type mapping through full HogQL query compilation (`test_group_boolean_property_types`, `test_group_property_types`, `test_group_types_a

pull_requests.title: feat: add multi-slack-integration support to subscriptions
pull_requests.body: ## Problem When a team has multiple Slack integrations, the subscription workflow might pick the wrong one. The backend uses `.first()` to select the integration, and the frontend hardcodes `slackIntegrations?.[0]`. This causes `SlackApiError: channel_not_found` because the UI shows channels from one integration but delivery uses a different one. There is no way for users to specify which Slack integration a subscription should use. Discovered in: https://posthoghelp.zendesk.com/agent/tickets/52177 ## Changes Model: Add integration FK to Subscription (SET_NULL if the integration is deleted). Migration: Schema change + data backfill that populates integration_id for all existing Slack subscriptions using the same integration that get_slack_integration_for_team would return (lowest id per team). Backend delivery: Use subscription.integration directly. Falls back to get_slack_integration_for_team() only if the integration was deleted (null). Frontend: Replace the hardcoded firstSlackIntegration with the IntegrationChoice picker component. Channel picker renders after an integration is selected. target_value is cleared only when the user actively switches integrations (not on initial auto-select). ## How did you test this code? - migration test to verify the exisiting behavior is preserved

pull_requests.title: fix(test): Normalize auto-increment IDs in snapshot
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Add regex normalization in `clean_varying_query_parts()` to replace auto-increment feature flag IDs embedded in `session_recording_linked_flag` JSON queries (`@> '{"id": <N>}'::jsonb`) with the stable placeholder `99999`
- Update the snapshot for `test_copy_feature_flag_create_new` to use the normalized ID

## Problem

`test_copy_feature_flag_create_new` keeps breaking because the `@snapshot_postgres_queries` decorator captures raw SQL containing `session_recording_linked_flag @> '{"id": 45}'::jsonb`. The `45` is the auto-increment PK of the feature flag created in setUp, which shifts whenever new tests are added to the class. The existing `clean_varying_query_parts()` normalizes `team_id`, `project_id`, etc., but didn't handle IDs embedded inside JSON literals.

## Fix

Rather than forcing a fixed PK in setUp (which is fragile), this adds a single regex to the existing normalization infrastructure — the same approach used for `team_id`, `cohort_id`, `organization_id`, etc. This makes the snapshot immune to ID shifts for any test that hits this code path.

Related insight: Snapshot test re-broken: test_copy_feature_flag_create_new ID shifted again


---

A user created an experiment, linked an existing feature flag, and clicked "Save as draft". pull_requests.title: perf(flags): optimize dependency graph with Kahn's queue and BFS closure
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The dependency graph construction and topological sorting in the feature flags evaluation pipeline had suboptimal algorithmic complexity:
- `build_dependency_graph` was constructing a graph from the full list of flags, even though only reachable flags matter
- `compute_stage_indices` used O(V²) repeated scans to find nodes with zero in-degree, instead of efficient topological sorting

## Changes

- Use BFS closure in `DependencyGraph::new()` to collect only reachable flags, avoiding full-list graph construction
- Collect edges during BFS and pass them to `from_nodes()`, which now accepts a pre-computed edge map, eliminating redundant `extract_dependencies()` calls
- Replace O(V²) repeated-scan topological sort in `compute_stage_indices` with Kahn's algorithm for O(V+E) batching
- Take `out_degree` by value to express consumption semantics
- Extract `inc_graph_tombstone` helper to DRY tombstone metric recording
- Expanded and reorganized test coverage, including a test that runtime-filtered active flag dependencies are not followed

## How did you test this code?

Comprehensive unit tests covering:
- Basic dependency chains and diamond dependencies
- Cycle detection (self-cycles, mutual cycles, transitive cycles)
- Disconnected subgraphs and isolated nodes
- Stage index computation correctness
- Runtime-filtered active flag dependencies not followed
- Edge cases (empty pools, missing dependencies, wide fan-out)

## Publish to changelog?

No

pull_requests.title: feat: add cursor pagination for EventsQuery CSV exports
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

CSV exports of events use offset pagination, which gets progressively slower with each page and eventually times out before getting to the 300k row limit. 

## Changes

Adds timestamp-based cursor pagination for `EventsQueryRunner`, routed runner-agnostically through `process_query_dict`.

**Schema:** Added `nextCursor` field to `EventsQueryResponse`.

**Base runner:** Added `apply_pagination_cursor(cursor)` no-op base method on `QueryRunner` for subclasses to override.

**Query service:** `process_query_dict` / `process_query_model` accept a `pagination_cursor` param, threaded through to `query_runner.apply_pagination_cursor()`.

**EventsQueryRunner:**
- `apply_pagination_cursor(cursor)` sets `self.query.before` (DESC) or `self.query.after` (ASC) based on sort direction
- Returns `nextCursor` (last row's timestamp) when ordering by timestamp, no aggregations, no source query

Why not use`HogQLCursorPaginator`? 
The presorted optimization builds an inner subquery that shares the same WHERE clause. `HogQLCursorPaginator` injects its cursor filter onto the outer query only, which would miss the inner subquery and return incorrect results.

**CSV exporter:** Passes cursor through `process_query_dict(pagination_cursor=cursor)` instead of mutating the query dict directly.

## How did you test this code?

- Added tests in `test_events_query_runner.py`
- Manually tested CSV export locally and verified cursor timestamps in ClickHouse queries

pull_requests.title: fix(experiments): Fix non-deterministic pagination on experiments list
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0A27HCTE2H/p1773068570670329

The experiments list can show inconsistent results across pages - experiments appearing/disappearing on refresh, and pagination counts not adding up.

Two issues:

1. On page load, afterMount fires loadExperiments() before urlToAction processes the URL. This is a leftover from before server-side pagination was added - urlToAction already triggers the load with the correct filters, making the afterMount call redundant and introducing a race between two loads with potentially different filters.
2. The backend doesn't apply a default sort order when no explicit sort is requested. Postgres doesn't guarantee row order without ORDER BY, so LIMIT/OFFSET pagination can theoretically return inconsistent results. In practice the order should be usually stable, but worth being defensive here.

## Changes

- Add default `ORDER BY -created_at` when no explicit sort is requested.
- Remove redundant `loadExperiments()` from `afterMount`. The initial load now goes through `urlToAction` → `setExperimentsFilters` → `loadExperiments()`, same pattern as feature flags UI uses.
- Skip the 300ms debounce on the initial load so the page doesn't briefly flash an empty state.

## How did you test this code?
I wasn't able to replicate the bug locally, but tested that these changes preserve current functionality.
pull_requests.author: jurajmajerik

pull_requests.title: fix(experiments): remove ExperimentProgressStatus
pull_requests.body:
## Problem
`ExperimentProgressStatus` was left over from when experiment state was derived on the frontend from `start_date` and `end_date`.
Since experiments now persist an explicit `status` field in the database, keeping a second status type in the frontend duplicated the source of truth and forced the app to translate between backend state and frontend-only state.
That made the code harder to follow and increased rollout risk, because the frontend and backend were not speaking the same status vocabulary.

## Changes
- remove frontend usage of `ExperimentProgressStatus` and use `ExperimentStatus` for experiment state instead
- keep `Paused` as a UI-only derived display state when a running experiment has an inactive feature flag
- keep `Complete` as a display label for backend `stopped`, rather than as a separate stored/frontend enum value (we can later rename the label to "stopped")
- align the experiments list filter backend with stored statuses, while keeping `status=complete` as a compatibility alias during rollout
- update experiment status helpers, tags, filters, and tests to reflect the simplified model

## Testing
- CI passes with new tests
- Tested locally, verified that filtering works and that status displays correctly on experiments

pull_requests.title: feat(llma): add playground analytics events and remove beta tag
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

We have no visibility into how users interact with the LLM analytics playground — which models they pick, whether they tweak parameters, how they use tools, or where they hit friction like rate limits. We also want to take the playground out of beta.

## Changes

- Remove `tags: ['beta']` from the playground entry in `manifest.tsx` and `products.tsx`
- Add 20 `posthog.capture` events covering all meaningful playground interactions:

| Event | Properties | Trigger |
|-------|-----------|---------|
| `llma playground prompt submitted` | `prompt_count`, `models`, `has_tools`, `total_message_count` | User runs prompts |
| `llma playground prompt completed` | `model`, `provider`, `latency_ms`, `ttft_ms`, tokens, `success`, `aborted` | Prompt stream finishes |
| `llma playground prompt aborted` | — | User stops generation |
| `llma playground opened from source` | `source_type` | Opened from trace/prompt/evaluation |
| `llma playground saved to source` | `action` | Save to linked or save as new |
| `llma playground source unlinked` | — | User unlinks from source |
| `llma playground reset` | — | User resets playground |
| `llma playground prompt config added` | `prompt_count` | User adds a prompt config |
| `llma playground prompt config removed` | `prompt_count` | User removes a prompt config |
| `llma playground model changed` | `model`, `is_byok` | User picks a different model |
| `llma playground parameters configured` | `max_tokens`, `temperature`, `top_p`, `reason

pull_requests.title: fix(mcp): Remove JSON stringification from MCP state tracking
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The MCP tool execution tracking is unnecessarily stringifying state objects before passing them to analytics events. This adds an extra serialization step and makes the data harder to work with downstream in analytics pipelines.

## Changes

- Removed `JSON.stringify()` calls on `inputState` and `outputState` variables in MCP tool execution tracking
- Changed error output state from stringified string to structured object format (`{ error: errorMessage }`)
- Added `ai_product: 'mcp'` field to all analytics event tracking calls for better product categorization

These changes ensure that state data is passed as native objects to the analytics layer, allowing for better data handling and filtering.

## How did you test this code?

The changes are straightforward refactoring of data serialization in analytics event tracking. Existing analytics infrastructure will handle the object format natively. No manual testing required as this is a data format change that maintains the same information while improving data structure.

## Publish to changelog?

no

5. Confirm the unsaved changes warning still fires on close
6. Create a new Mailgun destination and repeat steps 2-5

pull_requests.title: fix(experiments): make service writes atomic

## Problem

Experiment create and update operations are not ran in a single atomic transaction. Thus, if it fails half-way, you can end up in a partial state where f.ex the feature flag is persisted where as the experiment is not.

## Changes

Wrap the service calls in an atomic transaction to make them "all or nothing". If anything fails, the whole transaction will be aborted.

pull_requests.title: fix(insights): Tooltip poor performance when using breakdown
pull_requests.body:
## Problem
https://posthog.slack.com/archives/C0ACRAMJUAG/p1772539058481479
 The insight tooltip had `transition: all 0.4s` and a CSS rule that enabled `pointer-events: auto` when content overflowed. This caused hundreds of stacked CSS animations on `left`/`top` during mousemove, and the interactive tooltip would intercept scroll and mouse events, freezing the page, especially on charts with many series.

## Changes
1. Replace `transition: all 0.4s` with targeted `left 0.05s, top 0.05s, opacity 0.2s` transitions
2. Remove CSS `:has()` rule that toggled `pointer-events: auto` on overflow
3. Add JS-based interactive delay — tooltip becomes interactive (`pointer-events: auto`) only after the mouse stops moving for 500ms, so users can still scroll overflowing tooltip content
4. Reset interactivity on every reposition and tooltip hide/cleanup

pull_requests.title: fix(test): use insertText() to bypass Monaco autocomplete in HogQL test
pull_requests.body:
## Summary
- Eliminates flaky HogQL E2E test by using `keyboard.insertText()` to set the query atomically instead of typing character by character

## Problem
The previous fix ([PR #50390](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/50390)) added `Escape` after `keyboard.type()` to dismiss autocomplete popups. This reduced the failure rate but didn't eliminate it — 1 failure in 20 runs post-merge. The issue is that Monaco's autocomplete can accept a suggestion **during** character-by-character typing, before the final `Escape` is sent.

Monaco's HogQL autocomplete provider triggers on space, comma, period, and `{` characters (`hogQLAutocompleteProvider.ts:89`) with a 300ms delay (`QueryPane.tsx:67`). When `keyboard.type()` sends characters one at a time, the autocomplete popup can intercept keystrokes mid-query, corrupting the text.

## Fix
Replace `keyboard.type(query)` + `Escape` with `keyboard.insertText(query)`:

- **`insertText()`** dispatches a single `input` event with the full text — no `keydown`/`keyup` events that trigger autocomplete
- This completely bypasses key-by-key processing, making it impossible for autocomplete to interfere
- Also switched `Control+A` → `ControlOrMeta+a` (Playwright 1.45+ cross-platform modifier)

## Related
- Insight: [`01KK9XNDVZ445T05Y2BX8YM584`](https://app.mendral.com/insights/01KK9XNDVZ445T05Y2BX8YM584)
- Previous fix: [PR #50390](https://github.com/PostHog/posthog/pull/50390)



---

> [!NOTE]
> Created by [Mendral](https://mendral.com)

Updates the internal engineering documentation to reflect that MCP tool YAML definitions are now organized in product-specific folders rather than the shared `services/mcp/definitions/` location.

This follows the changes in #50438 which moved:
- `services/mcp/definitions/actions.yaml` → `products/actions/mcp/tools.yaml`
- `services/mcp/definitions/cohorts.yaml` → `products/cohorts/mcp/tools.yaml`

pull_requests.title: perf(cohorts): optimize realtime cohort query building by passing team objects
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Realtime cohort calculations were experiencing slow query building times (~800ms for p0-50 percentile). Additionally, overlapping temporal workflows were being queued instead of skipped, and there was no visibility into which specific cohorts were causing performance issues.

## Changes

- **Added optional `team` and `cohort` parameters** to `FOSSCohortQuery.unwrap_cohort()` to eliminate redundant database queries
- **Updated all callers** to pass preloaded team and cohort objects from `select_related("team")` queries
- **Maintained cross-team cohort access** by keeping `team__project_id` filtering for project-level cohort sharing
- **Changed temporal schedule overlap policy** from `BUFFER_ONE` to `SKIP` to prevent workflow queuing when one is already running
- **Added slow cohort logging** with warnings for cohorts taking longer than 10 seconds, including cohort ID, duration, team ID, and cohort name for identification

The services catalog listed each PostHog product as a separate top-level service with `paid` pricing. pull_requests.title: feat(flags): split flag definitions cache verification into two staggered tasks
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The flag definitions cache verification task processes both variants (with-cohorts and without-cohorts) sequentially in a single task with a 1-hour time limit. If the first variant is slow or fails, it can starve or block the second variant. The shared lock also prevents any parallel execution.

## Changes

- Split `verify_and_fix_flag_definitions_cache_task` into two independent tasks: one for with-cohorts (runs at minute 50, its original schedule) and one for without-cohorts (runs at minute 10)
- Each task gets its own distributed lock, allowing independent execution and preventing one variant from blocking the other
- Time limits are 25min soft / 30min hard per task, since each now only handles one variant
- Uses `functools.partial` to bind `include_cohorts` when constructing the verify function
- Added debug-level timing for batch DB loads in the hypercache verifier
- Downgraded a noisy info log to debug in flag dependency transformation

## How did you test this code?

Unit tests updated and passing — the test suite covers each task independently, including lock isolation between variants, error handling, and pushgateway metrics.

## Publish to changelog?

No

pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- The Temporal SDK's `workflow.metric_meter()` and `activity.metric_meter()` already include `workflow_type`, `activity_type`, `namespace`, and `task_queue` as default attributes (see [`_workflow_instance.py:1218`](https://github.com/temporalio/sdk-python/blob/main/temporalio/worker/_workflow_instance.py#L1218) and [`activity.py:234`](https://github.com/temporalio/sdk-python/blob/main/temporalio/activity.py#L234))
- Our `DeleteRecordingsMetricsInterceptor` was passing these same attributes again via `get_metric_meter({"workflow_type": ...})`, causing Prometheus to concatenate duplicate label values with semicolons (e.g. `delete-recordings-with-person;delete-recordings-with-person`)
- Removed the redundant `workflow_type` and `activity_type` attributes; only truly additional attributes like `status` are still passed

Note: the same bug exists in the llm_analytics interceptors (`metrics.py`, `sentiment/metrics.py`, `trace_clustering/metrics.py`, `trace_summarization/metrics.py`) — will fix in a follow-up.

## Test plan

- [ ] Deploy and verify the Grafana "Workflow throughput by type" panel on the session-replay-recording-api dashboard no longer shows duplicated names like `delete-recordings-with-person;delete-recordings-with-person`
- [ ] Verify existing metric queries in Grafana still work (the `workflow_type` label is still present, just no longer duplicated)

pull_requests.title: fix(flags): use filter set instead of removing flags for dependency graph integrity
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When flag B depends on flag A, and flag A is filtered out by runtime or evaluation tag filtering, the dependency resolver errors with `MissingDependency`. This happens because the old approach removed non-matching flags from the collection entirely, breaking the dependency graph.

## Changes

Instead of removing flags from the collection, we now track filtered-out flag IDs in a `HashSet<i32>` on `FeatureFlagList`. Flags stay in the collection so the dependency graph remains intact, but are skipped during evaluation, billing, experience continuity checks, DB preparation, and group type mapping initialization.

- **`FeatureFlagList`**: Add `filtered_out_flag_ids: HashSet<i32>` field (serde-skipped, request-scoped). Built from inactive, deleted, survey-excluded, runtime-mismatched, and tag-mismatched flags in `fetch_and_filter`.
- **Filter functions renamed**: `filter_flags_by_runtime` → `collect_excluded_by_runtime`, `filter_flags_by_evaluation_tags` → `collect_excluded_by_tags`. They now return IDs to exclude rather than filtered flag lists.
- **Evaluation pipeline**: `FeatureFlagMatcher` stores `filtered_out_flag_ids` as a field, set once from `FeatureFlagList` at the start of `evaluate_all_feature_flags`. Downstream functions (`evaluate_flags_in_level`, `initialize_group_type_mappings_if_needed`) read from `self.filtered_out_flag_ids`. `flags_require_db_preparation` takes it as a parameter.
- **Pre-seeding**: Filtered-out flags are pre-seeded as `false` in `flag_
