pull_requests.title: fix(insights): stop double timezone conversion on x-axis labels
pull_requests.body:
Datetime strings in the API response are already in the project timezone (ClickHouse applies toTimeZone before truncation), but the x-axis formatter was treating them as UTC and converting again. This caused hour labels to be offset by the project's UTC difference (e.g. 05:00 displayed as 13:00 for UTC+8).

Switch from dayjsUtcToTimezone() to dayjs.tz() to match the tooltip formatter, which already handles this correctly.

## Problem

Users in non-UTC timezones see inconsistent times between the x-axis labels and tooltips on hourly trend charts. For example, a UTC+8 user hovering over a data point sees "05:00 (UTC+8)" in the tooltip but the x-axis label at that position reads "13:00", an 8-hour offset matching their UTC difference.

## Changes

The x-axis formatter in `formatXAxisTick.ts` was treating datetime strings from the API as UTC and converting them to the project timezone using `dayjsUtcToTimezone()`. However, ClickHouse already applies `toTimeZone(e.timestamp, '<project_tz>')` before truncation, so the strings arrive **already in the project timezone**. This caused a double conversion (e.g. 05:00 + 8h = 13:00).

The fix replaces `dayjsUtcToTimezone(s, timezone, false)` with `dayjs.tz(s, timezone)`, which correctly interprets the string as already being in the target timezone — matching the tooltip formatter's existing behavior.

## How did you test this code?

I followed these testing steps:
1. Set your project timezone to something non-UTC (e.g. UT

- **Event pinning**: pin specific events to keep them visible at the top of the list
- **JSON export**: download filtered events as a JSON file
- **Pause/resume**: freeze the event stream to inspect events without new ones pushing them away; buffered event count shown while paused
- **Clear events**: trash button to reset the event list
- **Copy payload**: copy individual event payloads as JSON from expanded event details
- **Relative timestamps**: toggle between absolute (`HH:mm:ss.SSS`) and relative (`2 minutes ago`) via clock icon button

pull_requests.title: fix(test): dismiss Monaco autocomplete in HogQL E2E test
pull_requests.body:
## Summary
- Fixes the flaky E2E test "SQL / HogQL: write query, execute and verify results" by dismissing Monaco autocomplete after typing the query

## Problem
The test types a multi-line HogQL query character by character using `keyboard.type()`. Monaco's HogQL autocomplete provider has **space as a trigger character** (`triggerCharacters: [' ', ',', '.', '{']` in `hogQLAutocompleteProvider.ts:89`) with a 300ms delay (`quickSuggestionsDelay: 300` in `QueryPane.tsx:67`).

When `keyboard.type()` sends a space, Monaco starts a timer to show autocomplete suggestions. If the suggestion widget appears while subsequent characters are being typed, it can **intercept keystrokes** — corrupting the query. The corrupted query either fails to parse or returns wrong results, so `"Showing 10 rows"` never appears and the test times out.

The test has been flaky since it was first added (~Feb 23), appearing as a failure in 6+ distinct runs in the last 7 days on master.

## Fix
1. **Press `Escape` after typing** to dismiss any lingering autocomplete popup before the run button is clicked
2. **Replace `Meta+A` with `Control+A`** — on Linux CI, `Meta` maps to the Super/Windows key (not Ctrl), so `Meta+A` is a no-op. Not currently causing failures since the editor starts empty, but fixes a latent bug.

## Related
- Insight: [`01KK9XNDVZ445T05Y2BX8YM584`](https://app.mendral.com/insights/01KK9XNDVZ445T05Y2BX8YM584)



---

> [!NOTE]
> Created by [Mendral](https://mendral.com). Tag @mendral-app

## Pull request overview

This PR introduces the foundational data layer for logs alerting — the first spike (1.1) of a larger feature. It adds two Django models, their migration, a feature flag constant, and `ActivityScope` registration. pull_requests.title: fix(flags): use cohort.team_id to avoid N+1 without loading Team
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

`get_all_cohort_dependencies` accesses `cohort.team.project_id` when looking up dependent cohorts, which triggers a lazy load of the full `Team` object for every cohort in the dependency chain. This causes N+1 queries in the feature flags evaluation path.

## Changes

Replace `team__project_id=cohort.team.project_id` with `team_id=cohort.team_id`. The `team_id` foreign key is stored directly on the `Cohort` row, so no additional query is needed.

This narrows the filter from "any team in the same project" to "the exact same team," but cohorts are always team-scoped — cross-team cohort references don't exist. Confirmed by querying production data: zero cohorts reference a cohort on a different team within the same project.

## How did you test this code?

- Ran a production SQL query to confirm no cross-team cohort dependencies exist
- Existing tests pass — cohort dependency resolution is covered by `get_all_cohort_dependencies` tests

## Publish to changelog?

no

This PR aims to remove an N+1 query pattern in cohort dependency resolution during feature flag evaluation by avoiding lazy-loading `Team` when scoping cohort lookups.

pull_requests.title: feat(llma): add telemetry for eval event emission failures
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

When `emit_evaluation_event_activity` (Activity 4) fails in the eval workflow, the failure shows up as a generic `workflow_exception` in `llma_eval_errors` — indistinguishable from failures in any other activity. This means we cannot measure or alert on dropped `$ai_evaluation` events specifically.

During a recent ingestion backpressure incident, Activity 4 failures caused eval events to be silently dropped (the eval was computed and tokens were spent, but the result event was never recorded). We had no targeted metric to detect or measure this.

## Changes

Two new telemetry points:

- **`llma_eval_emit_event_outcome{outcome=success/failed}`** — counter in the activity itself, tracking every attempt to emit a `$ai_evaluation` event. Enables alerting on `rate(failed) > 0` and dashboarding the drop rate.
- **`llma_eval_errors{error_type=emit_evaluation_event_failed}`** — labeled error counter in the workflow, distinguishing Activity 4 failures from other workflow errors in the existing `llma_eval_errors` metric.

Suggested alerts:
- `rate(llma_eval_emit_event_outcome{outcome="failed"}[5m]) > 0`
- `rate(llma_eval_emit_event_outcome{outcome="failed"}[5m]) / rate(llma_eval_emit_event_outcome[5m]) > 0.01`

## How did you test this code?

Code review. The new metrics follow the same pattern as existing eval metrics (`increment_errors`, `increment_eval_signal_outcome`) using Temporal's built-in metric meter. No behavioral changes — the activity and workflow still raise

pull_requests.title: fix(llma): eliminate floating-point precision artifacts in LLM cost data
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

The automated LLM cost update script (`update-ai-costs.ts`) produces floating-point representation artifacts in `llm-costs.json`, such as `1.0000000000000001e-7` instead of `1e-7` and `0.0000032000000000000003` instead of `0.0000032`.

This happens because `parsePricingNumber` uses `js-big-decimal` for precise parsing but then converts back to a native JS `number` via `parseFloat()`, reintroducing IEEE 754 noise. The artifacts are cosmetic (precision error ~1e-23) but make diffs noisy and prices hard to review.

Spotted via bot review comments on #50160.

## Changes

Round parsed cost values to 10 significant digits before returning. This eliminates all visible floating-point artifacts while retaining far more precision than cost calculations require (IEEE 754 doubles only carry ~15.9 significant digits anyway).

## How did you test this code?

This is a one-line change to a code generation script. The next scheduled run of the `update-ai-costs` workflow will produce a clean diff without precision artifacts.

pull_requests.title: fix(llma): prevent Temporal interceptor from crashing workers on deadlock errors
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

During ingestion backpressure events, Temporal workers running LLM analytics eval workflows crash-loop instead of gracefully failing individual workflows.

The root cause is in `PostHogClientWorkflowInterceptor` (`posthog/temporal/common/posthog_client.py`) — shared infrastructure used by all Temporal workers. When a Temporal deadlock error (`TMPRL1101`) occurs, the workflow event loop is broken. The interceptor's `except` handler calls `workflow.info()` to gather context for error reporting, but this throws `_NotInWorkflowEventLoopError` when the event loop is dead. This unhandled exception crashes the entire worker process instead of just failing the workflow.

During a recent ClickHouse consumer lag spike, this turned what should have been transient workflow failures into 32 pods crash-looping (2-7 restarts each).

**Note:** This is shared Temporal infrastructure — the bug affects all Temporal workflow types, not just LLM analytics evals. The evals workers were the first to hit it because their `emit_evaluation_event_activity` uses `capture_internal()` via `database_sync_to_async`, making them sensitive to ingestion pipeline backpressure.

## Changes

Wrap the error-capture block in `_PostHogClientWorkflowInterceptor.execute_workflow` with a try/except so that failures in the interceptor's own error reporting (e.g. calling `workflow.info()` during a deadlock) never prevent the original exception from propagating cleanly. The original exception is al

pull_requests.title: fix(llma): reduce generation sentiment batch size to prevent timeouts
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

On the trace view, loading sentiment for generations with many IDs (e.g. 20) in a single API call causes an upstream request timeout. The Temporal workflow running ClickHouse queries + ONNX classification for all 20 generations cannot finish within the proxy timeout window.

## Changes

- Reduced `BATCH_MAX_SIZE` in `llmGenerationSentimentLazyLoaderLogic.ts` from 20 to 5
- Reduced `BATCH_MAX_GENERATION_IDS` in backend constants from 20 to 5
- Regenerated OpenAPI types to reflect the new limit

The existing `chunk()` + `Promise.allSettled()` logic already handles splitting into parallel requests, so a trace with 20 generations now sends 4 parallel requests of 5 instead of 1 request of 20. Each individual request completes within the timeout. This matches the trace-level sentiment loader which already uses batch size 5.

## How did you test this code?

This is a configuration change. The trace-level loader has been using batch size 5 successfully. The generation loader now matches that behavior.

pull_requests.title: feat(logs): add message lag histogram to rate limiter
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Logs rate limiter uses message observed timestamps for the token bucket, which causes incorrect rate limiting when the consumer is catching up after lag spikes.

It's possible that partitions that are behind get no refill because another partition already advanced the Redis timestamp.

## Changes

Adds a logs_rate_limiter_message_lag_seconds histogram tracking the gap between message timestamps and wall clock time, so we can observe catch-up behavior before changing rate limiter logic.  
  
This will let us know how effective it will be to disable the rate limiter for messages from the past

pull_requests.title: fix(test): pass job_id to _replay_v3_consumer in test_append_only_table
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- Fix flaky `test_append_only_table[non_dlt]` test (~7% failure rate on shard 5/7) by passing `job_id` to `_replay_v3_consumer`, matching the pattern used by the `_run()` helper
- Add `compact_table` mock around the direct `_execute_run` call to prevent real compaction against MinIO in CI

## Problem

`test_append_only_table` calls `_replay_v3_consumer` without `job_id`:
```python
await _execute_run(str(uuid.uuid4()), inputs, [])
await _replay_v3_consumer(team_id=team.pk, schema_id=inputs.external_data_schema_id)
```

The `_replay_v3_consumer` function has a safety check (lines 368–372) that skips replay if the job is already `COMPLETED` — but this check only activates when `job_id` is provided. Without it, Kafka messages are always replayed regardless of job status, inserting a 3rd duplicate row.

Additionally, the direct `_execute_run` call lacked the `compact_table` mock that `_run()` applies, meaning real compaction ran against MinIO in CI — a secondary risk for intermittent failures.

## Fix

Fetch the latest job after `_execute_run` and pass its `job_id` to `_replay_v3_consumer`, exactly matching the `_run()` helper pattern (lines 301–318). Also wrap `_execute_run` in `mock.patch.object(DeltaTableHelper, "compact_table")`.

## Expected outcome

`test_append_only_table[non_dlt]` failure rate drops from ~7% to 0%.


---

> [!NOTE]
> Created by [Mendral](https://mendral.com). Tag @mendral-app with feedback or questions.

pull_requests.body:
## Problem

`$ai_trace` and `$ai_span` events show "No input" / "No output" in the person profile events list, even though they contain valid data in `$ai_input_state` / `$ai_output_state`. `ConversationDisplay` only read `$ai_input` / `$ai_output_choices` (used by `$ai_generation` events).

Also covers facade products (`DataclassSerializer`) and streaming/SSE endpoints.

pull_requests.title: ci(sqlx-cli): cache compiled binary with dedicated actions/cache
pull_requests.body:
## Summary

- **Add `setup-sqlx-cli` composite action** that caches the compiled sqlx-cli binary using `actions/cache` with a stable key (`sqlx-cli-{version}-{os}-{arch}`), following the same pattern as `setup-protoc` and `setup-sccache`.
- **Replace all 6 inline `cargo install sqlx-cli` calls** across `ci-backend.yml` (4), `ci-rust.yml` (1), and `ci-dagster.yml` (1) with the new action.

## Problem

`Swatinem/rust-cache` (shared-key `v2-rust-backend`) had a **42% miss rate** on PR jobs because:
- `save-if: master-only` means PR jobs can only read the master cache
- The cache key covers the entire Rust dependency tree (~GBs), making it prone to eviction at GitHub's 10 GB limit
- Each miss recompiled sqlx-cli from source (~65 s), wasting **~2,300 compute-hours per 14-day period** across ~275K Backend CI jobs

## Solution

The new `setup-sqlx-cli` action uses a **dedicated, stable cache key** that:
- Only caches the sqlx-cli binary (~20 MB), not the entire cargo registry
- Uses a version-pinned key that never changes unless the sqlx-cli version is bumped
- Will almost always hit once warmed on master, since it won't be evicted by unrelated Rust dependency changes
- On cache hit: restores in ~2 s (vs ~65 s compilation)
- On cache miss: compiles from source (same as today), then saves for future runs

## Expected Impact

- **Cache hit**: ~2 s (restore from actions/cache) vs ~65 s (compile from source)
- **Cache miss rate**: Near 0% (stable key) vs 42% (Swatinem/rust-cache evictio

pull_requests.title: feat(experiments): backfill experiment status field
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Existing experiments has `status = None`

## Changes

Add a data migration to update status field on existing experiments.

This migration will run a single UPDATE statement on the experiments table in a single transaction. Given the size of the experiment table, and that no indexes has to be rewritten, this should be a safe operation, and run fast. Also, there is not much write activity to this table.

```
UPDATE "posthog_experiment"
SET "status" = CASE
    WHEN "start_date" IS NULL THEN 'draft'
    WHEN "start_date" IS NOT NULL AND "end_date" IS NULL THEN 'running'
    WHEN "start_date" IS NOT NULL AND "end_date" IS NOT NULL THEN 'stopped'
END
WHERE "status" IS NULL;
```

## How did you test this code?

- Ran the migration locally and verified that status field were set correctly

pull_requests.title: fix(batch_exports): adapt tests to DST changes
pull_requests.body:
## Problem

Batch export creation tests fail when they happen to run near a DST transition. The tests assert that consecutive daily runs are at least 23 hours 45 minutes apart, but during a spring-forward transition the actual UTC gap is only about 23 hours, causing the assertion to fail.

## Changes

The time-between-runs assertion now accounts for DST by computing the UTC offset difference between two consecutive scheduled runs and widening the acceptable bounds accordingly. For timezones without DST, behavior is unchanged.

Local tests passing

pull_requests.title: fix(test): scope insight delete button locator to visible side panel
pull_requests.body:
## Summary
- Fixes the consistently flaky E2E test "Deleting an insight from dashboard redirects back" by scoping the delete button locator to `#side-panel`

## Problem
The delete button is rendered via `createPortal` into `scenePanelElement`. There are **two portal targets**:

1. **Inline panel** (`Navigation.tsx:199`) — always has `display: none` for insights because `scenePanelOpenManual` defaults to `false`
2. **Side panel** (`SidePanelInfo.tsx:29`) — visible after opening the Info tab, registered via `useEffect`

The unscoped `page.getByTestId('insight-delete')` matches the button in the **hidden inline panel** and times out (90s) waiting for it to become visible. The button never becomes visible there because the inline panel is permanently hidden for insights.

PR #50057 attempted to fix this with `scrollIntoViewIfNeeded()` and waiting for `.scene-panel-actions-section` to be attached, but neither helps — the button *is* attached, just in a `display: none` container.

## Fix
Scope the locator to `#side-panel` so Playwright only finds the button once the portal content has moved to the visible side panel container:

```typescript
const deleteButton = this.page.locator('#side-panel').getByTestId('insight-delete')
```

This is a test-side fix. The product behavior is correct — the portal switch happens quickly and users never see the intermediate state.

## Related
- Insight: `01KJZDHSWQ6Q11TGBNVZMAVDWG`
- Previous fix attempt: PR #50057



---

> [!NOTE]
> Created by [Me
